UPDATE Statement modifies existing rows in tables. Basic syntax: UPDATE table_name SET column = value WHERE condition. SET clause specifies which columns to change and their new values. WHERE clause determines which rows to update - omitting WHERE updates ALL rows (dangerous). PostgreSQL supports updating multiple columns simultaneously, using expressions and subqueries in SET clause, and joins via FROM clause.

Key Features: Can update multiple rows in single statement, expressions can reference current column values (price = price * 1.1), subqueries allowed in SET and WHERE clauses, FROM clause enables joins for complex updates, constraints and triggers enforced during update. UPDATE returns count of modified rows. Use RETURNING to retrieve updated data. Updates are atomic within transaction.

Best Practices: Always include WHERE clause unless updating entire table, test WHERE clause with SELECT first, use transactions for multi-table updates, add WHERE clauses to prevent accidental mass updates, consider performance impact on large tables (indexes help), use CASE expressions for conditional updates within single statement. Beware: updating without WHERE modifies ALL rows.

UPDATE Syntax
-- Basic UPDATE
UPDATE table_name
SET column1 = value1, column2 = value2
WHERE condition;

-- UPDATE with expression
UPDATE table_name
SET column1 = column1 * 1.1
WHERE condition;

-- UPDATE with FROM clause (join)
UPDATE table_name
SET column1 = other_table.column1
FROM other_table
WHERE table_name.id = other_table.id;

-- UPDATE with subquery
UPDATE table_name
SET column1 = (SELECT value FROM other_table WHERE condition)
WHERE condition;

-- UPDATE with CASE (conditional values)
UPDATE table_name
SET column1 = CASE
    WHEN condition1 THEN value1
    WHEN condition2 THEN value2
    ELSE value3
END
WHERE condition;

-- UPDATE with RETURNING
UPDATE table_name
SET column1 = value1
WHERE condition
RETURNING *;

🎯 Example 1: Basic UPDATE with WHERE Clause

-- Update single customer's email
UPDATE customers
SET email = 'alice.j.new@email.com'
WHERE customer_id = 1001;

-- Update customer's city and country
UPDATE customers
SET city = 'Portland', country = 'USA'
WHERE email = 'bob.smith@email.com';

-- Verify updates
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name, email, city, country
FROM customers
WHERE customer_id IN (1001, 1002)
ORDER BY customer_id;
Result:
 customer_id | first_name | last_name |        email            |   city   | country
-------------+------------+-----------+-------------------------+----------+---------
        1001 | Alice      | Johnson   | alice.j.new@email.com   | Seattle  | USA
        1002 | Bob        | Smith     | bob.smith@email.com     | Portland | USA
(2 rows)

UPDATE 1  -- (for each UPDATE command)
Explanation: First UPDATE modifies single column (email) for customer_id 1001. Second UPDATE changes multiple columns (city, country) using email in WHERE clause. Each UPDATE affects 1 row - PostgreSQL returns "UPDATE 1". WHERE clause crucial - without it, ALL customers would be updated. Test WHERE with SELECT first: SELECT * FROM customers WHERE customer_id = 1001. Multiple columns updated with comma-separated SET clauses. Updates validate constraints - changing email to duplicate value would fail on unique constraint.

🎯 Example 2: UPDATE with Expression (Relative Changes)

-- Increase all Electronics prices by 10%
UPDATE products
SET price = price * 1.10
WHERE category_id = 1;

-- Decrease stock quantity for specific products
UPDATE products
SET stock_quantity = stock_quantity - 5
WHERE product_id IN (2001, 2002, 2003);

-- Verify price changes
SELECT product_id, product_name, category_id, price, stock_quantity
FROM products
WHERE product_id IN (2001, 2002, 2003)
ORDER BY product_id;
Result:
 product_id |  product_name  | category_id | price | stock_quantity
------------+----------------+-------------+-------+----------------
       2001 | Wireless Mouse |           1 | 32.99 |            145
       2002 | USB-C Cable    |           1 | 14.29 |            295
       2003 | Laptop Stand   |           2 | 49.99 |             70
(3 rows)

UPDATE 2  -- (first UPDATE, category_id=1)
UPDATE 3  -- (second UPDATE, 3 products)
Explanation: UPDATE can reference current column values in SET clause. First UPDATE: price = price * 1.10 increases prices by 10% (29.99 → 32.99, 12.99 → 14.29). Second UPDATE: stock_quantity = stock_quantity - 5 decrements inventory. Expressions can use arithmetic operators (+, -, *, /), functions (ROUND, UPPER), CASE statements. Common patterns: incrementing counters (views = views + 1), applying discounts, adjusting quantities. PostgreSQL evaluates expression for EACH row using current values. Atomic operation - all matching rows updated together in transaction.

🎯 Example 3: UPDATE with FROM Clause (Join Update)

-- Update order totals from order_items
UPDATE orders
SET total_amount = subquery.order_total
FROM (
    SELECT order_id, SUM(quantity * unit_price) AS order_total
    FROM order_items
    GROUP BY order_id
) AS subquery
WHERE orders.order_id = subquery.order_id;

-- Verify updated totals
SELECT o.order_id, o.customer_id, o.total_amount,
       (SELECT SUM(quantity * unit_price) FROM order_items WHERE order_id = o.order_id) AS calculated_total
FROM orders o
WHERE o.order_id = 3001;
Result:
 order_id | customer_id | total_amount | calculated_total
----------+-------------+--------------+------------------
     3001 |        1001 |       157.94 |           157.94
(1 row)

UPDATE 1
Explanation: FROM clause enables joins in UPDATE statements. Subquery calculates order totals by summing order_items (59.98 + 49.99 + 47.97 = 157.94). UPDATE joins orders with subquery on order_id, sets total_amount to calculated value. Useful for: synchronizing denormalized data, updating from staging tables, batch updates based on complex calculations. Alternative syntax: UPDATE orders SET total_amount = (SELECT SUM...) WHERE order_id = 3001 - but FROM clause more efficient for updating multiple rows. PostgreSQL-specific syntax - other databases use different join update syntax.

🎯 Example 4: Conditional UPDATE with CASE Expression

-- Update order status based on business rules
UPDATE orders
SET status = CASE
    WHEN total_amount = 0 THEN 'pending'
    WHEN total_amount > 0 AND total_amount < 100 THEN 'confirmed'
    WHEN total_amount >= 100 AND total_amount < 500 THEN 'processing'
    WHEN total_amount >= 500 THEN 'priority'
    ELSE 'unknown'
END
WHERE status = 'pending';

-- Update product prices with tiered discount
UPDATE products
SET price = price * CASE
    WHEN stock_quantity > 200 THEN 0.85  -- 15% off high stock
    WHEN stock_quantity > 100 THEN 0.90  -- 10% off medium stock
    WHEN stock_quantity > 50 THEN 0.95   -- 5% off low stock
    ELSE 1.00  -- no discount
END
WHERE category_id = 1;

-- Verify conditional updates
SELECT product_id, product_name, stock_quantity, price
FROM products
WHERE category_id = 1
ORDER BY product_id;
Result:
 product_id |  product_name  | stock_quantity | price
------------+----------------+----------------+-------
       2001 | Wireless Mouse |            145 |  29.69
       2002 | USB-C Cable    |            295 |  12.15
(2 rows)

UPDATE 2
Explanation: CASE expression enables conditional updates within single statement. First UPDATE sets different status values based on total_amount ranges - applies business rules in SQL. Second UPDATE applies tiered discounts: USB-C Cable has 295 stock → 85% of 14.29 = 12.15, Wireless Mouse has 145 stock → 90% of 32.99 = 29.69. CASE evaluates conditions sequentially, uses first match. More efficient than multiple UPDATE statements (single table scan). ELSE clause handles unmatched rows (default value). Common use cases: status transitions, tiered pricing, data categorization, conditional field updates. Alternative: use WHERE clause with multiple UPDATEs (less efficient).

🎯 Example 5: UPDATE with Subquery and RETURNING

-- Update customer's loyalty tier based on total purchase amount
UPDATE customers
SET loyalty_tier = CASE
    WHEN (SELECT SUM(total_amount) FROM orders WHERE orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id) >= 1000 THEN 'Gold'
    WHEN (SELECT SUM(total_amount) FROM orders WHERE orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id) >= 500 THEN 'Silver'
    WHEN (SELECT SUM(total_amount) FROM orders WHERE orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id) >= 100 THEN 'Bronze'
    ELSE 'Standard'
END
WHERE customer_id = 1001
RETURNING customer_id, first_name, last_name, loyalty_tier,
          (SELECT SUM(total_amount) FROM orders WHERE orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id) AS total_spent;

-- Update product stock and return updated values
UPDATE products
SET stock_quantity = stock_quantity - 10,
    last_restock_date = CURRENT_DATE
WHERE product_id = 2001
RETURNING product_id, product_name, stock_quantity, last_restock_date;
Result (customer update):
 customer_id | first_name | last_name | loyalty_tier | total_spent
-------------+------------+-----------+--------------+-------------
        1001 | Alice      | Johnson   | Bronze       |      157.94
(1 row)
Result (product update):
 product_id |  product_name  | stock_quantity | last_restock_date
------------+----------------+----------------+-------------------
       2001 | Wireless Mouse |            135 | 2026-01-25
(1 row)
Explanation: UPDATE can use correlated subqueries in SET and WHERE clauses. First UPDATE calculates total purchase amount from orders table (157.94), determines loyalty tier using CASE (Bronze for 100-499 range). Subquery executes for each row being updated (correlated via customer_id). RETURNING includes calculated expression showing total_spent. Second UPDATE demonstrates simple decrement with RETURNING - retrieves updated values in single operation. Subqueries powerful but can be slow on large tables - consider materialized views or denormalized columns for frequently updated calculations. RETURNING eliminates need for SELECT after UPDATE.

6.3 Deleting Data

📖 Full Description

DELETE Statement removes rows from tables based on conditions. Basic syntax: DELETE FROM table_name WHERE condition. WHERE clause determines which rows to delete - omitting WHERE deletes ALL rows (dangerous). PostgreSQL supports USING clause for joins, RETURNING clause to retrieve deleted data, and CASCADE deletion through foreign key constraints. DELETE removes rows but doesn't reset auto-increment sequences.

Key Features: Conditional deletion with WHERE clause, complex conditions using subqueries and joins, USING clause for multi-table deletes, RETURNING shows deleted data, foreign key CASCADE automatically deletes dependent rows, DELETE fires triggers (BEFORE/AFTER DELETE). DELETE is row-by-row operation - slower than TRUNCATE for entire tables. Deleted rows remain in transaction until COMMIT.

Best Practices: Always use WHERE clause (prevent accidental mass deletion), test WHERE with SELECT first, use transactions for safety (ROLLBACK if mistake), implement soft deletes for audit trails (deleted_at column instead of DELETE), use TRUNCATE for fast table clearing (no WHERE, no triggers, resets sequences), handle foreign key constraints properly (CASCADE or manual deletion), consider archiving data before deletion for compliance/recovery.

DELETE Syntax
-- Basic DELETE
DELETE FROM table_name
WHERE condition;

-- DELETE with USING clause (join)
DELETE FROM table_name
USING other_table
WHERE table_name.id = other_table.id
  AND other_condition;

-- DELETE with subquery
DELETE FROM table_name
WHERE column IN (SELECT column FROM other_table WHERE condition);

-- DELETE all rows (use with caution!)
DELETE FROM table_name;

-- DELETE with RETURNING
DELETE FROM table_name
WHERE condition
RETURNING *;

-- TRUNCATE (fast alternative for deleting all rows)
TRUNCATE TABLE table_name;
TRUNCATE TABLE table_name RESTART IDENTITY CASCADE;

🎯 Example 1: Basic DELETE with WHERE Clause

-- Delete single customer
DELETE FROM customers
WHERE customer_id = 1002;

-- Delete customers from specific city
DELETE FROM customers
WHERE city = 'Portland' AND country = 'USA';

-- Verify deletions
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name, city, country
FROM customers
WHERE customer_id >= 1000
ORDER BY customer_id;
Result:
 customer_id | first_name | last_name |  city   | country
-------------+------------+-----------+---------+---------
        1001 | Alice      | Johnson   | Seattle | USA
(1 row)

DELETE 1  -- (first DELETE)
DELETE 1  -- (second DELETE, Bob was from Portland)
Explanation: First DELETE removes customer_id 1002 (Bob Smith). Second DELETE targets Portland, USA customers - also matches Bob (already deleted). Each DELETE returns count of removed rows. WHERE clause critical - DELETE FROM customers without WHERE would remove ALL customers. Test WHERE condition first: SELECT * FROM customers WHERE city = 'Portland'. If customer has related orders with foreign key, DELETE might fail (constraint violation) unless CASCADE configured. Deleted data unrecoverable unless in transaction (can ROLLBACK) or have backups.

🎯 Example 2: DELETE with Subquery

-- Delete products that have never been ordered
DELETE FROM products
WHERE product_id NOT IN (
    SELECT DISTINCT product_id
    FROM order_items
);

-- Delete orders older than 2 years with specific status
DELETE FROM orders
WHERE order_date < CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '2 years'
  AND status IN ('cancelled', 'refunded');

-- Count remaining products and orders
SELECT 
    (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM products) AS total_products,
    (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders) AS total_orders;
Result:
 total_products | total_orders
----------------+--------------
           2003 |          156
(1 row)

DELETE 2  -- (2 products never ordered)
DELETE 8  -- (8 old cancelled/refunded orders)
Explanation: Subqueries enable complex deletion logic. First DELETE removes products not found in order_items (never sold) - NOT IN subquery returns distinct product_ids that exist in orders. Second DELETE combines date arithmetic (order_date < CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '2 years') with status filter. Subquery executes once (not correlated) - efficient. Alternative: NOT EXISTS more efficient for large tables: WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM order_items WHERE product_id = products.product_id). Be cautious with NOT IN when subquery contains NULLs - can return unexpected results. Use transactions for safety when deleting based on complex conditions.

🎯 Example 3: DELETE with USING Clause (Join Delete)

-- Delete order_items for cancelled orders
DELETE FROM order_items
USING orders
WHERE order_items.order_id = orders.order_id
  AND orders.status = 'cancelled';

-- Delete products in discontinued categories
DELETE FROM products
USING categories
WHERE products.category_id = categories.category_id
  AND categories.status = 'discontinued';

-- Verify deletions
SELECT COUNT(*) AS remaining_items FROM order_items;
Result:
 remaining_items
-----------------
            445
(1 row)

DELETE 12  -- (12 items from cancelled orders removed)
Explanation: USING clause enables joins in DELETE statements - PostgreSQL-specific syntax (not SQL standard). First DELETE joins order_items with orders, removes items where order status is 'cancelled'. USING provides FROM-like capability - can join multiple tables. More readable than subquery approach: DELETE FROM order_items WHERE order_id IN (SELECT order_id FROM orders WHERE status = 'cancelled'). Standard SQL alternative: DELETE FROM order_items WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders WHERE orders.order_id = order_items.order_id AND status = 'cancelled'). USING clause efficient for multi-table deletion logic.

🎯 Example 4: DELETE with CASCADE (Foreign Key Cascading)

-- First, verify order and its items exist
SELECT o.order_id, o.customer_id, COUNT(oi.item_id) AS item_count
FROM orders o
LEFT JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
WHERE o.order_id = 3001
GROUP BY o.order_id, o.customer_id;

-- Delete order (CASCADE deletes order_items automatically if FK has ON DELETE CASCADE)
-- Assuming: FOREIGN KEY (order_id) REFERENCES orders(order_id) ON DELETE CASCADE
DELETE FROM orders
WHERE order_id = 3001
RETURNING order_id, customer_id, status, total_amount;

-- Verify cascade deletion
SELECT COUNT(*) AS remaining_items
FROM order_items
WHERE order_id = 3001;
Result (before delete):
 order_id | customer_id | item_count
----------+-------------+------------
     3001 |        1001 |          3
(1 row)
Result (delete with RETURNING):
 order_id | customer_id | status  | total_amount
----------+-------------+---------+--------------
     3001 |        1001 | pending |       157.94
(1 row)
Result (verify cascade):
 remaining_items
-----------------
               0
(1 row)
Explanation: Foreign key with ON DELETE CASCADE automatically deletes child rows when parent deleted. Deleting order 3001 automatically removes its 3 order_items. CASCADE options: ON DELETE CASCADE (auto-delete children), ON DELETE SET NULL (set FK to NULL), ON DELETE RESTRICT (prevent deletion if children exist), ON DELETE NO ACTION (same as RESTRICT). RETURNING shows deleted parent row data. CASCADE powerful but dangerous - can delete unexpected data chains. Best practice: query dependent data first (SELECT to preview impact), use transactions (can ROLLBACK), configure CASCADE carefully based on business rules. Some scenarios prefer SET NULL or manual deletion for control.

🎯 Example 5: TRUNCATE vs DELETE Comparison

-- Create test table
CREATE TABLE test_logs (
    log_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    log_message TEXT,
    log_date TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

-- Insert test data
INSERT INTO test_logs (log_message)
SELECT 'Test log entry ' || generate_series(1, 1000);

-- Method 1: DELETE all rows (slow, fires triggers)
DELETE FROM test_logs;
-- Returns: DELETE 1000

-- Refill table
INSERT INTO test_logs (log_message)
SELECT 'Test log entry ' || generate_series(1, 1000);

-- Method 2: TRUNCATE (fast, no triggers, resets sequence)
TRUNCATE TABLE test_logs RESTART IDENTITY;
-- Returns: TRUNCATE TABLE

-- Verify sequence reset
INSERT INTO test_logs (log_message) VALUES ('New entry') RETURNING log_id;
Result (after TRUNCATE and new INSERT):
 log_id | log_message
--------+-------------
      1 | New entry
(1 row)
Explanation: TRUNCATE faster than DELETE for removing all rows: doesn't scan rows, doesn't fire triggers, immediately reclaims disk space. DELETE removes rows one-by-one (slow for large tables), fires triggers, doesn't reset SERIAL sequences. TRUNCATE resets sequences when using RESTART IDENTITY (next insert gets ID 1). TRUNCATE can't use WHERE clause (all-or-nothing). TRUNCATE CASCADE removes from tables with foreign keys. Trade-offs: TRUNCATE fast but can't recover in transaction (uses table lock), DELETE slower but more flexible (WHERE clause, RETURNING works better). Use TRUNCATE for: clearing staging tables, resetting test data, fast table cleanup. Use DELETE for: conditional removal, when triggers must fire, when needing RETURNING data.

6.4 Returning Data from Modified Rows

📖 Full Description

RETURNING Clause retrieves data from rows modified by INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE in the same statement. Basic syntax: INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE ... RETURNING column1, column2 or RETURNING * for all columns. Eliminates need for separate SELECT query after modification, reducing round-trips to database. PostgreSQL-specific feature - not all databases support RETURNING (Oracle uses similar RETURN INTO, MySQL doesn't support).

Key Features: Returns generated values (SERIAL, IDENTITY, DEFAULT), shows old values (before UPDATE/DELETE) or new values (after INSERT/UPDATE), can include expressions and calculations, works with single or multiple rows, integrates with CTEs (Common Table Expressions) for complex multi-step operations. RETURNING processes in order rows were modified. Useful for: getting auto-generated IDs, confirming changes, audit logging, chaining operations in CTEs.

Best Practices: Use RETURNING to get generated IDs instead of SELECT currval(), combine with CTEs for multi-table atomic operations, specify columns explicitly rather than RETURNING * for performance, leverage for optimistic locking (verify version column), use in triggers for logging modifications, integrate with application ORM layers to reduce queries. RETURNING particularly valuable in batch operations - returns all modified rows at once.

RETURNING Syntax
-- INSERT with RETURNING
INSERT INTO table_name (column1, column2)
VALUES (value1, value2)
RETURNING *;  -- or specific columns

-- INSERT multiple rows with RETURNING
INSERT INTO table_name (column1, column2)
VALUES (value1a, value2a), (value1b, value2b)
RETURNING column1, column2, generated_column;

-- UPDATE with RETURNING
UPDATE table_name
SET column1 = value1
WHERE condition
RETURNING column1, column2;

-- DELETE with RETURNING
DELETE FROM table_name
WHERE condition
RETURNING *;

-- RETURNING with expressions
INSERT INTO table_name (quantity, price)
VALUES (10, 25.50)
RETURNING quantity, price, (quantity * price) AS total;

-- RETURNING in CTE (WITH clause)
WITH deleted_rows AS (
    DELETE FROM table_name
    WHERE condition
    RETURNING *
)
INSERT INTO archive_table
SELECT * FROM deleted_rows;

🎯 Example 1: INSERT with RETURNING (Getting Generated IDs)

-- Insert customer and return generated ID
INSERT INTO customers (first_name, last_name, email, city, country)
VALUES ('Charlie', 'Brown', 'charlie.brown@email.com', 'Denver', 'USA')
RETURNING customer_id, first_name, last_name, email, registration_date;

-- Insert multiple products and return all generated data
INSERT INTO products (product_name, category_id, price, stock_quantity)
VALUES 
    ('Mechanical Keyboard', 1, 89.99, 50),
    ('Monitor Stand', 2, 39.99, 75),
    ('Desk Organizer', 2, 24.99, 100)
RETURNING product_id, product_name, price, stock_quantity, 
          (price * 0.9) AS discounted_price;
Result (customer insert):
 customer_id | first_name | last_name |          email           |    registration_date
-------------+------------+-----------+--------------------------+-------------------------
        1003 | Charlie    | Brown     | charlie.brown@email.com  | 2026-01-25 11:00:00
(1 row)
Result (products insert):
 product_id |    product_name     | price | stock_quantity | discounted_price
------------+---------------------+-------+----------------+------------------
       2006 | Mechanical Keyboard | 89.99 |             50 |            80.99
       2007 | Monitor Stand       | 39.99 |             75 |            35.99
       2008 | Desk Organizer      | 24.99 |            100 |            22.49
(3 rows)
Explanation: RETURNING retrieves inserted data in single operation - no separate SELECT needed. First INSERT returns customer_id (SERIAL auto-generated 1003) and registration_date (DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP). Second INSERT returns all 3 products with calculated expression (price * 0.9). Eliminates race conditions from SELECT after INSERT (another transaction might insert between). Common pattern: var customer_id = INSERT...RETURNING customer_id; use customer_id for related inserts. More efficient than SELECT currval('customers_customer_id_seq'). RETURNING especially useful in ORMs (Hibernate, Entity Framework) - frameworks automatically capture returned values.

🎯 Example 2: UPDATE with RETURNING (Audit Trail)

-- Update product price and return old and new values
UPDATE products
SET price = 79.99
WHERE product_id = 2006
RETURNING product_id, product_name, 
          price AS new_price,
          (SELECT price FROM products WHERE product_id = 2006) AS old_price;

-- Update multiple orders and log changes
UPDATE orders
SET status = 'shipped', 
    shipped_date = CURRENT_DATE
WHERE status = 'processing' 
  AND order_date < CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '2 days'
RETURNING order_id, customer_id, status, shipped_date, total_amount;
Result (product price update):
 product_id |    product_name     | new_price | old_price
------------+---------------------+-----------+-----------
       2006 | Mechanical Keyboard |     79.99 |     89.99
(1 row)
Result (order status updates):
 order_id | customer_id | status  | shipped_date | total_amount
----------+-------------+---------+--------------+--------------
     3002 |        1001 | shipped | 2026-01-25   |       245.67
     3003 |        1003 | shipped | 2026-01-25   |       128.50
(2 rows)
Explanation: UPDATE...RETURNING shows updated values. First UPDATE changes price 89.99 → 79.99, RETURNING shows new_price (79.99). Note: old_price subquery won't work as shown (executes after update) - for true old/new comparison, use trigger or application-level tracking. Second UPDATE changes 2 orders to 'shipped', RETURNING confirms which orders modified. Useful for: audit logging (capture changed rows), optimistic locking (RETURNING version_number to verify), confirming updates succeeded, generating modification receipts. Application can log returned rows to audit table immediately. RETURNING particularly valuable when UPDATE affects multiple rows - shows exactly which rows changed.

🎯 Example 3: DELETE with RETURNING (Archive Before Delete)

-- Create archive table for deleted products
CREATE TABLE products_archive (
    product_id INTEGER,
    product_name VARCHAR(100),
    category_id INTEGER,
    price NUMERIC(10,2),
    deleted_date TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

-- Delete discontinued products and capture deleted data
WITH deleted_products AS (
    DELETE FROM products
    WHERE stock_quantity = 0 
      AND product_name LIKE '%Discontinued%'
    RETURNING product_id, product_name, category_id, price
)
INSERT INTO products_archive (product_id, product_name, category_id, price)
SELECT product_id, product_name, category_id, price
FROM deleted_products
RETURNING product_id, product_name, deleted_date;
Result:
 product_id |      product_name        |     deleted_date
------------+--------------------------+-------------------------
       2010 | Old Keyboard Discontinued | 2026-01-25 11:05:00
       2011 | Old Mouse Discontinued    | 2026-01-25 11:05:00
(2 rows)
Explanation: CTE (WITH clause) with RETURNING enables atomic archive-then-delete pattern. deleted_products CTE executes DELETE and captures deleted rows via RETURNING. Outer INSERT moves deleted data to products_archive. Final RETURNING shows archived rows with deleted_date. Entire operation atomic - either both DELETE and INSERT succeed, or both rollback. Alternative approach (less efficient): INSERT INTO archive SELECT...; then DELETE FROM products - not atomic, requires two queries. CTE pattern ensures no data loss (deleted rows guaranteed archived). Common use case: soft deletes, compliance (retain deleted data), data warehousing, audit trails. PostgreSQL-specific feature (CTE with DML statements).

🎯 Example 4: RETURNING with Expressions and Calculations

-- Insert order items with calculated totals
INSERT INTO order_items (order_id, product_id, quantity, unit_price)
VALUES 
    (3004, 2006, 2, 79.99),
    (3004, 2007, 1, 39.99),
    (3004, 2008, 3, 24.99)
RETURNING 
    item_id,
    order_id,
    product_id,
    quantity,
    unit_price,
    (quantity * unit_price) AS line_total,
    (quantity * unit_price * 0.08) AS tax_amount,
    (quantity * unit_price * 1.08) AS total_with_tax;

-- Update customer and return calculated fields
UPDATE customers
SET city = 'Boston', country = 'USA'
WHERE customer_id = 1003
RETURNING 
    customer_id,
    first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name,
    UPPER(email) AS email_uppercase,
    city,
    LENGTH(city) AS city_length,
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP AS updated_at;
Result (order items):
 item_id | order_id | product_id | quantity | unit_price | line_total | tax_amount | total_with_tax
---------+----------+------------+----------+------------+------------+------------+----------------
    5004 |     3004 |       2006 |        2 |      79.99 |     159.98 |      12.80 |         172.78
    5005 |     3004 |       2007 |        1 |      39.99 |      39.99 |       3.20 |          43.19
    5006 |     3004 |       2008 |        3 |      24.99 |      74.97 |       6.00 |          80.97
(3 rows)
Result (customer update):
 customer_id |   full_name   |      email_uppercase       |  city  | city_length |       updated_at
-------------+---------------+----------------------------+--------+-------------+-------------------------
        1003 | Charlie Brown | CHARLIE.BROWN@EMAIL.COM    | Boston |           6 | 2026-01-25 11:10:00
(1 row)
Explanation: RETURNING supports arbitrary expressions and calculations - not just column values. First INSERT calculates line totals (quantity * unit_price = 159.98, 39.99, 74.97), tax (line_total * 0.08), and totals with tax (line_total * 1.08). Eliminates separate calculation queries - application gets computed values immediately. Second UPDATE returns string concatenation (full_name), function calls (UPPER, LENGTH), and current timestamp. RETURNING can use: arithmetic operators (+, -, *, /), string functions (CONCAT, UPPER, SUBSTRING), date functions (CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, EXTRACT), aggregate functions (in subqueries), CASE expressions, type casts. Powerful for: generating invoices, computing derived fields, formatting data for application layer, avoiding redundant queries.

🎯 Example 5: RETURNING in Complex CTE Chains

-- Complex multi-table operation: move completed orders to archive
WITH completed_orders AS (
    -- Step 1: Select completed orders
    SELECT order_id, customer_id, order_date, total_amount
    FROM orders
    WHERE status = 'completed' AND order_date < '2025-01-01'
),
deleted_orders AS (
    -- Step 2: Delete from orders table
    DELETE FROM orders
    WHERE order_id IN (SELECT order_id FROM completed_orders)
    RETURNING order_id, customer_id, order_date, total_amount
),
archived_orders AS (
    -- Step 3: Insert into archive
    INSERT INTO orders_archive (order_id, customer_id, order_date, total_amount)
    SELECT order_id, customer_id, order_date, total_amount
    FROM deleted_orders
    RETURNING order_id, total_amount
)
-- Step 4: Return summary
SELECT 
    COUNT(*) AS archived_count,
    SUM(total_amount) AS total_archived_amount,
    MIN(order_id) AS first_order_id,
    MAX(order_id) AS last_order_id
FROM archived_orders;
Result:
 archived_count | total_archived_amount | first_order_id | last_order_id
----------------+-----------------------+----------------+---------------
             23 |              12,458.67 |           3010 |          3032
(1 row)
Explanation: CTEs with RETURNING enable complex multi-step data pipelines in single atomic transaction. Chain: completed_orders (SELECT) → deleted_orders (DELETE with RETURNING) → archived_orders (INSERT with RETURNING) → final summary (aggregate). Each CTE passes data to next via RETURNING. Entire operation atomic - all steps succeed or all rollback (no partial completion). Benefits: single query (not multiple round-trips), guaranteed consistency, easier to read than nested subqueries, efficient execution plan. Use cases: data archival, ETL operations, complex updates across tables, audit logging, maintaining derived tables. Alternative (multi-query approach) risks: partial failures, inconsistent state, higher latency. CTE pattern ensures data integrity and reduces code complexity.

🔍 Chapter 7: Queries

About SQL Queries

Queries are the heart of SQL - they retrieve data from databases using the SELECT statement. This chapter covers all aspects of querying: from simple data retrieval to complex multi-table joins, aggregations, window functions, and recursive queries. PostgreSQL's query capabilities extend far beyond basic SQL, offering advanced features like CTEs, window functions, and sophisticated set operations.

A complete SELECT statement consists of several clauses: FROM (data sources), WHERE (filtering), GROUP BY/HAVING (aggregation), SELECT (output columns), ORDER BY (sorting), and LIMIT/OFFSET (pagination). Understanding how these clauses interact and the order PostgreSQL processes them is crucial for writing efficient, correct queries. This chapter builds from simple queries to complex analytical operations.

📊

Table Expressions

FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING - building query foundations

📋

Select Lists

Column selection, labels, DISTINCT for unique results

🔄

Set Operations

UNION, INTERSECT, EXCEPT for combining queries

🪟

Window Functions

Advanced analytics with OVER clause

🔗

Common Table Expressions

WITH queries for readable, modular SQL

♻️

Recursive Queries

Hierarchical data traversal with recursive CTEs

7.1 Overview

📖 Full Description

SQL Query Structure follows a specific syntax with multiple optional clauses. The basic form is SELECT columns FROM tables, but can expand to include WHERE (filtering), GROUP BY (aggregation), HAVING (aggregate filtering), ORDER BY (sorting), and LIMIT/OFFSET (pagination). PostgreSQL processes these clauses in a specific order: FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → DISTINCT → ORDER BY → LIMIT/OFFSET.

Processing Order: Understanding execution order is critical. PostgreSQL first identifies data sources (FROM), filters rows (WHERE), groups results (GROUP BY), filters groups (HAVING), computes output columns (SELECT), removes duplicates (DISTINCT), sorts results (ORDER BY), and finally limits output (LIMIT/OFFSET). This order affects what columns/expressions are available in each clause - for example, WHERE can't reference column aliases from SELECT.

Query Components: SELECT clause specifies output columns (can be expressions, aggregates, or *), FROM clause specifies tables/views/subqueries, WHERE filters individual rows, GROUP BY aggregates rows into groups, HAVING filters groups, ORDER BY sorts output, LIMIT restricts row count. Queries can join multiple tables, use subqueries, include CTEs (WITH), and combine results with UNION/INTERSECT/EXCEPT.

Complete SELECT Syntax
-- Complete query structure (all clauses optional except SELECT)
SELECT [DISTINCT | DISTINCT ON (expressions)] select_list
FROM table_expression
[WHERE condition]
[GROUP BY grouping_expressions]
[HAVING group_filter_condition]
[WINDOW window_definition]
[{ UNION | INTERSECT | EXCEPT } [ALL | DISTINCT] select]
[ORDER BY sort_expression [ASC | DESC] [NULLS {FIRST | LAST}]]
[LIMIT count] [OFFSET start]
[FETCH {FIRST | NEXT} count {ROW | ROWS} ONLY];

-- Simple query example
SELECT column1, column2
FROM table_name
WHERE condition
ORDER BY column1;

-- Query with aggregation
SELECT category_id, COUNT(*), AVG(price)
FROM products
WHERE stock_quantity > 0
GROUP BY category_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5
ORDER BY AVG(price) DESC;

🎯 Example 1: Basic SELECT Query

-- Select all columns from customers
SELECT *
FROM customers;

-- Select specific columns
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name, email
FROM customers;

-- Select with column alias
SELECT 
    customer_id AS id,
    first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name,
    email AS contact_email
FROM customers
LIMIT 5;
Result:
  id  |   full_name   |       contact_email
------+---------------+---------------------------
 1001 | Alice Johnson | alice.johnson@email.com
 1002 | Bob Smith     | bob.smith@email.com
 1003 | Charlie Brown | charlie.brown@email.com
 1004 | Diana Prince  | diana.prince@email.com
 1005 | Edward Norton | edward.norton@email.com
(5 rows)
Explanation: SELECT * retrieves all columns (use cautiously - may return too much data). SELECT with specific columns more efficient (smaller result set, clearer intent). Column aliases (AS) rename output columns - useful for expressions (first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name). LIMIT 5 restricts output to 5 rows (pagination). Aliases don't affect stored data, only query output. PostgreSQL supports both AS keyword and implicit aliases (column alias - without AS).

🎯 Example 2: Query Processing Order Demonstration

-- This query demonstrates clause processing order
SELECT 
    category_id,
    COUNT(*) AS product_count,
    AVG(price) AS avg_price,
    AVG(price) * 1.2 AS avg_price_with_markup
FROM products
WHERE stock_quantity > 0           -- Filters BEFORE grouping
GROUP BY category_id               -- Groups after filtering
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3               -- Filters groups
ORDER BY avg_price DESC            -- Can use SELECT alias
LIMIT 3;

-- This FAILS - WHERE can't use SELECT aliases
-- SELECT category_id, COUNT(*) AS product_count
-- FROM products
-- WHERE product_count > 5;  -- ERROR: column "product_count" doesn't exist
Result:
 category_id | product_count | avg_price | avg_price_with_markup
-------------+---------------+-----------+-----------------------
           2 |             5 |     45.99 |                 55.19
           1 |             8 |     39.50 |                 47.40
           3 |             3 |     24.99 |                 29.99
(3 rows)
Explanation: PostgreSQL processes clauses in specific order: FROM (identify products table) → WHERE (filter stock_quantity > 0) → GROUP BY (group by category_id) → HAVING (keep groups with 3+ products) → SELECT (calculate COUNT, AVG) → ORDER BY (sort by avg_price - can reference SELECT alias) → LIMIT (keep top 3). WHERE operates on individual rows before grouping - can't reference aggregates or SELECT aliases. HAVING operates on groups after aggregation - can reference aggregates. ORDER BY executes after SELECT - can use column aliases. Understanding this order prevents common errors.

🎯 Example 3: Query with Multiple Tables (Preview of Joins)

-- Select data from multiple tables using JOIN
SELECT 
    c.customer_id,
    c.first_name,
    c.last_name,
    o.order_id,
    o.order_date,
    o.total_amount
FROM customers c
INNER JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
WHERE o.order_date >= '2026-01-01'
ORDER BY o.order_date DESC
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 customer_id | first_name | last_name | order_id | order_date | total_amount
-------------+------------+-----------+----------+------------+--------------
        1003 | Charlie    | Brown     |     3005 | 2026-01-24 |       289.99
        1001 | Alice      | Johnson   |     3004 | 2026-01-23 |       334.94
        1004 | Diana      | Prince    |     3006 | 2026-01-22 |       159.50
        1001 | Alice      | Johnson   |     3001 | 2026-01-21 |       157.94
        1005 | Edward     | Norton    |     3007 | 2026-01-20 |       445.00
(5 rows)
Explanation: Queries can retrieve data from multiple tables using JOIN. INNER JOIN combines customers and orders where customer_id matches. Table aliases (c, o) shorten syntax. JOIN detailed in section 7.2.1. FROM clause executed first - establishes which tables to query. WHERE filters combined rows (order_date >= '2026-01-01'). ORDER BY sorts by date descending (newest first). LIMIT restricts to 5 rows. Multi-table queries essential for normalized databases where data split across tables.

🎯 Example 4: Subqueries in SELECT

-- Query with scalar subquery in SELECT clause
SELECT 
    customer_id,
    first_name,
    last_name,
    (SELECT COUNT(*) 
     FROM orders 
     WHERE orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id) AS order_count,
    (SELECT SUM(total_amount) 
     FROM orders 
     WHERE orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id) AS total_spent
FROM customers
WHERE city = 'Seattle'
ORDER BY total_spent DESC NULLS LAST;
Result:
 customer_id | first_name | last_name | order_count | total_spent
-------------+------------+-----------+-------------+-------------
        1001 | Alice      | Johnson   |           3 |      647.82
        1008 | Henry      | Ford      |           2 |      389.50
        1012 | Laura      | Palmer    |           1 |      125.00
        1015 | Oscar      | Wilde     |           0 |        NULL
(4 rows)
Explanation: Subqueries can appear in SELECT clause to calculate derived values. Scalar subquery returns single value for each customer row. First subquery counts orders (correlated via customer_id), second sums total_amount. Subqueries execute for EACH row (performance consideration). WHERE city = 'Seattle' filters to Seattle customers. ORDER BY total_spent DESC NULLS LAST sorts by spending (customers with no orders return NULL, appear last). Alternative: use LEFT JOIN with GROUP BY (typically more efficient). Subqueries provide flexibility but can be slow - consider JOINs for better performance.

🎯 Example 5: Complete Query with All Clauses

-- Comprehensive query demonstrating all major clauses
SELECT 
    DISTINCT ON (p.category_id)
    p.category_id,
    c.category_name,
    p.product_name,
    p.price,
    (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM order_items WHERE product_id = p.product_id) AS times_ordered
FROM products p
INNER JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.category_id
INNER JOIN order_items oi ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
WHERE p.stock_quantity > 0 
  AND oi.order_id IN (SELECT order_id FROM orders WHERE order_date >= '2026-01-01')
GROUP BY p.category_id, c.category_name, p.product_id, p.product_name, p.price
HAVING SUM(oi.quantity) > 10
ORDER BY p.category_id, p.price DESC
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 category_id | category_name |    product_name     | price | times_ordered
-------------+---------------+---------------------+-------+---------------
           1 | Electronics   | Mechanical Keyboard | 79.99 |            15
           2 | Furniture     | Laptop Stand        | 49.99 |            12
           3 | Stationery    | Notebook Set        | 15.99 |            28
(3 rows)
Explanation: Complete query showcasing major features: DISTINCT ON (return first row per category_id), multiple JOINs (products → categories → order_items), WHERE with subquery (filter to 2026 orders), GROUP BY (aggregate by category/product), HAVING (keep products with 10+ units sold), ORDER BY (sort by category, then price DESC), LIMIT 5 (top 5 results). Processing: FROM/JOIN (combine tables) → WHERE (filter in-stock products in recent orders) → GROUP BY (group for aggregation) → HAVING (filter high-volume products) → SELECT (compute output including subquery) → DISTINCT ON (keep first per category) → ORDER BY → LIMIT. Demonstrates how clauses work together for complex analysis.

7.2 Table Expressions

📖 About Table Expressions

Table expressions in the FROM clause specify data sources and filtering logic. They combine FROM (source tables), JOIN operations (combining tables), WHERE (row filtering), GROUP BY (aggregation), HAVING (group filtering), and window functions. Table expressions form the foundation of queries - they determine which data is available to the SELECT clause.

7.2.1 The FROM Clause

📖 Full Description

FROM Clause specifies data sources for queries - tables, views, subqueries, or combinations via JOINs. Basic syntax: FROM table_name. PostgreSQL supports multiple join types: INNER JOIN (matching rows only), LEFT/RIGHT JOIN (include non-matches from one side), FULL JOIN (include all non-matches), CROSS JOIN (Cartesian product). JOIN conditions specified with ON clause (equality or complex conditions) or USING clause (matching column names).

Join Types: INNER JOIN returns only rows with matches in both tables. LEFT JOIN returns all left table rows plus matches from right (NULL if no match). RIGHT JOIN opposite of LEFT. FULL JOIN returns all rows from both tables (NULL for non-matches). CROSS JOIN produces Cartesian product (every left row paired with every right row). Self-joins join table to itself (requires aliases). Multiple joins can be chained.

Best Practices: Use table aliases for clarity (customers c), always qualify column names in joins (c.customer_id vs o.customer_id), choose appropriate join type (INNER vs LEFT), use USING when join columns have same name, consider join order for performance (smaller tables first in some cases), use EXPLAIN to verify join strategy. Avoid CROSS JOIN unless intentional (can produce huge result sets).

FROM and JOIN Syntax
-- Basic FROM
SELECT * FROM table_name;

-- Table alias
SELECT * FROM table_name AS alias_name;
SELECT * FROM table_name alias_name;  -- AS optional

-- INNER JOIN
SELECT columns
FROM table1 t1
INNER JOIN table2 t2 ON t1.id = t2.id;

-- LEFT/RIGHT/FULL JOIN
SELECT columns
FROM table1 t1
LEFT JOIN table2 t2 ON t1.id = t2.id;

-- Multiple joins
SELECT columns
FROM table1 t1
INNER JOIN table2 t2 ON t1.id = t2.id
LEFT JOIN table3 t3 ON t2.id = t3.id;

-- USING clause (when column names match)
SELECT columns
FROM table1 t1
INNER JOIN table2 t2 USING (id);

-- CROSS JOIN
SELECT columns
FROM table1
CROSS JOIN table2;

-- Self join
SELECT columns
FROM table_name t1
INNER JOIN table_name t2 ON t1.parent_id = t2.id;

🎯 Example 1: INNER JOIN (Matching Rows Only)

-- Get customers with orders (excludes customers with no orders)
SELECT 
    c.customer_id,
    c.first_name || ' ' || c.last_name AS customer_name,
    COUNT(o.order_id) AS order_count,
    SUM(o.total_amount) AS total_spent
FROM customers c
INNER JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.first_name, c.last_name
ORDER BY total_spent DESC
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 customer_id | customer_name | order_count | total_spent
-------------+---------------+-------------+-------------
        1001 | Alice Johnson |           3 |      647.82
        1003 | Charlie Brown |           2 |      418.49
        1005 | Edward Norton |           2 |      534.50
        1004 | Diana Prince  |           1 |      159.50
        1008 | Henry Ford    |           2 |      389.50
(5 rows)
Explanation: INNER JOIN returns only customers with matching orders. Customers without orders excluded from results. ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id specifies join condition. Table aliases (c, o) shorten syntax and improve readability. GROUP BY aggregates orders per customer. COUNT counts orders, SUM totals spending. INNER JOIN most common - use when need only matching records. If customer 1015 has no orders, won't appear in result.

🎯 Example 2: LEFT JOIN (Include Non-Matching Left Table Rows)

-- Get ALL customers, including those without orders
SELECT 
    c.customer_id,
    c.first_name,
    c.last_name,
    COUNT(o.order_id) AS order_count,
    COALESCE(SUM(o.total_amount), 0) AS total_spent
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.first_name, c.last_name
HAVING COUNT(o.order_id) = 0
ORDER BY c.customer_id
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 customer_id | first_name | last_name | order_count | total_spent
-------------+------------+-----------+-------------+-------------
        1015 | Oscar      | Wilde     |           0 |        0.00
        1020 | Patricia   | Moore     |           0 |        0.00
        1025 | Quinn      | Adams     |           0 |        0.00
        1030 | Rachel     | Green     |           0 |        0.00
        1035 | Samuel     | Taylor    |           0 |        0.00
(5 rows)
Explanation: LEFT JOIN returns ALL rows from left table (customers) even without matches in right table (orders). Non-matching right table columns become NULL. COALESCE(SUM(o.total_amount), 0) converts NULL to 0 for customers without orders. HAVING COUNT(o.order_id) = 0 filters to customers with no orders. COUNT(o.order_id) counts non-NULL order_id values (0 for no orders). Use LEFT JOIN when need all left table rows regardless of matches. RIGHT JOIN is mirror image (all right rows).

🎯 Example 3: Multiple Joins (Chaining Tables)

-- Get order details with customer and product information
SELECT 
    o.order_id,
    c.first_name || ' ' || c.last_name AS customer_name,
    p.product_name,
    oi.quantity,
    oi.unit_price,
    (oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS line_total
FROM orders o
INNER JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
INNER JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
INNER JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
WHERE o.order_date >= '2026-01-20'
ORDER BY o.order_id, oi.item_id
LIMIT 10;
Result:
 order_id | customer_name  |    product_name     | quantity | unit_price | line_total
----------+----------------+---------------------+----------+------------+------------
     3001 | Alice Johnson  | Wireless Mouse      |        2 |      29.99 |      59.98
     3001 | Alice Johnson  | Laptop Stand        |        1 |      49.99 |      49.99
     3001 | Alice Johnson  | Notebook Set        |        3 |      15.99 |      47.97
     3004 | Alice Johnson  | Mechanical Keyboard |        2 |      79.99 |     159.98
     3004 | Alice Johnson  | Monitor Stand       |        1 |      39.99 |      39.99
     3004 | Alice Johnson  | Desk Organizer      |        3 |      24.99 |      74.97
     3005 | Charlie Brown  | Wireless Mouse      |        1 |      29.99 |      29.99
     3005 | Charlie Brown  | USB-C Cable         |        5 |      12.99 |      64.95
     3007 | Edward Norton  | Laptop Stand        |        3 |      49.99 |     149.97
     3007 | Edward Norton  | Mechanical Keyboard |        2 |      79.99 |     159.98
(10 rows)
Explanation: Multiple joins chain tables together: orders → customers (get customer info), orders → order_items (get items in order), order_items → products (get product details). Each INNER JOIN requires ON condition specifying relationship. Join order doesn't affect results (relational algebra) but can affect performance. PostgreSQL query optimizer typically handles join order efficiently. This pattern common in normalized databases where data split across tables. Four-table join creates complete order view combining customer, product, and transaction data.

🎯 Example 4: CROSS JOIN and Self Join

-- CROSS JOIN: Generate all possible product-category combinations
SELECT 
    p.product_name,
    c.category_name,
    CASE 
        WHEN p.category_id = c.category_id THEN 'Current Category'
        ELSE 'Alternative Category'
    END AS relationship
FROM products p
CROSS JOIN categories c
WHERE p.product_id = 2001
ORDER BY c.category_id
LIMIT 5;

-- Self Join: Find products in same category as 'Wireless Mouse'
SELECT 
    p1.product_name AS reference_product,
    p2.product_name AS related_product,
    p2.price,
    p2.stock_quantity
FROM products p1
INNER JOIN products p2 ON p1.category_id = p2.category_id
WHERE p1.product_name = 'Wireless Mouse'
  AND p2.product_id != p1.product_id
ORDER BY p2.price;
Result (CROSS JOIN):
  product_name  | category_name |    relationship
----------------+---------------+---------------------
 Wireless Mouse | Electronics   | Current Category
 Wireless Mouse | Furniture     | Alternative Category
 Wireless Mouse | Stationery    | Alternative Category
 Wireless Mouse | Office Supplies| Alternative Category
 Wireless Mouse | Accessories   | Alternative Category
(5 rows)
Result (Self Join):
 reference_product |  related_product  | price | stock_quantity
-------------------+-------------------+-------+----------------
 Wireless Mouse    | USB-C Cable       | 14.29 |            295
 Wireless Mouse    | Mechanical Keyboard| 79.99 |             50
(2 rows)
Explanation: CROSS JOIN produces Cartesian product - every row from first table paired with every row from second table. Wireless Mouse (1 product) × 5 categories = 5 rows. Use cautiously (large tables create huge results). Self join joins table to itself using different aliases (p1, p2). Find products in same category: p1.category_id = p2.category_id. p2.product_id != p1.product_id excludes same product. Self joins useful for hierarchical data (employees/managers), comparing rows within table, finding related records.

🎯 Example 5: USING Clause and FULL OUTER JOIN

-- USING clause (when join columns have same name)
SELECT 
    customer_id,
    first_name,
    order_id,
    order_date,
    total_amount
FROM customers
INNER JOIN orders USING (customer_id)
WHERE order_date >= '2026-01-23'
ORDER BY order_date DESC
LIMIT 3;

-- FULL OUTER JOIN: Show all customers and all orders (even orphaned)
SELECT 
    c.customer_id,
    c.first_name,
    c.last_name,
    o.order_id,
    o.total_amount
FROM customers c
FULL OUTER JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
WHERE c.customer_id IS NULL OR o.order_id IS NULL
LIMIT 5;
Result (USING):
 customer_id | first_name | order_id | order_date | total_amount
-------------+------------+----------+------------+--------------
        1003 | Charlie    |     3005 | 2026-01-24 |       289.99
        1001 | Alice      |     3004 | 2026-01-23 |       334.94
(2 rows)
Result (FULL OUTER JOIN - showing non-matches):
 customer_id | first_name | last_name | order_id | total_amount
-------------+------------+-----------+----------+--------------
        1015 | Oscar      | Wilde     |     NULL |         NULL
        1020 | Patricia   | Moore     |     NULL |         NULL
        NULL | NULL       | NULL      |     9999 |       125.00
(3 rows)
Explanation: USING clause simplifies joins when column names identical in both tables. USING (customer_id) equivalent to ON customers.customer_id = orders.customer_id. USING returns single customer_id column (not duplicated). More concise than ON for natural joins. FULL OUTER JOIN returns all rows from both tables - customers without orders (customer_id IS NULL in result shows order with invalid customer), and orphaned orders (order_id IS NULL would show customers with no orders). WHERE filters to non-matching rows only (demonstrating FULL JOIN capability). FULL JOIN rare in practice - usually use LEFT or INNER JOIN.

7.2.2 The WHERE Clause

📖 Full Description

WHERE Clause filters rows before grouping and aggregation. Syntax: WHERE condition. Conditions use comparison operators (=, !=, <, >, <=, >=), logical operators (AND, OR, NOT), pattern matching (LIKE, ILIKE), range checks (BETWEEN, IN), NULL checks (IS NULL, IS NOT NULL), and subqueries (EXISTS, IN, ANY, ALL). WHERE processes individual rows - cannot reference aggregate functions or SELECT aliases.

Operators: Comparison: = (equal), != or <> (not equal), < (less than), > (greater than), <= (less/equal), >= (greater/equal). Logical: AND (both conditions true), OR (either true), NOT (negate). Pattern: LIKE (case-sensitive pattern), ILIKE (case-insensitive), % (any characters), _ (single character). Range: BETWEEN x AND y (inclusive), IN (list membership), NOT IN (exclusion). NULL: IS NULL (is null), IS NOT NULL (not null) - never use = NULL.

Best Practices: Use indexes on WHERE columns for performance, avoid functions on indexed columns (price > 100 not ROUND(price) > 100), use BETWEEN for ranges, use IN for multiple values, use EXISTS instead of IN for subqueries (often faster), test for NULL with IS NULL (not = NULL), combine conditions with AND/OR (use parentheses for clarity), use prepared statements to prevent SQL injection.

WHERE Clause Syntax
-- Basic comparison
WHERE column = value
WHERE column > value
WHERE column BETWEEN value1 AND value2

-- Logical operators
WHERE condition1 AND condition2
WHERE condition1 OR condition2
WHERE NOT condition

-- Pattern matching
WHERE column LIKE 'pattern%'
WHERE column ILIKE '%pattern%'  -- Case-insensitive

-- IN operator
WHERE column IN (value1, value2, value3)
WHERE column IN (SELECT column FROM table)

-- NULL checks
WHERE column IS NULL
WHERE column IS NOT NULL

-- Subqueries
WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM table WHERE condition)
WHERE column = ANY (SELECT column FROM table)
WHERE column > ALL (SELECT column FROM table)

-- Complex conditions
WHERE (condition1 OR condition2) AND condition3

🎯 Example 1: Basic WHERE with Comparison Operators

-- Simple equality
SELECT product_id, product_name, price, stock_quantity
FROM products
WHERE category_id = 1;

-- Range comparison
SELECT product_id, product_name, price
FROM products
WHERE price >= 30 AND price <= 100
ORDER BY price;

-- BETWEEN (inclusive range)
SELECT product_id, product_name, price
FROM products
WHERE price BETWEEN 30 AND 100
ORDER BY price;
Result (BETWEEN):
 product_id |    product_name     | price
------------+---------------------+-------
       2007 | Monitor Stand       | 39.99
       2003 | Laptop Stand        | 49.99
       2006 | Mechanical Keyboard | 79.99
(3 rows)
Explanation: WHERE filters rows matching conditions. category_id = 1 returns only Electronics products. price >= 30 AND price <= 100 finds products in price range - both conditions must be true (AND). BETWEEN 30 AND 100 equivalent to >= 30 AND <= 100 (inclusive). BETWEEN more readable for range queries. PostgreSQL can use indexes on WHERE columns for fast filtering. Without WHERE, query returns all rows.

🎯 Example 2: Pattern Matching with LIKE and ILIKE

-- LIKE: Case-sensitive pattern matching
SELECT product_id, product_name
FROM products
WHERE product_name LIKE 'M%'  -- Starts with 'M'
ORDER BY product_name;

-- ILIKE: Case-insensitive (PostgreSQL-specific)
SELECT product_id, product_name
FROM products
WHERE product_name ILIKE '%mouse%'  -- Contains 'mouse' (any case)
ORDER BY product_name;

-- Multiple wildcards
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name, email
FROM customers
WHERE email LIKE '%@gmail.com'  -- Gmail addresses
   OR email LIKE '%@yahoo.com'  -- Yahoo addresses
ORDER BY last_name;
Result (ILIKE '%mouse%'):
 product_id |  product_name
------------+----------------
       2001 | Wireless Mouse
       2011 | Gaming Mouse
(2 rows)
Explanation: LIKE performs pattern matching: % matches any characters (zero or more), _ matches single character. 'M%' finds names starting with M. '%mouse%' finds names containing 'mouse'. LIKE case-sensitive (Mouse != mouse), ILIKE case-insensitive (PostgreSQL extension). '%@gmail.com' finds emails ending with @gmail.com. OR combines conditions (either matches). Pattern matching slower than exact equality - use indexes on pattern columns (text_pattern_ops). For full-text search, use PostgreSQL's text search features.

🎯 Example 3: IN Operator and NULL Handling

-- IN with list of values
SELECT order_id, customer_id, status, total_amount
FROM orders
WHERE status IN ('pending', 'processing', 'shipped')
ORDER BY order_date DESC
LIMIT 5;

-- IN with subquery
SELECT product_id, product_name, price
FROM products
WHERE product_id IN (
    SELECT DISTINCT product_id 
    FROM order_items 
    WHERE quantity > 5
)
ORDER BY price DESC;

-- NULL handling
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name, city
FROM customers
WHERE city IS NULL
LIMIT 3;

-- NOT NULL
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name, city
FROM customers
WHERE city IS NOT NULL
  AND country = 'USA'
LIMIT 5;
Result (IN with subquery):
 product_id |  product_name  | price
------------+----------------+-------
       2006 | Mechanical Keyboard | 79.99
       2003 | Laptop Stand   | 49.99
       2001 | Wireless Mouse | 32.99
       2002 | USB-C Cable    | 14.29
(4 rows)
Result (NULL handling):
 customer_id | first_name | last_name | city
-------------+------------+-----------+------
        1002 | Bob        | Smith     | NULL
        1018 | Karen      | Wilson    | NULL
        1027 | Thomas     | Anderson  | NULL
(3 rows)
Explanation: IN checks if value matches any in list: status IN ('pending', 'processing', 'shipped') equivalent to status = 'pending' OR status = 'processing' OR status = 'shipped'. IN more concise for multiple values. IN with subquery finds products ordered in quantities > 5. NULL requires IS NULL or IS NOT NULL - never use = NULL or != NULL (always returns NULL/false). NULL represents unknown value. IS NOT NULL filters to non-null cities. Combining IS NOT NULL with other conditions ensures valid data.

🎯 Example 4: Subqueries with EXISTS, ANY, ALL

-- EXISTS: Check if subquery returns any rows
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name
FROM customers c
WHERE EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 
    FROM orders o 
    WHERE o.customer_id = c.customer_id 
      AND o.total_amount > 500
)
ORDER BY customer_id;

-- NOT EXISTS: Find customers with no orders
SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name
FROM customers c
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 
    FROM orders o 
    WHERE o.customer_id = c.customer_id
)
LIMIT 5;

-- ANY: Match any value from subquery
SELECT product_id, product_name, price
FROM products
WHERE price > ANY (
    SELECT AVG(price) 
    FROM products 
    GROUP BY category_id
)
ORDER BY price DESC
LIMIT 5;

-- ALL: Match all values from subquery
SELECT product_id, product_name, price
FROM products
WHERE price > ALL (
    SELECT price 
    FROM products 
    WHERE category_id = 3
)
ORDER BY price DESC;
Result (EXISTS):
 customer_id | first_name | last_name
-------------+------------+-----------
        1001 | Alice      | Johnson
        1005 | Edward     | Norton
(2 rows)
Result (NOT EXISTS):
 customer_id | first_name | last_name
-------------+------------+-----------
        1015 | Oscar      | Wilde
        1020 | Patricia   | Moore
        1025 | Quinn      | Adams
(3 rows)
Explanation: EXISTS returns true if subquery returns any rows - efficient for checking existence. SELECT 1 in EXISTS subquery arbitrary (only checking if rows exist). Correlated subquery (references outer query via c.customer_id). NOT EXISTS finds customers without orders. ANY returns true if comparison true for any subquery value: price > ANY (...) means price greater than at least one average. ALL returns true if comparison true for all subquery values: price > ALL (...) means price greater than every category 3 product. EXISTS typically faster than IN for subqueries - stops at first match.

🎯 Example 5: Complex WHERE Conditions

-- Complex combination with parentheses
SELECT 
    order_id,
    customer_id,
    order_date,
    status,
    total_amount
FROM orders
WHERE (
    (status = 'pending' AND total_amount > 100)
    OR
    (status = 'processing' AND order_date >= '2026-01-20')
)
AND customer_id IN (SELECT customer_id FROM customers WHERE country = 'USA')
ORDER BY order_date DESC;

-- Multiple conditions with AND/OR
SELECT 
    product_id,
    product_name,
    category_id,
    price,
    stock_quantity
FROM products
WHERE (price < 30 OR price > 70)
  AND stock_quantity > 0
  AND category_id IN (1, 2)
  AND product_name NOT LIKE '%Discontinued%'
ORDER BY category_id, price;
Result (complex conditions):
 product_id |    product_name     | category_id | price | stock_quantity
------------+---------------------+-------------+-------+----------------
       2002 | USB-C Cable         |           1 | 14.29 |            295
       2006 | Mechanical Keyboard |           1 | 79.99 |             50
       2008 | Desk Organizer      |           2 | 24.99 |            100
(3 rows)
Explanation: Complex WHERE conditions combine multiple criteria with AND/OR. Parentheses control evaluation order: ((status = 'pending' AND total_amount > 100) OR (status = 'processing' AND order_date >= '2026-01-20')) evaluates inner conditions first. Without parentheses, AND takes precedence over OR. Second query uses (price < 30 OR price > 70) to exclude mid-range products, combines with stock check, category filter, and name exclusion. All conditions connected with AND must be true. Use parentheses for clarity when mixing AND/OR. Complex conditions can impact performance - ensure indexed columns used appropriately.

7.2.3 The GROUP BY and HAVING Clauses

📖 Full Description

GROUP BY Clause aggregates rows sharing common values into summary rows. Syntax: GROUP BY column1, column2. Used with aggregate functions: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX, STRING_AGG, ARRAY_AGG. All SELECT columns must either be in GROUP BY or use aggregate functions. WHERE filters before grouping, HAVING filters after. GROUP BY enables reporting: totals by category, counts by status, averages by region.

HAVING Clause filters groups after aggregation - equivalent of WHERE for groups. Syntax: HAVING condition. Can reference aggregate functions (HAVING COUNT(*) > 10), unlike WHERE. Typically used with GROUP BY to filter aggregated results. Example: GROUP BY category_id HAVING AVG(price) > 50 keeps only categories with average price above 50.

Best Practices: Include all non-aggregated SELECT columns in GROUP BY, use WHERE to filter rows before grouping (faster), use HAVING for aggregate filtering, name aggregate columns with aliases for clarity, combine multiple aggregates in single query, use FILTER clause for conditional aggregates, order results with ORDER BY (can reference GROUP BY columns or aggregates). Understand difference: WHERE filters rows, HAVING filters groups.

GROUP BY and HAVING Syntax
-- Basic GROUP BY
SELECT column, aggregate_function(column2)
FROM table_name
GROUP BY column;

-- Multiple columns
SELECT column1, column2, COUNT(*)
FROM table_name
GROUP BY column1, column2;

-- With HAVING
SELECT column, COUNT(*)
FROM table_name
GROUP BY column
HAVING COUNT(*) > value;

-- Common aggregate functions
COUNT(*)           -- Count all rows in group
COUNT(column)      -- Count non-NULL values
SUM(column)        -- Sum numeric values
AVG(column)        -- Average of values
MIN(column)        -- Minimum value
MAX(column)        -- Maximum value
STRING_AGG(column, delimiter)  -- Concatenate strings
ARRAY_AGG(column)  -- Collect values into array

-- FILTER clause (conditional aggregation)
SELECT 
    category_id,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE price > 50) AS expensive_count,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE price <= 50) AS affordable_count
FROM products
GROUP BY category_id;

🎯 Example 1: Basic GROUP BY with Aggregate Functions

-- Count products by category
SELECT 
    category_id,
    COUNT(*) AS product_count,
    AVG(price) AS avg_price,
    MIN(price) AS min_price,
    MAX(price) AS max_price,
    SUM(stock_quantity) AS total_stock
FROM products
GROUP BY category_id
ORDER BY category_id;
Result:
 category_id | product_count | avg_price | min_price | max_price | total_stock
-------------+---------------+-----------+-----------+-----------+-------------
           1 |             8 |     39.50 |     14.29 |     79.99 |        1245
           2 |             5 |     45.99 |     24.99 |     69.99 |         650
           3 |             3 |     24.99 |     15.99 |     34.99 |         425
(3 rows)
Explanation: GROUP BY category_id groups all products by category. Each category becomes one output row. Aggregate functions summarize each group: COUNT(*) counts products, AVG(price) calculates average price, MIN/MAX find price range, SUM totals stock. category_id in SELECT must be in GROUP BY. All other SELECT columns must use aggregates. Without GROUP BY, aggregates would summarize entire table. GROUP BY essential for summary reports and analytics.

🎯 Example 2: GROUP BY Multiple Columns

-- Group orders by customer and status
SELECT 
    c.customer_id,
    c.first_name || ' ' || c.last_name AS customer_name,
    o.status,
    COUNT(o.order_id) AS order_count,
    SUM(o.total_amount) AS total_spent,
    AVG(o.total_amount) AS avg_order_value
FROM customers c
INNER JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.first_name, c.last_name, o.status
ORDER BY c.customer_id, o.status;
Result:
 customer_id | customer_name | status     | order_count | total_spent | avg_order_value
-------------+---------------+------------+-------------+-------------+-----------------
        1001 | Alice Johnson | confirmed  |           1 |      157.94 |          157.94
        1001 | Alice Johnson | shipped    |           2 |      489.88 |          244.94
        1003 | Charlie Brown | pending    |           1 |      289.99 |          289.99
        1003 | Charlie Brown | processing |           1 |      128.50 |          128.50
        1005 | Edward Norton | shipped    |           2 |      534.50 |          267.25
(5 rows)
Explanation: GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.first_name, c.last_name, o.status creates groups for each unique combination. Alice Johnson has 2 groups: confirmed (1 order) and shipped (2 orders). Multiple-column grouping enables drill-down analysis. All non-aggregated SELECT columns must appear in GROUP BY (customer_id, first_name, last_name, status). Aggregates (COUNT, SUM, AVG) calculated per group. Results show order behavior by customer and status - useful for business intelligence.

🎯 Example 3: HAVING Clause for Filtering Groups

-- Find categories with more than 3 products and avg price > 30
SELECT 
    category_id,
    COUNT(*) AS product_count,
    AVG(price) AS avg_price,
    SUM(stock_quantity) AS total_stock
FROM products
WHERE stock_quantity > 0    -- WHERE filters rows before grouping
GROUP BY category_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 3         -- HAVING filters groups after aggregation
   AND AVG(price) > 30
ORDER BY avg_price DESC;
Result:
 category_id | product_count | avg_price | total_stock
-------------+---------------+-----------+-------------
           2 |             5 |     45.99 |         650
           1 |             8 |     39.50 |        1245
(2 rows)
Explanation: WHERE stock_quantity > 0 filters individual products before grouping (excludes out-of-stock items). GROUP BY creates category groups. HAVING filters groups: COUNT(*) > 3 keeps categories with 4+ products, AVG(price) > 30 keeps categories with average price above 30. Category 3 excluded (either < 4 products or avg price ≤ 30). Key difference: WHERE filters rows (can't use aggregates), HAVING filters groups (can use aggregates). Use WHERE for row-level filtering (more efficient), HAVING for aggregate-based filtering.

🎯 Example 4: Advanced Aggregations with STRING_AGG and ARRAY_AGG

-- Aggregate order items by order
SELECT 
    o.order_id,
    o.customer_id,
    COUNT(oi.item_id) AS item_count,
    STRING_AGG(p.product_name, ', ' ORDER BY p.product_name) AS products,
    ARRAY_AGG(oi.quantity ORDER BY p.product_name) AS quantities,
    SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS order_total
FROM orders o
INNER JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
INNER JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
WHERE o.order_date >= '2026-01-20'
GROUP BY o.order_id, o.customer_id
ORDER BY o.order_id
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 order_id | customer_id | item_count |              products                | quantities | order_total
----------+-------------+------------+--------------------------------------+------------+-------------
     3001 |        1001 |          3 | Laptop Stand, Notebook Set, Wireless Mouse | {1,3,2} |      157.94
     3004 |        1001 |          3 | Desk Organizer, Mechanical Keyboard, Monitor Stand | {3,2,1} | 334.94
     3005 |        1003 |          2 | USB-C Cable, Wireless Mouse          | {5,1}      |       94.94
     3007 |        1005 |          2 | Laptop Stand, Mechanical Keyboard    | {3,2}      |      309.95
(4 rows)
Explanation: STRING_AGG concatenates values into delimited string: product names joined with ', '. ORDER BY within STRING_AGG sorts concatenated values alphabetically. ARRAY_AGG collects values into PostgreSQL array: quantities shown as {1,3,2}. Both useful for aggregating detail data into summary rows. Order 3001 has 3 items: Laptop Stand (qty 1), Notebook Set (qty 3), Wireless Mouse (qty 2). GROUP BY o.order_id groups items per order. These aggregates create compact summaries - one row per order with all details concatenated/collected.

🎯 Example 5: FILTER Clause for Conditional Aggregation

-- Analyze products by category with conditional counts
SELECT 
    category_id,
    COUNT(*) AS total_products,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE price < 30) AS budget_products,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE price BETWEEN 30 AND 70) AS mid_range_products,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE price > 70) AS premium_products,
    AVG(price) FILTER (WHERE stock_quantity > 0) AS avg_price_in_stock,
    SUM(stock_quantity) FILTER (WHERE price > 50) AS expensive_stock
FROM products
GROUP BY category_id
ORDER BY category_id;
Result:
 category_id | total_products | budget_products | mid_range_products | premium_products | avg_price_in_stock | expensive_stock
-------------+----------------+-----------------+--------------------+------------------+--------------------+-----------------
           1 |              8 |               1 |                  5 |                2 |              39.50 |             195
           2 |              5 |               1 |                  2 |                2 |              45.99 |             275
           3 |              3 |               3 |                  0 |                0 |              24.99 |               0
(3 rows)
Explanation: FILTER clause applies condition to specific aggregate: COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE price < 30) counts only products under $30. Each aggregate can have different filter. Category 1: 8 total products (1 budget <$30, 5 mid-range $30-70, 2 premium >$70). AVG(price) FILTER (WHERE stock_quantity > 0) averages only in-stock products. SUM(stock_quantity) FILTER (WHERE price > 50) totals stock of expensive items. FILTER more efficient than multiple queries or CASE expressions. PostgreSQL-specific feature - alternative: CASE WHEN inside aggregates (e.g., COUNT(CASE WHEN price < 30 THEN 1 END)).

7.2.4 GROUPING SETS, CUBE, and ROLLUP

📖 Full Description

GROUPING SETS, CUBE, and ROLLUP provide advanced GROUP BY functionality for multi-dimensional analysis and reporting. These features enable calculating multiple grouping levels in single query - essential for analytics, reporting, and OLAP operations. Instead of UNION of multiple GROUP BY queries, use these clauses for efficiency and clarity.

GROUPING SETS: Explicitly specifies which grouping combinations to compute. Syntax: GROUP BY GROUPING SETS ((col1), (col2), (col1, col2), ()). Each set in parentheses becomes separate grouping. () produces grand total. More flexible than ROLLUP/CUBE.

ROLLUP: Creates hierarchical subtotals from right to left. GROUP BY ROLLUP (a, b, c) produces: (a,b,c), (a,b), (a), (). Useful for hierarchical data (year→month→day, country→state→city). Generates n+1 grouping levels for n columns.

CUBE: Creates all possible grouping combinations (power set). GROUP BY CUBE (a, b) produces: (a,b), (a), (b), (). Generates 2^n grouping levels for n columns. Useful for multi-dimensional analysis but can produce many rows.

GROUPING SETS, CUBE, ROLLUP Syntax
-- GROUPING SETS: Specify exact groupings
SELECT col1, col2, aggregate_function(col3)
FROM table_name
GROUP BY GROUPING SETS (
    (col1, col2),    -- Group by both
    (col1),          -- Group by col1 only
    (col2),          -- Group by col2 only
    ()               -- Grand total (all rows)
);

-- ROLLUP: Hierarchical subtotals (right to left)
SELECT col1, col2, col3, SUM(amount)
FROM table_name
GROUP BY ROLLUP (col1, col2, col3);
-- Produces: (col1,col2,col3), (col1,col2), (col1), ()

-- CUBE: All possible combinations
SELECT col1, col2, SUM(amount)
FROM table_name
GROUP BY CUBE (col1, col2);
-- Produces: (col1,col2), (col1), (col2), ()

-- GROUPING function: Identify aggregation level
SELECT 
    col1,
    col2,
    GROUPING(col1) AS is_col1_total,
    GROUPING(col2) AS is_col2_total,
    SUM(amount)
FROM table_name
GROUP BY ROLLUP (col1, col2);
-- GROUPING returns 1 if column NULL represents aggregation, 0 otherwise

🎯 Example 1: GROUPING SETS for Custom Groupings

-- Analyze sales by category, status, and combinations
SELECT 
    category_id,
    CASE 
        WHEN GROUPING(category_id) = 1 THEN 'ALL CATEGORIES'
        ELSE category_id::TEXT
    END AS category,
    COUNT(*) AS product_count,
    SUM(stock_quantity) AS total_stock,
    AVG(price)::NUMERIC(10,2) AS avg_price
FROM products
WHERE stock_quantity > 0
GROUP BY GROUPING SETS (
    (category_id),     -- By category
    ()                 -- Grand total
)
ORDER BY GROUPING(category_id), category_id;
Result:
 category_id |    category     | product_count | total_stock | avg_price
-------------+-----------------+---------------+-------------+-----------
           1 | 1               |             8 |        1245 |     39.50
           2 | 2               |             5 |         650 |     45.99
           3 | 3               |             3 |         425 |     24.99
        NULL | ALL CATEGORIES  |            16 |        2320 |     38.27
(4 rows)
Explanation: GROUPING SETS ((category_id), ()) creates two groupings: by category_id and grand total. Grand total row has NULL for category_id (represents all categories). GROUPING(category_id) returns 1 when category_id NULL represents aggregation (not actual NULL value). CASE expression labels total row 'ALL CATEGORIES'. First 3 rows show per-category stats, last row shows overall totals (16 products, 2320 total stock, $38.27 average). More efficient than UNION: SELECT ... GROUP BY category_id UNION ALL SELECT ... (no GROUP BY).

🎯 Example 2: ROLLUP for Hierarchical Totals

-- Sales analysis with hierarchical rollup
WITH order_details AS (
    SELECT 
        c.country,
        c.city,
        o.status,
        o.total_amount
    FROM orders o
    INNER JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
    WHERE o.order_date >= '2026-01-01'
)
SELECT 
    COALESCE(country, 'ALL COUNTRIES') AS country,
    COALESCE(city, 'All Cities') AS city,
    COALESCE(status, 'All Statuses') AS status,
    COUNT(*) AS order_count,
    SUM(total_amount)::NUMERIC(10,2) AS total_sales,
    GROUPING(country) AS country_grouping,
    GROUPING(city) AS city_grouping,
    GROUPING(status) AS status_grouping
FROM order_details
GROUP BY ROLLUP (country, city, status)
ORDER BY country_grouping, country, city_grouping, city, status_grouping, status
LIMIT 12;
Result:
 country | city     | status     | order_count | total_sales | country_grouping | city_grouping | status_grouping
---------+----------+------------+-------------+-------------+------------------+---------------+-----------------
 USA     | Boston   | confirmed  |           2 |      445.50 |                0 |             0 |               0
 USA     | Boston   | shipped    |           1 |      289.99 |                0 |             0 |               0
 USA     | Boston   | All Statuses|          3 |      735.49 |                0 |             0 |               1
 USA     | Denver   | pending    |           1 |      289.99 |                0 |             0 |               0
 USA     | Denver   | processing |           1 |      128.50 |                0 |             0 |               0
 USA     | Denver   | All Statuses|          2 |      418.49 |                0 |             0 |               1
 USA     | Seattle  | shipped    |           3 |      647.82 |                0 |             0 |               0
 USA     | Seattle  | All Statuses|          3 |      647.82 |                0 |             0 |               1
 USA     | All Cities| All Statuses|         8 |     1801.80 |                0 |             1 |               1
 ALL COUNTRIES|All Cities|All Statuses|      8 |  1801.80 |                1 |             1 |               1
(10 rows)
Explanation: ROLLUP (country, city, status) creates hierarchy: (country,city,status) → (country,city) → (country) → (). Boston confirmed: 2 orders, $445.50. Boston subtotal (all statuses): 3 orders, $735.49. USA subtotal (all cities): 8 orders, $1801.80. Grand total: 8 orders, $1801.80. GROUPING columns show aggregation level: 0 = actual value, 1 = aggregated. COALESCE replaces NULL with labels. ROLLUP perfect for hierarchical reports: drill-down from country → city → status. ORDER BY uses GROUPING to organize hierarchy properly.

🎯 Example 3: CUBE for Multi-Dimensional Analysis

-- Multi-dimensional product analysis
SELECT 
    CASE WHEN GROUPING(category_id) = 1 THEN 'All Categories' ELSE category_id::TEXT END AS category,
    CASE 
        WHEN GROUPING(CASE WHEN price >= 50 THEN 'Premium' ELSE 'Standard' END) = 1 THEN 'All Price Tiers'
        WHEN price >= 50 THEN 'Premium'
        ELSE 'Standard'
    END AS price_tier,
    COUNT(*) AS product_count,
    SUM(stock_quantity) AS total_stock,
    AVG(price)::NUMERIC(10,2) AS avg_price
FROM products
WHERE stock_quantity > 0
GROUP BY CUBE (
    category_id,
    CASE WHEN price >= 50 THEN 'Premium' ELSE 'Standard' END
)
ORDER BY 
    GROUPING(category_id),
    category_id,
    GROUPING(CASE WHEN price >= 50 THEN 'Premium' ELSE 'Standard' END),
    price_tier;
Result:
 category | price_tier       | product_count | total_stock | avg_price
