Explanation: OFFSET n ROWS FETCH FIRST m ROWS ONLY is SQL standard syntax (SQL:2008). Equivalent to LIMIT m OFFSET n. FETCH FIRST/NEXT interchangeable. PostgreSQL supports both syntaxes - LIMIT/OFFSET more common, FETCH FIRST more portable. ROWS keyword optional: OFFSET 5 FETCH FIRST 10 ONLY also valid. Use SQL standard syntax for cross-database compatibility (Oracle, SQL Server, DB2). PostgreSQL-specific features (LIMIT/OFFSET) more concise but less portable.

🎯 Example 4: Keyset Pagination (Alternative to OFFSET)

-- Traditional pagination with OFFSET (inefficient for large offsets)
-- Page 1000: LIMIT 10 OFFSET 9990 processes 9990 rows

-- Keyset pagination: More efficient for large datasets
-- Page 1: Get first 10
SELECT product_id, product_name, price
FROM products
ORDER BY product_id
LIMIT 10;

-- Page 2: Use last ID from previous page (e.g., last_id = 2010)
SELECT product_id, product_name, price
FROM products
WHERE product_id > 2010  -- Start after last seen
ORDER BY product_id
LIMIT 10;

-- Page 3: Use last ID from page 2 (e.g., last_id = 2020)
SELECT product_id, product_name, price
FROM products
WHERE product_id > 2020
ORDER BY product_id
LIMIT 10;

-- For non-unique columns, combine with unique column
SELECT order_id, order_date, total_amount
FROM orders
WHERE (order_date, order_id) > ('2026-01-20', 3005)  -- Row constructor
ORDER BY order_date, order_id
LIMIT 10;
Result (keyset page 2):
 product_id |    product_name     | price
------------+---------------------+-------
       2011 | Wireless Keyboard   | 45.99
       2012 | Pen Set             | 24.99
       2013 | Sticky Notes        | 15.99
       2014 | Desk Chair          | 189.99
       2015 | File Cabinet        | 129.99
       2016 | Desk Lamp           | 34.99
       2017 | Mouse Pad           | 9.99
       2018 | Webcam              | 79.99
       2019 | Headset             | 59.99
       2020 | Cable Organizer     | 12.99
(10 rows)
Explanation: Keyset pagination uses WHERE instead of OFFSET - more efficient for large datasets. WHERE product_id > 2010 starts after last seen ID, avoids processing skipped rows. Each page uses last ID from previous page as starting point. Requires stable sort key (unique column like product_id or PRIMARY KEY). For non-unique columns, use row constructor: (order_date, order_id) > (last_date, last_id). Benefits: constant performance regardless of page number, no wasted processing of skipped rows. Drawbacks: can't jump to arbitrary page (no page numbers), only next/previous navigation. Use keyset for infinite scroll, real-time feeds, large datasets. Use OFFSET for small datasets or when need page numbers.

🎯 Example 5: LIMIT 0 and Dynamic Pagination

-- LIMIT 0: Get table structure without data
SELECT * FROM products LIMIT 0;
-- Returns column names and types, no rows (useful for metadata)

-- Dynamic pagination with variables (application layer)
-- Page size: 20, Page number: 3
SELECT 
    customer_id,
    first_name,
    last_name,
    email,
    city
FROM customers
ORDER BY customer_id
LIMIT 20 OFFSET (3 - 1) * 20;  -- OFFSET 40 (skip pages 1-2)

-- Total count for pagination metadata
SELECT 
    COUNT(*) AS total_records,
    CEIL(COUNT(*) / 20.0) AS total_pages,
    20 AS page_size,
    3 AS current_page
FROM customers;

-- Combined query: data + count (two queries)
-- Query 1: Get page data
-- Query 2: Get total count
-- Application combines both for complete pagination response
Result (metadata):
 total_records | total_pages | page_size | current_page
---------------+-------------+-----------+--------------
           150 |           8 |        20 |            3
(1 row)
Explanation: LIMIT 0 returns column structure without data - useful for metadata queries, schema inspection, preparing result sets. Dynamic pagination calculates OFFSET from page number: (page_num - 1) * page_size. Page 3 with size 20: OFFSET 40. Total count query provides pagination metadata (total pages, current position). Applications typically run two queries: one for page data (LIMIT/OFFSET), one for total count (display "Page 3 of 8"). Performance tip: cache total count (rarely changes), invalidate on INSERT/DELETE. For large tables, consider approximate count: SELECT reltuples FROM pg_class WHERE relname = 'customers'. Use prepared statements with LIMIT/OFFSET parameters to prevent SQL injection.

7.7 VALUES Lists

📖 Full Description

VALUES creates inline table data without referencing actual tables. Syntax: VALUES (row1_col1, row1_col2), (row2_col1, row2_col2). Produces table-like result with rows and columns. Can use in SELECT, INSERT, or standalone. Each row must have same column count. Column names default to column1, column2, etc. - override with table alias and column list.

Use Cases: Generate test data, create lookup tables on-the-fly, bulk INSERT with literal values, pivot/unpivot operations, generate series of values, compare against literal values in joins. VALUES more concise than multiple UNION ALL SELECT statements. PostgreSQL extension: can use VALUES in FROM clause like a table.

Best Practices: Use VALUES for small datasets only (performance degrades with large sets), name columns with table alias (VALUES (...) AS t(col1, col2)), ensure data types consistent across rows, combine with CTEs for complex queries, prefer generate_series() for numeric sequences, use INSERT with VALUES for bulk inserts, avoid VALUES for production data storage (use proper tables).

VALUES Syntax
-- Basic VALUES
VALUES (1, 'Alice'), (2, 'Bob'), (3, 'Charlie');

-- VALUES with column names
VALUES (1, 'Alice'), (2, 'Bob')
AS t(id, name);

-- VALUES in FROM clause
SELECT * FROM (VALUES (1, 'A'), (2, 'B'), (3, 'C')) AS t(num, letter);

-- VALUES in JOIN
SELECT p.*, v.discount
FROM products p
JOIN (VALUES (1, 0.10), (2, 0.15), (3, 0.20)) AS v(category_id, discount)
ON p.category_id = v.category_id;

-- VALUES in INSERT
INSERT INTO table_name (col1, col2)
VALUES (val1, val2), (val3, val4), (val5, val6);

-- VALUES in CTE
WITH constants AS (
    VALUES ('pending', 1), ('processing', 2), ('shipped', 3)
    AS t(status, priority)
)
SELECT * FROM constants;

🎯 Example 1: Basic VALUES for Inline Data

-- Generate inline lookup table
SELECT * 
FROM (VALUES 
    (1, 'Electronics', 'Gadgets and devices'),
    (2, 'Furniture', 'Office and home furniture'),
    (3, 'Stationery', 'Paper and writing supplies')
) AS categories(id, name, description);

-- VALUES with expressions
SELECT *
FROM (VALUES
    ('Product A', 100, 10, 100 * 10),
    ('Product B', 50, 20, 50 * 20),
    ('Product C', 75, 15, 75 * 15)
) AS sales(product, price, quantity, total);

-- VALUES for quick tests
SELECT *
FROM (VALUES 
    (1), (2), (3), (4), (5)
) AS numbers(n);
Result (categories):
 id |    name     |        description
----+-------------+----------------------------
  1 | Electronics | Gadgets and devices
  2 | Furniture   | Office and home furniture
  3 | Stationery  | Paper and writing supplies
(3 rows)
Result (sales):
  product  | price | quantity | total
-----------+-------+----------+-------
 Product A |   100 |       10 |  1000
 Product B |    50 |       20 |  1000
 Product C |    75 |       15 |  1125
(3 rows)
Explanation: VALUES creates inline table without referencing database tables. First example creates 3-row lookup table with categories - useful for quick reference or testing. AS categories(id, name, description) names table and columns. Second example includes calculated values (100 * 10 = 1000). Third example generates simple number sequence. VALUES useful for quick tests, prototyping queries, generating small reference data. Alternative to creating temporary tables or multiple UNION ALL SELECT statements.

🎯 Example 2: VALUES in JOIN Operations

-- Apply different discounts by category using VALUES
SELECT 
    p.product_id,
    p.product_name,
    p.category_id,
    p.price,
    v.discount_rate,
    (p.price * (1 - v.discount_rate))::NUMERIC(10,2) AS discounted_price
FROM products p
INNER JOIN (VALUES 
    (1, 0.15),  -- Electronics: 15% off
    (2, 0.10),  -- Furniture: 10% off
    (3, 0.20)   -- Stationery: 20% off
) AS v(category_id, discount_rate) ON p.category_id = v.category_id
WHERE p.stock_quantity > 0
ORDER BY p.category_id, p.product_id
LIMIT 8;
Result:
 product_id |    product_name     | category_id | price | discount_rate | discounted_price
------------+---------------------+-------------+-------+---------------+------------------
       2001 | Wireless Mouse      |           1 | 32.99 |          0.15 |            28.04
       2002 | USB-C Cable         |           1 | 14.29 |          0.15 |            12.15
       2006 | Mechanical Keyboard |           1 | 79.99 |          0.15 |            67.99
       2003 | Laptop Stand        |           2 | 49.99 |          0.10 |            44.99
       2007 | Monitor Stand       |           2 | 39.99 |          0.10 |            35.99
       2008 | Desk Organizer      |           2 | 24.99 |          0.10 |            22.49
       2005 | Notebook Set        |           3 | 34.99 |          0.20 |            27.99
       2013 | Sticky Notes        |           3 | 15.99 |          0.20 |            12.79
(8 rows)
Explanation: VALUES used as join table - provides discount rates by category without creating permanent table. JOIN matches p.category_id with v.category_id from VALUES. Each category gets different discount: Electronics 15%, Furniture 10%, Stationery 20%. Discounted price calculated using discount_rate from VALUES. Pattern useful for ad-hoc calculations, temporary mappings, quick analysis. Alternative: create temporary table or use CASE expression (VALUES cleaner for multiple conditions). VALUES-based JOINs excellent for prototyping before creating permanent lookup tables.

🎯 Example 3: VALUES for Bulk INSERT

-- Insert multiple rows with single statement
INSERT INTO products (product_name, category_id, price, stock_quantity)
VALUES 
    ('Wireless Charger', 1, 29.99, 100),
    ('Standing Desk', 2, 399.99, 20),
    ('Highlighter Set', 3, 8.99, 250),
    ('USB Drive 64GB', 1, 19.99, 150),
    ('Office Chair Mat', 2, 45.99, 75)
RETURNING product_id, product_name, price;

-- INSERT with expressions in VALUES
INSERT INTO orders (customer_id, order_date, status, total_amount)
VALUES
    (1001, CURRENT_DATE, 'pending', 0),
    (1003, CURRENT_DATE - 1, 'processing', 0),
    (1005, CURRENT_DATE - 2, 'shipped', 0)
RETURNING order_id, customer_id, order_date, status;
Result (RETURNING):
 product_id |    product_name    | price
------------+--------------------+--------
       2021 | Wireless Charger   |  29.99
       2022 | Standing Desk      | 399.99
       2023 | Highlighter Set    |   8.99
       2024 | USB Drive 64GB     |  19.99
       2025 | Office Chair Mat   |  45.99
(5 rows)
Explanation: INSERT with multiple VALUES rows performs bulk insert - more efficient than separate INSERT statements. Single transaction, one server round-trip, reduced overhead. RETURNING shows inserted rows with generated product_id values. Second INSERT uses expressions in VALUES: CURRENT_DATE, CURRENT_DATE - 1 (yesterday), CURRENT_DATE - 2 (two days ago). VALUES in INSERT can include literals, expressions, function calls. For very large bulk inserts (1000+ rows), consider COPY command (faster). VALUES INSERT good for moderate bulk inserts (10-100 rows), application-driven inserts, test data generation.

🎯 Example 4: VALUES in CTE and Unpivot Operations

-- VALUES in CTE for readable queries
WITH status_priority AS (
    SELECT * FROM (VALUES
        ('pending', 1, 'red'),
        ('processing', 2, 'yellow'),
        ('shipped', 3, 'green'),
        ('delivered', 4, 'blue'),
        ('cancelled', 5, 'gray')
    ) AS t(status, priority, color)
)
SELECT 
    o.order_id,
    o.status,
    sp.priority,
    sp.color AS status_color
FROM orders o
INNER JOIN status_priority sp ON o.status = sp.status
ORDER BY sp.priority, o.order_date DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- Unpivot using VALUES (convert columns to rows)
WITH monthly_sales AS (
    SELECT 
        2025 AS year,
        100 AS jan, 150 AS feb, 200 AS mar,
        180 AS apr, 220 AS may, 250 AS jun
)
SELECT year, month, sales
FROM monthly_sales,
LATERAL (VALUES
    ('January', jan),
    ('February', feb),
    ('March', mar),
    ('April', apr),
    ('May', may),
    ('June', jun)
) AS t(month, sales);
Result (status priority):
 order_id | status     | priority | status_color
----------+------------+----------+--------------
     3008 | pending    |        1 | red
     3012 | pending    |        1 | red
     3009 | processing |        2 | yellow
     3013 | processing |        2 | yellow
     3001 | shipped    |        3 | green
     3004 | shipped    |        3 | green
(6 rows)
Result (unpivot):
 year |  month   | sales
------+----------+-------
 2025 | January  |   100
 2025 | February |   150
 2025 | March    |   200
 2025 | April    |   180
 2025 | May      |   220
 2025 | June     |   250
(6 rows)
Explanation: VALUES in CTE creates named reference data usable throughout query. status_priority CTE defines status order, priority levels, display colors. Main query joins orders with CTE for enriched results. Cleaner than multiple CASE statements. Second example uses VALUES with LATERAL for unpivot operation - converts column-based data (jan, feb, mar) to row-based (month, sales). LATERAL enables referencing monthly_sales columns in VALUES. Unpivot transforms wide format to long format - useful for reporting, charting, normalization. VALUES-based unpivot more concise than UNION ALL of multiple SELECTs.

🎯 Example 5: VALUES vs Alternatives

-- Approach 1: VALUES (clean and concise)
SELECT * FROM (VALUES (1), (2), (3), (4), (5)) AS t(n);

-- Approach 2: UNION ALL (verbose)
SELECT 1 AS n UNION ALL
SELECT 2 UNION ALL
SELECT 3 UNION ALL
SELECT 4 UNION ALL
SELECT 5;

-- Approach 3: generate_series() (best for sequences)
SELECT generate_series(1, 5) AS n;

-- When to use each:
-- VALUES: Small literal datasets, mixed types, quick tests
SELECT * FROM (VALUES
    ('Alice', 25, 'USA'),
    ('Bob', 30, 'Canada'),
    ('Charlie', 28, 'UK')
) AS people(name, age, country);

-- generate_series: Number/date sequences
SELECT generate_series(1, 100) AS id;
SELECT generate_series('2026-01-01'::date, '2026-01-31'::date, '1 day') AS day;

-- Temporary table: Large datasets, reused multiple times
CREATE TEMP TABLE temp_data (id INT, value TEXT);
INSERT INTO temp_data VALUES (1, 'A'), (2, 'B');
Result (VALUES):
 n
---
 1
 2
 3
 4
 5
(5 rows)
Explanation: VALUES vs alternatives: VALUES most concise for small literal datasets (1-20 rows), mixed data types, ad-hoc reference data. UNION ALL verbose but sometimes required for compatibility. generate_series() better for numeric/date sequences - more efficient, supports ranges, intervals. Temporary tables better for large datasets (100+ rows), data reused multiple times, complex manipulation. Choose based on use case: VALUES for quick inline data, generate_series() for sequences, temp tables for substantial datasets. VALUES particularly useful in CTEs, JOINs, subqueries where creating permanent tables overkill. Limitation: VALUES performance degrades with large datasets (use temp tables or COPY instead).

7.8 WITH Queries (Common Table Expressions)

📖 Full Description

WITH Queries (Common Table Expressions or CTEs) define temporary named result sets usable within SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE statement. Syntax: WITH cte_name AS (query) SELECT ... FROM cte_name. CTEs improve readability - break complex queries into logical steps, name intermediate results. Similar to temporary views but scoped to single query. Multiple CTEs separated by commas: WITH cte1 AS (...), cte2 AS (...). Later CTEs can reference earlier CTEs.

Advantages Over Subqueries: Named results (self-documenting), reusable (reference CTE multiple times), readable (avoid nested subqueries), recursive capability (hierarchies, graphs), modular (build complex queries incrementally). CTEs not stored - defined and used within single statement. Optimizer may inline or materialize CTEs based on cost estimation.

CTE Types: Non-recursive CTEs (standard WITH), Recursive CTEs (WITH RECURSIVE for hierarchies), Data-modifying CTEs (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE with RETURNING). Best practices: use descriptive CTE names, one CTE per logical step, prefer CTEs over nested subqueries for readability, use MATERIALIZED hint when optimizer gets it wrong, beware recursive CTE infinite loops (set recursion limit).

WITH (CTE) Syntax
-- Basic CTE
WITH cte_name AS (
    SELECT columns FROM table WHERE conditions
)
SELECT * FROM cte_name;

-- Multiple CTEs
WITH 
    cte1 AS (SELECT ...),
    cte2 AS (SELECT ... FROM cte1),
    cte3 AS (SELECT ... FROM cte1 JOIN cte2 ...)
SELECT * FROM cte3;

-- Recursive CTE
WITH RECURSIVE cte_name AS (
    -- Base case (anchor member)
    SELECT ... WHERE ...
    UNION ALL
    -- Recursive case (recursive member)
    SELECT ... FROM cte_name WHERE ...
)
SELECT * FROM cte_name;

-- CTE with data modification
WITH deleted_rows AS (
    DELETE FROM table WHERE condition
    RETURNING *
)
SELECT * FROM deleted_rows;

-- Materialization hints (PostgreSQL 12+)
WITH cte AS MATERIALIZED (...)      -- Force materialization
WITH cte AS NOT MATERIALIZED (...)  -- Force inline

🎯 Example - CTE Structure Overview

-- Complex query using CTEs instead of nested subqueries
WITH 
    -- Step 1: Calculate customer statistics
    customer_stats AS (
        SELECT 
            customer_id,
            COUNT(order_id) AS order_count,
            SUM(total_amount) AS total_spent,
            MAX(order_date) AS last_order_date
        FROM orders
        GROUP BY customer_id
    ),
    -- Step 2: Classify customers by spending
    customer_segments AS (
        SELECT 
            customer_id,
            order_count,
            total_spent,
            CASE 
                WHEN total_spent >= 1000 THEN 'VIP'
                WHEN total_spent >= 500 THEN 'Premium'
                WHEN total_spent >= 100 THEN 'Standard'
                ELSE 'New'
            END AS segment
        FROM customer_stats
    )
-- Step 3: Combine with customer details
SELECT 
    c.customer_id,
    c.first_name || ' ' || c.last_name AS customer_name,
    c.email,
    cs.order_count,
    cs.total_spent,
    cs.segment
FROM customers c
INNER JOIN customer_segments cs ON c.customer_id = cs.customer_id
WHERE cs.segment IN ('VIP', 'Premium')
ORDER BY cs.total_spent DESC
LIMIT 10;
Result:
 customer_id | customer_name | email                | order_count | total_spent | segment
-------------+---------------+----------------------+-------------+-------------+---------
        1003 | Charlie Davis | charlie.davis@em.com |           4 |     1538.50 | VIP
        1002 | Bob Smith     | bob.smith@email.com  |           3 |     1248.75 | VIP
        1005 | Eve Wilson    | eve.wilson@email.com |           3 |      987.90 | Premium
        1001 | Alice Johnson | alice.j@email.com    |           2 |      825.50 | Premium
(4 rows)
Explanation: WITH defines two CTEs: customer_stats calculates order metrics per customer, customer_segments classifies customers into segments based on spending. Main query joins customers with segmented data, filters VIP/Premium only. CTEs break complex query into logical steps: aggregate → classify → join. More readable than nested subqueries. Each CTE has descriptive name. Later CTEs reference earlier ones (customer_segments uses customer_stats). Alternative without CTEs would use nested SELECT statements or temporary tables. CTEs scoped to query - don't persist after execution.

7.8.1 SELECT in WITH

SELECT in WITH is most common CTE usage - define named SELECT result sets. CTE can contain any valid SELECT query: simple queries, complex joins, aggregations, subqueries. Multiple CTEs define sequence of transformations. CTEs referenced like tables in main query - can join CTEs, union CTEs, nest CTEs. CTEs make complex queries maintainable.

🎯 Example 1: Simple CTE Replacing Subquery
-- Without CTE (subquery in FROM)
SELECT p.*, sub.avg_price
FROM products p
CROSS JOIN (
    SELECT AVG(price) AS avg_price FROM products
) sub
WHERE p.price > sub.avg_price;

-- With CTE (cleaner and more readable)
WITH avg_price_cte AS (
    SELECT AVG(price) AS avg_price 
    FROM products
)
SELECT 
    p.product_id,
    p.product_name,
    p.price,
    a.avg_price,
    (p.price - a.avg_price)::NUMERIC(10,2) AS price_diff
FROM products p, avg_price_cte a
WHERE p.price > a.avg_price
ORDER BY price_diff DESC
LIMIT 8;
Result:
 product_id |    product_name     | price | avg_price | price_diff
------------+---------------------+-------+-----------+------------
       2006 | Mechanical Keyboard | 79.99 |     44.18 |      35.81
       2010 | Laptop Bag          | 69.99 |     44.18 |      25.81
       2003 | Laptop Stand        | 49.99 |     44.18 |       5.81
       2001 | Wireless Mouse      | 32.99 |     44.18 |     -11.19
(4 rows)
Explanation: CTE replaces subquery in FROM clause. avg_price_cte calculates average price once, reusable in main query. Named CTE more readable than inline subquery. Main query crosses joins products with CTE (single-row CTE acts as constant). Calculates price difference from average. WITH...AS names intermediate result. CTEs particularly valuable when same subquery referenced multiple times - CTE evaluated once, subquery evaluated each time.
🎯 Example 2: Multiple CTEs in Sequence
-- Chain of CTEs - each builds on previous
WITH 
    -- CTE 1: Recent orders (last 30 days)
    recent_orders AS (
        SELECT order_id, customer_id, order_date, total_amount
        FROM orders
        WHERE order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'
    ),
    -- CTE 2: Customer totals from recent orders
    customer_totals AS (
        SELECT 
            customer_id,
            COUNT(*) AS recent_order_count,
            SUM(total_amount) AS recent_total
        FROM recent_orders
        GROUP BY customer_id
    ),
    -- CTE 3: Enrich with customer details
    customer_summary AS (
        SELECT 
            c.customer_id,
            c.first_name || ' ' || c.last_name AS customer_name,
            c.email,
            ct.recent_order_count,
            ct.recent_total
        FROM customers c
        INNER JOIN customer_totals ct ON c.customer_id = ct.customer_id
    )
-- Main query: Filter and display
SELECT 
    customer_name,
    email,
    recent_order_count,
    recent_total,
    (recent_total / recent_order_count)::NUMERIC(10,2) AS avg_order_value
FROM customer_summary
WHERE recent_order_count >= 2
ORDER BY recent_total DESC;
Result:
 customer_name | email                | recent_order_count | recent_total | avg_order_value
---------------+----------------------+--------------------+--------------+-----------------
 Charlie Davis | charlie.davis@em.com |                  3 |       825.50 |          275.17
 Bob Smith     | bob.smith@email.com  |                  2 |       698.90 |          349.45
(2 rows)
Explanation: Three CTEs chained: recent_orders filters last 30 days, customer_totals aggregates by customer, customer_summary joins with customer details. Each CTE builds on previous. Comma separates multiple CTEs. Later CTEs reference earlier (customer_totals uses recent_orders, customer_summary uses customer_totals). Main query uses final CTE only. Pipeline of transformations: filter → aggregate → enrich → display. Without CTEs, would require deeply nested subqueries. Multiple CTEs enable step-by-step logic, easy debugging (test each CTE independently).
🎯 Example 3: Reusing CTE Multiple Times
-- CTE referenced multiple times in same query
WITH high_value_orders AS (
    SELECT order_id, customer_id, order_date, total_amount
    FROM orders
    WHERE total_amount >= 200
)
SELECT 
    -- Summary statistics
    (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM high_value_orders) AS total_high_value_orders,
    (SELECT SUM(total_amount) FROM high_value_orders) AS total_revenue,
    (SELECT AVG(total_amount)::NUMERIC(10,2) FROM high_value_orders) AS avg_order_value,
    
    -- Top 3 customers by high-value order count
    (SELECT customer_id 
     FROM high_value_orders 
     GROUP BY customer_id 
     ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC 
     LIMIT 1) AS top_customer_id,
    
    -- Most recent high-value order date
    (SELECT MAX(order_date) FROM high_value_orders) AS latest_order_date;
Result:
 total_high_value_orders | total_revenue | avg_order_value | top_customer_id | latest_order_date
-------------------------+---------------+-----------------+-----------------+-------------------
                       8 |       2538.90 |          317.36 |            1003 | 2026-01-30
(1 row)
Explanation: high_value_orders CTE referenced 5 times in main query. CTE evaluated once, results cached for all references. Without CTE, would repeat same subquery 5 times (inefficient, harder to maintain). CTEs enable result reuse - change filter once (WHERE total_amount >= 200), affects all usages. Query calculates statistics from same filtered dataset: count, sum, average, top customer, latest date. Subquery approach would evaluate WHERE condition 5 separate times. CTE approach: single evaluation, multiple references.
🎯 Example 4: CTE with Complex JOIN
-- CTE simplifies complex multi-table JOIN
WITH order_details AS (
    -- Combine order, customer, and product information
    SELECT 
        o.order_id,
        o.order_date,
        c.customer_id,
        c.first_name || ' ' || c.last_name AS customer_name,
        p.product_id,
        p.product_name,
        oi.quantity,
        oi.unit_price,
        (oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS line_total
    FROM orders o
    INNER JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
    INNER JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
    INNER JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
    WHERE o.order_date >= '2026-01-01'
)
SELECT 
    product_name,
    SUM(quantity) AS total_quantity_sold,
    COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) AS order_count,
    SUM(line_total)::NUMERIC(10,2) AS total_revenue,
    (SUM(line_total) / SUM(quantity))::NUMERIC(10,2) AS avg_unit_price
FROM order_details
GROUP BY product_name
HAVING SUM(quantity) >= 3
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC
LIMIT 8;
Result:
    product_name     | total_quantity_sold | order_count | total_revenue | avg_unit_price
---------------------+---------------------+-------------+---------------+----------------
 Wireless Mouse      |                   8 |           4 |        263.92 |          32.99
 USB-C Cable         |                   5 |           3 |         71.45 |          14.29
 Mechanical Keyboard |                   3 |           2 |        239.97 |          79.99
 Notebook Set        |                   4 |           2 |        139.96 |          34.99
(4 rows)
Explanation: order_details CTE performs 4-table JOIN (orders, customers, order_items, products), creates denormalized result. Main query aggregates CTE results by product. CTE separates JOIN logic from aggregation logic - easier to understand. Alternative without CTE: nested subquery or combine JOIN+GROUP BY in single complex query. CTE approach: JOIN in CTE, aggregate in main query (modular). Can test CTE independently (SELECT * FROM order_details), verify JOIN correctness before adding aggregation.
🎯 Example 5: CTE with Window Functions
-- CTE with window functions for ranking and filtering
WITH ranked_products AS (
    SELECT 
        p.product_id,
        p.product_name,
        p.category_id,
        p.price,
        p.stock_quantity,
        -- Rank products by price within each category
        RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY category_id ORDER BY price DESC) AS price_rank,
        -- Calculate percentage of category total stock
        (p.stock_quantity * 100.0 / 
         SUM(stock_quantity) OVER (PARTITION BY category_id))::NUMERIC(5,2) AS stock_pct
    FROM products p
    WHERE p.stock_quantity > 0
)
SELECT 
    product_id,
    product_name,
    price,
    price_rank,
    stock_quantity,
    stock_pct
FROM ranked_products
WHERE price_rank <= 3  -- Top 3 most expensive in each category
ORDER BY category_id, price_rank;
Result:
 product_id |    product_name     | price | price_rank | stock_quantity | stock_pct
------------+---------------------+-------+------------+----------------+-----------
       2006 | Mechanical Keyboard | 79.99 |          1 |             40 |     21.62
       2001 | Wireless Mouse      | 32.99 |          2 |            120 |     64.86
       2002 | USB-C Cable         | 14.29 |          3 |             25 |     13.51
       2003 | Laptop Stand        | 49.99 |          1 |             50 |     33.33
       2007 | Monitor Stand       | 39.99 |          2 |             75 |     50.00
       2008 | Desk Organizer      | 24.99 |          3 |             25 |     16.67
(6 rows)
Explanation: ranked_products CTE uses window functions RANK() and SUM() OVER to calculate per-category rankings and stock percentages. PARTITION BY category_id creates separate ranking per category. Main query filters price_rank <= 3 (top 3 per category). Cannot filter on window function in WHERE (window functions evaluated after WHERE). CTE solves this: calculate rankings in CTE, filter in main query. Pattern: CTE with window functions → main query filters/aggregates windowed results. Alternative: nested subquery, but CTE more readable.

7.8.2 Recursive Queries

Recursive CTEs enable queries on hierarchical or graph-structured data. Syntax: WITH RECURSIVE containing UNION ALL joining base case (anchor) and recursive case. Base case provides starting rows. Recursive case references CTE itself, processes one level per iteration. Recursion continues until recursive case returns no rows. Common uses: organizational hierarchies (employee-manager), category trees, bill of materials, graph traversal, path finding.

Pattern: WITH RECURSIVE cte AS (base_query UNION ALL recursive_query). base_query runs once (starting point). recursive_query references cte, processes next level. Each iteration uses previous iteration's results. Termination: recursive query returns empty set. Safety: PostgreSQL has max_stack_depth limit, default recursion depth ~100. Use WHERE conditions to prevent infinite loops.

Recursive CTE Syntax
WITH RECURSIVE cte_name AS (
    -- Base case (anchor member): Starting point
    SELECT id, parent_id, name, 1 AS level
    FROM table
    WHERE parent_id IS NULL  -- Root nodes
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Recursive case (recursive member): References cte_name
    SELECT t.id, t.parent_id, t.name, cte.level + 1
    FROM table t
    INNER JOIN cte_name cte ON t.parent_id = cte.id
    WHERE cte.level < 10  -- Prevent infinite recursion
)
SELECT * FROM cte_name;
🎯 Example 1: Employee Hierarchy (Manager-Subordinate)
-- Traverse employee hierarchy from CEO downward
WITH RECURSIVE employee_hierarchy AS (
    -- Base case: CEO (no manager)
    SELECT 
        employee_id,
        first_name,
        last_name,
        manager_id,
        job_title,
        1 AS level,
        first_name::TEXT AS path,
        ARRAY[employee_id] AS path_array
    FROM employees
    WHERE manager_id IS NULL
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Recursive case: Employees reporting to previous level
    SELECT 
        e.employee_id,
        e.first_name,
        e.last_name,
        e.manager_id,
        e.job_title,
        eh.level + 1,
        eh.path || ' -> ' || e.first_name,
        eh.path_array || e.employee_id
    FROM employees e
    INNER JOIN employee_hierarchy eh ON e.manager_id = eh.employee_id
    WHERE eh.level < 10  -- Prevent infinite loops
)
SELECT 
    REPEAT('  ', level - 1) || first_name || ' ' || last_name AS employee,
    job_title,
    level,
    path
FROM employee_hierarchy
ORDER BY path_array;
Result:
        employee         |    job_title     | level |           path
-------------------------+------------------+-------+---------------------------
 Michael Scott           | Regional Manager |     1 | Michael
   Jim Halpert           | Sales Manager    |     2 | Michael -> Jim
     Dwight Schrute      | Salesman         |     3 | Michael -> Jim -> Dwight
     Stanley Hudson      | Salesman         |     3 | Michael -> Jim -> Stanley
   Pam Beesly            | Receptionist     |     2 | Michael -> Pam
   Angela Martin         | Accountant       |     2 | Michael -> Angela
     Oscar Martinez      | Accountant       |     3 | Michael -> Angela -> Oscar
(7 rows)
Explanation: Recursive CTE traverses employee hierarchy. Base case selects CEO (manager_id IS NULL), level = 1. Recursive case joins employees to employee_hierarchy via manager_id, increments level. Each iteration finds next level: iteration 1 (CEO) → iteration 2 (direct reports) → iteration 3 (reports of reports). UNION ALL combines base + recursive results. path tracks organizational path (Michael -> Jim -> Dwight). path_array enables correct sorting by hierarchy. Recursion stops when no more employees found (leaf nodes). REPEAT(' ', level - 1) indents output by level. WHERE level < 10 prevents infinite loops (circular references).
🎯 Example 2: Category Tree Traversal
-- Navigate category tree from root to leaves
WITH RECURSIVE category_tree AS (
    -- Base case: Top-level categories (no parent)
    SELECT 
        category_id,
        category_name,
        parent_category_id,
        1 AS depth,
        category_name::TEXT AS full_path,
        ARRAY[category_id] AS path_ids
    FROM categories
    WHERE parent_category_id IS NULL
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Recursive case: Child categories
    SELECT 
        c.category_id,
        c.category_name,
        c.parent_category_id,
        ct.depth + 1,
        ct.full_path || ' > ' || c.category_name,
        ct.path_ids || c.category_id
    FROM categories c
    INNER JOIN category_tree ct ON c.parent_category_id = ct.category_id
    WHERE ct.depth < 5  -- Limit tree depth
)
SELECT 
    category_id,
    REPEAT('  ', depth - 1) || '└─ ' || category_name AS category_display,
    depth,
    full_path,
    array_length(path_ids, 1) AS path_length
FROM category_tree
ORDER BY path_ids;
Result:
 category_id |    category_display    | depth |         full_path          | path_length
-------------+------------------------+-------+----------------------------+-------------
           1 | └─ Electronics         |     1 | Electronics                |           1
           4 |   └─ Computers         |     2 | Electronics > Computers    |           2
           7 |     └─ Laptops         |     3 | Electronics > Computers > Laptops |     3
           8 |     └─ Desktops        |     3 | Electronics > Computers > Desktops|    3
           5 |   └─ Accessories       |     2 | Electronics > Accessories  |           2
           2 | └─ Furniture           |     1 | Furniture                  |           1
           6 |   └─ Office Chairs     |     2 | Furniture > Office Chairs  |           2
           3 | └─ Stationery          |     1 | Stationery                 |           1
(8 rows)
Explanation: Recursive CTE builds category tree. Base case selects root categories (parent_category_id IS NULL). Recursive case finds child categories, joins to parent via parent_category_id = category_id. depth tracks tree depth, full_path shows hierarchical path (Electronics > Computers > Laptops). path_ids array stores category ID path for sorting. Each recursion processes one tree level. Tree structure: Electronics (depth 1) → Computers (depth 2) → Laptops (depth 3). REPEAT creates visual indentation with └─ symbols. Use case: display category menus, find all subcategories, calculate category aggregates.
🎯 Example 3: Graph Traversal with Cycle Detection
-- Find all connected locations starting from location 1
-- Detect cycles to prevent infinite loops
WITH RECURSIVE connected_locations AS (
    -- Base case: Starting location
    SELECT 
        1 AS location_id,
        'Warehouse'::TEXT AS location_name,
        ARRAY[1] AS visited,
        0 AS distance,
        'Warehouse'::TEXT AS path
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Recursive case: Connected locations
    SELECT 
        r.to_location_id,
        l.location_name,
        cl.visited || r.to_location_id,
        cl.distance + r.distance_km,
        cl.path || ' -> ' || l.location_name
    FROM connected_locations cl
    INNER JOIN routes r ON cl.location_id = r.from_location_id
    INNER JOIN locations l ON r.to_location_id = l.location_id
    WHERE NOT (r.to_location_id = ANY(cl.visited))  -- Cycle detection
      AND cl.distance < 1000  -- Distance limit
)
SELECT 
    location_id,
    location_name,
    distance,
    path,
    array_length(visited, 1) AS hops
FROM connected_locations
ORDER BY distance, location_id;
Result:
 location_id | location_name | distance |              path              | hops
-------------+---------------+----------+--------------------------------+------
           1 | Warehouse     |        0 | Warehouse                      |    1
           2 | Store A       |       10 | Warehouse -> Store A           |    2
           3 | Store B       |       15 | Warehouse -> Store B           |    2
           4 | Distribution  |       25 | Warehouse -> Store A -> Distribution | 3
           5 | Store C       |       30 | Warehouse -> Store B -> Store C|    3
(5 rows)
Explanation: Recursive CTE traverses graph of connected locations. visited array tracks visited nodes. NOT (to_location_id = ANY(visited)) prevents revisiting nodes (cycle detection). Without cycle detection, circular routes cause infinite recursion. Base case starts at Warehouse (location 1). Recursive case follows routes table edges, accumulates distance. path shows route taken. distance < 1000 limits total distance (alternate termination condition). Use cases: find reachable nodes, shortest paths (add ORDER BY + LIMIT 1 per destination), network analysis, dependency resolution.
🎯 Example 4: Recursive Aggregation (Bill of Materials)
-- Calculate total cost of product including all components
WITH RECURSIVE bom AS (
    -- Base case: Top-level product
    SELECT 
        product_id,
        product_name,
        component_id,
        1 AS quantity,
        price,
        1 AS level,
        product_name::TEXT AS assembly_path
    FROM products
    WHERE product_id = 2050  -- Specific product
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Recursive case: Components of components
    SELECT 
        p.product_id,
        p.product_name,
        pc.component_id,
        bom.quantity * pc.quantity,  -- Multiply quantities down tree
        p.price,
        bom.level + 1,
        bom.assembly_path || ' > ' || p.product_name
    FROM bom
    INNER JOIN product_components pc ON bom.product_id = pc.product_id
    INNER JOIN products p ON pc.component_id = p.product_id
    WHERE bom.level < 10
)
SELECT 
    product_id,
    product_name,
    quantity,
    price,
    (quantity * price)::NUMERIC(10,2) AS total_cost,
    level,
    assembly_path
FROM bom
ORDER BY level, product_id;
Result:
 product_id |   product_name    | quantity | price | total_cost | level |         assembly_path
------------+-------------------+----------+-------+------------+-------+-------------------------------
       2050 | Complete Desktop  |        1 | 899.99|     899.99 |     1 | Complete Desktop
       2051 | Computer Case     |        1 |  49.99|      49.99 |     2 | Complete Desktop > Computer Case
       2052 | Motherboard       |        1 | 199.99|     199.99 |     2 | Complete Desktop > Motherboard
       2053 | RAM 16GB          |        2 |  79.99|     159.98 |     2 | Complete Desktop > RAM 16GB
       2054 | Power Supply      |        1 |  69.99|      69.99 |     2 | Complete Desktop > Power Supply
       2055 | CPU               |        1 | 299.99|     299.99 |     3 | Complete Desktop > Motherboard > CPU
(6 rows)

Total Cost: SUM(total_cost) = 1679.93
Explanation: Recursive CTE calculates bill of materials (BOM) - all components needed to build product. Base case selects target product (2050). Recursive case joins product_components to find subcomponents. quantity multiplies down tree: 2 RAM modules × 1 parent = 2 total. Each level shows component at that depth: level 1 (final product), level 2 (direct components), level 3 (subcomponents). total_cost = quantity × price per component. SUM(total_cost) gives total manufacturing cost. Use cases: manufacturing cost calculation, inventory requirements, supply chain planning, product explosion/implosion.
🎯 Example 5: Finding All Paths with Depth Limit
-- Find all possible paths between categories with depth limit
WITH RECURSIVE category_paths AS (
    -- Base case: Start from each root category
    SELECT 
        category_id AS start_category,
        category_id AS end_category,
        category_name,
        1 AS depth,
        ARRAY[category_id] AS path,
        category_name::TEXT AS path_names
    FROM categories
    WHERE parent_category_id IS NULL
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Recursive case: Extend paths
    SELECT 
        cp.start_category,
        c.category_id,
        c.category_name,
        cp.depth + 1,
        cp.path || c.category_id,
        cp.path_names || ' → ' || c.category_name
    FROM category_paths cp
    INNER JOIN categories c ON cp.end_category = c.parent_category_id
    WHERE cp.depth < 4  -- Maximum path depth
      AND NOT (c.category_id = ANY(cp.path))  -- Prevent cycles
)
SELECT 
    start_category,
    end_category,
    depth,
    path_names,
    cardinality(path) AS path_length
FROM category_paths
WHERE depth >= 2  -- Only show paths with at least 2 nodes
ORDER BY start_category, depth, end_category
LIMIT 12;
Result:
 start_category | end_category | depth |            path_names             | path_length
----------------+--------------+-------+-----------------------------------+-------------
              1 |            4 |     2 | Electronics → Computers           |           2
              1 |            5 |     2 | Electronics → Accessories         |           2
              1 |            7 |     3 | Electronics → Computers → Laptops |           3
              1 |            8 |     3 | Electronics → Computers → Desktops|           3
              2 |            6 |     2 | Furniture → Office Chairs         |           2
(5 rows)
Explanation: Recursive CTE finds all paths through category tree from each root. Base case starts at root categories. Recursive case extends each path by one category. path array tracks category IDs visited (prevents cycles with NOT ANY(path)). path_names shows human-readable path. depth limits exploration depth (depth < 4 explores max 4 levels). Each recursion tries all possible extensions of current paths. Results show all possible navigation paths through category hierarchy. Use cases: find all paths between nodes, explore graph structure, generate menu hierarchies, analyze connectivity. cardinality(path) counts array elements (path length).

7.8.3 Common Table Expression Materialization

CTE Materialization controls how PostgreSQL executes CTEs. Two strategies: MATERIALIZED (CTE evaluated once, results stored temporarily, reused) vs NOT MATERIALIZED (CTE inlined into main query, evaluated per reference). PostgreSQL 12+ allows explicit control via MATERIALIZED/NOT MATERIALIZED hints. Pre-12: CTEs always materialized. Post-12: optimizer decides based on cost estimation.

When to Materialize: CTE referenced multiple times (materialize once, reuse), expensive CTE (avoid reevaluation), CTE with volatile functions (consistent results), optimization fence needed (prevent unwanted optimization). When NOT to Materialize: CTE referenced once, CTE has selective WHERE (inline allows index usage), small CTE (materialization overhead), want parallel query execution.

Default Behavior (PostgreSQL 12+): Simple CTEs referenced once: NOT MATERIALIZED (inlined). CTEs referenced multiple times: MATERIALIZED if beneficial. Recursive CTEs: always materialized. Use EXPLAIN to see optimizer decision. Override with MATERIALIZED/NOT MATERIALIZED keywords when optimizer wrong.

Materialization Syntax
-- Let optimizer decide (default)
WITH cte AS (
    SELECT ...
)
SELECT * FROM cte;

-- Force materialization (PostgreSQL 12+)
WITH cte AS MATERIALIZED (
    SELECT ...
)
SELECT * FROM cte;

-- Prevent materialization (force inline, PostgreSQL 12+)
WITH cte AS NOT MATERIALIZED (
    SELECT ...
)
SELECT * FROM cte;

-- Check materialization with EXPLAIN
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, VERBOSE, BUFFERS)
WITH cte AS (SELECT * FROM large_table WHERE ...)
SELECT * FROM cte;
🎯 Example 1: Default Behavior - Optimizer Decides
-- Simple CTE referenced once (likely NOT MATERIALIZED)
WITH recent_orders AS (
    SELECT order_id, customer_id, total_amount
    FROM orders
    WHERE order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '7 days'
)
SELECT c.customer_id, c.first_name, ro.order_id, ro.total_amount
FROM customers c
INNER JOIN recent_orders ro ON c.customer_id = ro.customer_id
WHERE c.city = 'New York';

-- Check execution plan:
EXPLAIN 
WITH recent_orders AS (
    SELECT order_id, customer_id, total_amount
    FROM orders
    WHERE order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '7 days'
)
SELECT c.customer_id, c.first_name, ro.order_id, ro.total_amount
FROM customers c
INNER JOIN recent_orders ro ON c.customer_id = ro.customer_id
WHERE c.city = 'New York';
EXPLAIN Result:
                                  QUERY PLAN
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Hash Join  (cost=25.50..45.20 rows=10 width=120)
   Hash Cond: (orders.customer_id = c.customer_id)
   ->  Seq Scan on orders  (cost=0.00..15.50 rows=50 width=80)
         Filter: (order_date >= (CURRENT_DATE - '7 days'::interval))
   ->  Hash  (cost=25.00..25.00 rows=40 width=48)
         ->  Seq Scan on customers c  (cost=0.00..25.00 rows=40 width=48)
               Filter: (city = 'New York'::text)
(7 rows)

-- No "CTE Scan" node = CTE was inlined (NOT MATERIALIZED)
Explanation: Simple CTE referenced once. PostgreSQL 12+ optimizer likely inlines CTE (NOT MATERIALIZED by default). EXPLAIN shows no "CTE Scan" node - CTE merged into main query. Orders filter (date >= ...) and customers filter (city = 'New York') both applied, allowing index usage if available. Inlining enables better optimization: push-down predicates, use indexes, parallel execution. Pre-PostgreSQL 12: CTE always materialized, forced evaluation of all recent_orders before joining. Post-12: smarter - only processes necessary rows.
🎯 Example 2: MATERIALIZED Hint - Multiple References
-- CTE referenced multiple times - force materialization
WITH expensive_calculation AS MATERIALIZED (
    SELECT 
        customer_id,
        COUNT(*) AS order_count,
        SUM(total_amount) AS total_spent,
        AVG(total_amount) AS avg_order_value,
        MAX(order_date) AS last_order
    FROM orders
    WHERE order_date >= '2025-01-01'
    GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT 
    'High Value' AS segment,
    COUNT(*) AS customer_count,
    AVG(total_spent) AS avg_total_spent
FROM expensive_calculation
WHERE total_spent >= 500

UNION ALL

SELECT 
    'Medium Value' AS segment,
    COUNT(*) AS customer_count,
    AVG(total_spent) AS avg_total_spent
FROM expensive_calculation
WHERE total_spent >= 100 AND total_spent < 500

UNION ALL

SELECT 
    'Low Value' AS segment,
    COUNT(*) AS customer_count,
    AVG(total_spent) AS avg_total_spent
FROM expensive_calculation
WHERE total_spent < 100;
Result:
   segment    | customer_count | avg_total_spent
--------------+----------------+-----------------
 High Value   |              3 |          875.00
 Medium Value |              5 |          285.60
 Low Value    |              2 |           45.80
(3 rows)
Explanation: expensive_calculation CTE referenced 3 times (three UNION ALL branches). MATERIALIZED hint forces evaluation once, stores results temporarily. Without MATERIALIZED, optimizer might re-run aggregation 3 times (expensive). Materialization: compute GROUP BY once, scan materialized results 3 times (faster). Trade-off: materialization cost + storage vs multiple evaluations. Use MATERIALIZED when: CTE expensive (aggregations, complex joins), referenced multiple times, consistent results needed. EXPLAIN shows "CTE Scan on expensive_calculation" confirming materialization.
🎯 Example 3: NOT MATERIALIZED Hint - Index Usage
-- Force inlining to enable index usage
WITH customer_filter AS NOT MATERIALIZED (
    SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name, email
    FROM customers
    WHERE city = 'Seattle'  -- Assume index on city
      AND registration_date >= '2025-01-01'
)
SELECT cf.*, o.order_id, o.total_amount
FROM customer_filter cf
INNER JOIN orders o ON cf.customer_id = o.customer_id
WHERE o.order_date >= '2026-01-01'  -- Recent orders only
ORDER BY o.order_date DESC
LIMIT 20;

-- Compare with MATERIALIZED:
-- MATERIALIZED: Materializes all Seattle customers from 2025+, then joins
-- NOT MATERIALIZED: Can use indexes on customers.city, orders.order_date
--                   simultaneously, processes only necessary rows
Result (first 3 rows):
 customer_id | first_name | last_name |       email         | order_id | total_amount
-------------+------------+-----------+---------------------+----------+--------------
        1025 | John       | Smith     | john.s@email.com    |     3156 |       285.90
        1025 | John       | Smith     | john.s@email.com    |     3142 |       198.50
        1030 | Sarah      | Lee       | sarah.lee@email.com |     3138 |       425.75
(3 rows)
Explanation: NOT MATERIALIZED forces CTE inlining. Inlined CTE allows optimizer to use indexes on both customers (city index) and orders (order_date index). With MATERIALIZED: materialize all Seattle customers → join with orders → filter by order_date → sort → limit. Without materialization (inlined): scan orders by order_date DESC using index → join to customers with city filter → stop at 20 rows. NOT MATERIALIZED enables "late" optimization decisions. Use NOT MATERIALIZED when: CTE has selective filters, indexes available, want parallel execution, processing small subset of large table.
🎯 Example 4: Optimization Fence - MATERIALIZED for Stability
-- Force materialization as optimization fence
WITH monthly_summary AS MATERIALIZED (
    SELECT 
        DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month,
        COUNT(*) AS order_count,
        SUM(total_amount) AS monthly_revenue,
        COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS unique_customers
    FROM orders
    WHERE order_date >= '2025-01-01'
    GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date)
)
SELECT 
    month,
    order_count,
    monthly_revenue,
    LAG(monthly_revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS prev_month_revenue,
    (monthly_revenue - LAG(monthly_revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month))::NUMERIC(10,2) AS revenue_change
FROM monthly_summary
ORDER BY month;
Result:
   month    | order_count | monthly_revenue | prev_month_revenue | revenue_change
------------+-------------+-----------------+--------------------+----------------
 2025-01-01 |          25 |         3250.00 |               NULL |           NULL
 2025-02-01 |          30 |         3875.50 |            3250.00 |         625.50
 2025-03-01 |          28 |         3625.90 |            3875.50 |        -249.60
 2025-04-01 |          35 |         4320.75 |            3625.90 |         694.85
(4 rows)
Explanation: MATERIALIZED creates optimization fence - separates aggregation from window functions. Prevents optimizer from rearranging query in unexpected ways. monthly_summary aggregates by month, then window functions (LAG) process aggregated results. Materialization ensures: consistent evaluation order, predictable performance, aggregation happens before windowing. Without MATERIALIZED, optimizer might try complex rewrite (not always beneficial). Use MATERIALIZED as fence when: query has multiple optimization stages, want predictable execution plan, prevent over-optimization, debugging performance issues (isolate stages).
🎯 Example 5: Performance Comparison - EXPLAIN ANALYZE
-- Test 1: Let optimizer decide
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
WITH product_sales AS (
    SELECT product_id, SUM(quantity) AS total_qty
    FROM order_items
    GROUP BY product_id
)
SELECT p.product_name, ps.total_qty
FROM products p
INNER JOIN product_sales ps ON p.product_id = ps.product_id
WHERE ps.total_qty > 10;

-- Test 2: Force MATERIALIZED
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
WITH product_sales AS MATERIALIZED (
    SELECT product_id, SUM(quantity) AS total_qty
    FROM order_items
    GROUP BY product_id
)
SELECT p.product_name, ps.total_qty
FROM products p
INNER JOIN product_sales ps ON p.product_id = ps.product_id
WHERE ps.total_qty > 10;

-- Test 3: Force NOT MATERIALIZED
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
WITH product_sales AS NOT MATERIALIZED (
    SELECT product_id, SUM(quantity) AS total_qty
    FROM order_items
    GROUP BY product_id
)
SELECT p.product_name, ps.total_qty
FROM products p
INNER JOIN product_sales ps ON p.product_id = ps.product_id
WHERE ps.total_qty > 10;
Performance Comparison:
Strategy          | Execution Time | Planning Time | Buffers
------------------+----------------+---------------+---------
Auto (optimizer)  | 12.5 ms        | 0.8 ms        | 48
MATERIALIZED      | 14.2 ms        | 0.7 ms        | 52
NOT MATERIALIZED  | 11.8 ms        | 1.2 ms        | 45

Interpretation:
- Auto: Optimizer chose NOT MATERIALIZED (similar to explicit NOT MATERIALIZED)
- MATERIALIZED: Slightly slower (materialization overhead)
- NOT MATERIALIZED: Fastest (inlined, pushed down total_qty > 10 filter)
Explanation: EXPLAIN ANALYZE measures actual execution time. Compare three strategies: automatic, forced MATERIALIZED, forced NOT MATERIALIZED. This example: NOT MATERIALIZED fastest because optimizer can push WHERE ps.total_qty > 10 filter into aggregation (compute only needed products). MATERIALIZED computes all products then filters. Optimizer chose NOT MATERIALIZED automatically (smart). Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to: verify materialization decision, compare strategies, identify performance issues, justify hint usage. Results vary by query, data distribution, indexes, hardware. Always benchmark with real data before choosing explicit hint.

7.8.4 Data-Modifying Statements in WITH

Data-Modifying CTEs enable INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE within WITH clause. Must use RETURNING to expose modified rows to rest of query. Syntax: WITH cte AS (DELETE/UPDATE/INSERT ... RETURNING *). Enables complex multi-table modifications in single statement. All CTEs execute independently (isolation). Use cases: move data between tables, audit trail creation, conditional modifications, complex data transformations.

Key Features: All modification CTEs execute before main query runs, modifications isolated (each CTE sees database snapshot at query start), ORDER of CTEs in WITH doesn't matter (all execute in parallel conceptually), RETURNING required to pass data to main query, main query can be SELECT (read), INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE. Transaction guarantees: all CTEs succeed or all rollback (atomic).

Best Practices: Use RETURNING to capture modified data, one modification per table per CTE, test in transaction (BEGIN; ... ROLLBACK;), use for atomic multi-table operations, document data flow between CTEs, be aware of trigger execution (triggers fire during CTE execution), consider constraints and foreign keys (all CTEs respect constraints).

Data-Modifying CTE Syntax
-- DELETE with RETURNING
WITH deleted_rows AS (
    DELETE FROM table WHERE condition
    RETURNING *
)
SELECT * FROM deleted_rows;

-- UPDATE with RETURNING
WITH updated_rows AS (
    UPDATE table SET column = value WHERE condition
    RETURNING *
)
SELECT * FROM updated_rows;

-- INSERT with RETURNING
WITH inserted_rows AS (
    INSERT INTO table (columns) VALUES (...)
    RETURNING *
)
SELECT * FROM inserted_rows;

-- Multiple modifications
WITH 
    deleted AS (DELETE FROM table1 WHERE ... RETURNING *),
    inserted AS (INSERT INTO table2 SELECT * FROM deleted RETURNING *)
SELECT * FROM inserted;
🎯 Example 1: Archive Old Records
-- Move completed orders older than 1 year to archive table
WITH archived_orders AS (
    DELETE FROM orders
    WHERE status = 'completed'
      AND order_date < CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 year'
    RETURNING *
),
inserted_archives AS (
    INSERT INTO orders_archive 
    SELECT * FROM archived_orders
    RETURNING order_id, customer_id, order_date, total_amount
)
SELECT 
    COUNT(*) AS archived_count,
    SUM(total_amount) AS archived_total_value,
    MIN(order_date) AS oldest_archived,
    MAX(order_date) AS newest_archived
FROM inserted_archives;
Result:
 archived_count | archived_total_value | oldest_archived | newest_archived
----------------+----------------------+-----------------+-----------------
             45 |              8250.75 | 2024-01-15      | 2024-12-28
(1 row)

-- Verification queries:
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE order_date < CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 year';
-- Result: 0 (all archived)

SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders_archive WHERE order_date < CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 year';
-- Result: 45 (newly archived)
Explanation: Two-step archival: archived_orders CTE deletes old completed orders, RETURNING captures deleted rows. inserted_archives CTE inserts archived_orders into orders_archive table. Main query aggregates archived data (count, total value, date range). Atomic operation: both DELETE and INSERT succeed together or both fail (transaction safety). RETURNING essential - passes deleted data to INSERT. Without CTE, would require: temporary table or application logic. CTE approach: single statement, atomic, efficient. Use case: data archival, table partitioning, ETL operations, compliance (data retention policies).
🎯 Example 2: Update with Audit Trail
-- Update product prices and log changes to audit table
WITH price_updates AS (
    UPDATE products
    SET 
        price = price * 1.10,  -- 10% price increase
        updated_at = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
    WHERE category_id = 1  -- Electronics only
    RETURNING 
        product_id, 
        product_name, 
        price / 1.10 AS old_price,  -- Calculate old price
        price AS new_price,
        CURRENT_TIMESTAMP AS change_date
),
audit_inserts AS (
    INSERT INTO price_audit (product_id, old_price, new_price, change_date, change_type)
    SELECT 
        product_id, 
        old_price, 
        new_price, 
        change_date,
        'increase' AS change_type
    FROM price_updates
    RETURNING *
)
SELECT 
    product_id,
    product_name,
    old_price::NUMERIC(10,2),
    new_price::NUMERIC(10,2),
    (new_price - old_price)::NUMERIC(10,2) AS price_increase,
    ((new_price - old_price) / old_price * 100)::NUMERIC(5,2) AS increase_pct
FROM price_updates
ORDER BY price_increase DESC;
Result:
 product_id |    product_name     | old_price | new_price | price_increase | increase_pct
------------+---------------------+-----------+-----------+----------------+--------------
       2006 | Mechanical Keyboard |     79.99 |     87.99 |           8.00 |        10.00
       2001 | Wireless Mouse      |     32.99 |     36.29 |           3.30 |        10.00
       2002 | USB-C Cable         |     14.29 |     15.72 |           1.43 |        10.00
       2009 | USB Hub             |     32.99 |     36.29 |           3.30 |        10.00
(4 rows)
Explanation: price_updates CTE updates product prices (+10%), RETURNING captures old and new prices. audit_inserts CTE logs changes to price_audit table. Main query displays update summary with calculated differences. Pattern: modify data + audit simultaneously in single statement. old_price calculated as price / 1.10 (reverses the 1.10 multiplication). Audit trail automatic - no application logic needed. Use cases: price management, inventory adjustments, status changes with logging, compliance (audit requirements), change tracking. All operations atomic - updates and audits succeed together.
🎯 Example 3: Conditional Multi-Table Insert
-- Process new orders: insert into orders, update inventory, create notifications
WITH new_order AS (
    INSERT INTO orders (customer_id, order_date, status, total_amount)
    VALUES (1001, CURRENT_DATE, 'pending', 199.98)
    RETURNING order_id, customer_id, total_amount
),
order_items_insert AS (
    INSERT INTO order_items (order_id, product_id, quantity, unit_price)
    SELECT 
        (SELECT order_id FROM new_order),
        2001,  -- product_id
        2,     -- quantity
        99.99  -- unit_price
    RETURNING order_id, product_id, quantity
),
inventory_update AS (
    UPDATE inventory
    SET 
        quantity = quantity - oi.quantity,
        updated_at = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
    FROM order_items_insert oi
    WHERE inventory.product_id = oi.product_id
    RETURNING inventory.product_id, inventory.quantity AS remaining_stock
),
notification AS (
    INSERT INTO notifications (customer_id, message, created_at)
    SELECT 
        customer_id,
        'Order #' || order_id || ' placed successfully. Total: $' || total_amount,
        CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
    FROM new_order
    RETURNING notification_id, message
)
SELECT 
    no.order_id,
    no.total_amount,
    iu.product_id,
    iu.remaining_stock,
    n.message
FROM new_order no
CROSS JOIN inventory_update iu
CROSS JOIN notification n;
Result:
 order_id | total_amount | product_id | remaining_stock |                    message
----------+--------------+------------+-----------------+-----------------------------------------------
     3201 |       199.98 |       2001 |             118 | Order #3201 placed successfully. Total: $199.98
(1 row)
Explanation: Complex order processing in single statement: new_order inserts order record, order_items_insert adds items, inventory_update decrements stock, notification creates customer notification. Four CTEs coordinate multi-table transaction. Each CTE uses RETURNING to pass data to subsequent CTEs. order_items_insert references new_order via subquery. inventory_update uses FROM order_items_insert (CTE join in UPDATE). All succeed or all rollback (atomic). Use case: order processing, booking systems, complex workflows, maintain referential integrity. Alternative requires multiple statements, application coordination, potential race conditions. CTE approach: single statement, database handles coordination.
🎯 Example 4: Bulk Update with Result Classification
-- Update stock for multiple products and classify results
WITH stock_updates AS (
    UPDATE products
    SET 
        stock_quantity = stock_quantity + v.additional_stock
    FROM (VALUES 
        (2001, 50),   -- product_id, additional_stock
        (2002, 30),
        (2003, -10),  -- Reduction
        (2006, 100)
    ) AS v(product_id, additional_stock)
    WHERE products.product_id = v.product_id
    RETURNING 
        products.product_id,
        products.product_name,
        products.stock_quantity - v.additional_stock AS old_quantity,
        products.stock_quantity AS new_quantity,
        v.additional_stock,
        CASE 
            WHEN v.additional_stock > 0 THEN 'restock'
            WHEN v.additional_stock < 0 THEN 'reduction'
            ELSE 'no_change'
        END AS change_type
)
SELECT 
    change_type,
    COUNT(*) AS update_count,
    SUM(additional_stock) AS total_change,
    STRING_AGG(product_name, ', ') AS affected_products
FROM stock_updates
GROUP BY change_type
ORDER BY change_type;
Result:
 change_type | update_count | total_change |          affected_products
-------------+--------------+--------------+---------------------------------------
 reduction   |            1 |          -10 | Laptop Stand
 restock     |            3 |          180 | Wireless Mouse, USB-C Cable, Mechanical Keyboard
(2 rows)
Detailed updates:
 product_id |    product_name     | old_quantity | new_quantity | additional_stock | change_type
------------+---------------------+--------------+--------------+------------------+-------------
       2001 | Wireless Mouse      |          120 |          170 |               50 | restock
       2002 | USB-C Cable         |           25 |           55 |               30 | restock
       2003 | Laptop Stand        |           50 |           40 |              -10 | reduction
       2006 | Mechanical Keyboard |           40 |          140 |              100 | restock
(4 rows)
Explanation: Bulk stock update using VALUES for multiple products. stock_updates CTE updates products joined with VALUES (inline data). RETURNING captures before/after quantities plus change classification. Main query aggregates by change_type (restock vs reduction). old_quantity calculated as new_quantity - additional_stock. change_type determined by CASE in RETURNING. Use cases: batch updates, inventory management, bulk price changes, reconciliation. VALUES-based updates cleaner than multiple UPDATE statements. Single transaction ensures consistency. STRING_AGG creates comma-separated product list per category.
🎯 Example 5: Complex Data Migration with Validation
-- Migrate data with validation and rollback summary
WITH 
    -- Step 1: Identify records to migrate
    records_to_migrate AS (
        SELECT customer_id, first_name, last_name, email, city
        FROM customers_staging
        WHERE validated = true
          AND NOT EXISTS (
              SELECT 1 FROM customers 
              WHERE customers.email = customers_staging.email
          )
    ),
    -- Step 2: Insert validated records
    migrated_records AS (
        INSERT INTO customers (first_name, last_name, email, city, registration_date)
        SELECT 
            first_name, 
            last_name, 
            email, 
            city,
            CURRENT_DATE
        FROM records_to_migrate
        RETURNING customer_id, email, city
    ),
    -- Step 3: Mark staging records as processed
    staging_updates AS (
        UPDATE customers_staging
        SET 
            processed = true,
            processed_date = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
        FROM records_to_migrate rtm
        WHERE customers_staging.email = rtm.email
        RETURNING customers_staging.customer_id AS staging_id
    ),
    -- Step 4: Log migration
    migration_log AS (
        INSERT INTO migration_log (migration_date, record_count, source_table, target_table)
        VALUES (
            CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
            (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM migrated_records),
            'customers_staging',
            'customers'
        )
        RETURNING log_id, record_count
    )
-- Summary of migration
SELECT 
    ml.log_id,
    ml.record_count AS migrated_count,
    (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM migrated_records WHERE city = 'New York') AS ny_count,
    (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM migrated_records WHERE city = 'Seattle') AS seattle_count,
    (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM staging_updates) AS staging_processed
FROM migration_log ml;
Result:
 log_id | migrated_count | ny_count | seattle_count | staging_processed
--------+----------------+----------+---------------+-------------------
   1001 |             15 |        6 |             4 |                15
(1 row)
Explanation: Complex 4-step ETL in single statement: records_to_migrate selects validated staging records (avoiding duplicates), migrated_records inserts into production table, staging_updates marks staging as processed, migration_log records migration audit. Each CTE builds on previous using RETURNING. NOT EXISTS prevents duplicate emails. All steps atomic - partial migration impossible. Main query provides migration summary with city breakdown. Use cases: ETL operations, data migration, staging to production promotion, import/export workflows. Advantages: single transaction, rollback on any failure, automatic logging, no orphaned records. Alternative multi-statement approach risks partial completion, requires complex error handling.

Chapter 8. Data Types

📖 About PostgreSQL Data Types

PostgreSQL has a rich type system that supports a wide variety of data types. In addition to standard SQL types, PostgreSQL provides specialized types for geometric data, network addresses, JSON documents, full-text search, and more. Understanding data types is crucial for database design, performance optimization, and ensuring data integrity.

PostgreSQL allows users to define their own custom types through domains, composite types, and enumerated types. This chapter covers all built-in types and explains how to use them effectively in your applications.

📊 Data Type Categories

🔢
Numeric Types Integers, decimals, floating-point, serial
📝
Text Types Character strings, text, varchar, char
📅
Date/Time Types Dates, times, timestamps, intervals
✅
Boolean Type True/false/null values
📐
Geometric Types Points, lines, circles, polygons
🌐
Network Types IP addresses, MAC addresses, CIDR
📦
JSON Types JSON and JSONB for document storage
🔤
Arrays & Composite Multi-dimensional arrays, custom types

8.1 Numeric Types

📖 About Numeric Types

Numeric types in PostgreSQL include integers (smallint, integer, bigint), arbitrary precision decimals (numeric, decimal), floating-point numbers (real, double precision), and auto-incrementing integers (serial types). Each type has specific storage requirements, range limitations, and performance characteristics.

Choosing the Right Type: Use INTEGER for most whole number columns, BIGINT for large IDs or counters, NUMERIC for financial calculations (exact precision), REAL/DOUBLE PRECISION for scientific calculations (approximate), and SERIAL for auto-incrementing primary keys. Consider storage size and performance trade-offs.

Best Practices: Always use NUMERIC for money amounts (avoid floating-point rounding errors), specify precision for NUMERIC types (e.g., NUMERIC(10,2) for currency), use appropriate integer sizes (don't use BIGINT when INTEGER suffices), leverage SERIAL types for surrogate keys, and be aware of arithmetic overflow possibilities.

📊 Numeric Types Comparison

Type Name Storage Range Use Case
SMALLINT 2 bytes -32,768 to 32,767 Small integers, age, quantity
INTEGER 4 bytes -2.1B to 2.1B Most common integer type, IDs
BIGINT 8 bytes -9.2 quintillion to 9.2 quintillion Large counters, timestamps
NUMERIC(p,s) Variable Up to 131,072 digits Financial data, exact precision
REAL 4 bytes 6 decimal digits precision Scientific calculations
DOUBLE PRECISION 8 bytes 15 decimal digits precision High-precision scientific
SERIAL 4 bytes 1 to 2.1B Auto-increment primary keys
BIGSERIAL 8 bytes 1 to 9.2 quintillion Large auto-increment IDs

8.1.1 Integer Types

📖 Full Description

Integer types store whole numbers without fractional components. PostgreSQL provides three integer types: SMALLINT (2 bytes, range -32,768 to 32,767), INTEGER or INT (4 bytes, range -2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647), and BIGINT (8 bytes, range -9,223,372,036,854,775,808 to 9,223,372,036,854,775,807). Integer arithmetic is fast and exact.

Type Selection Guidelines: Use SMALLINT for small ranges (age, quantity, status codes where range is limited), INTEGER for most general-purpose integer columns (the default choice), and BIGINT for large values (timestamps in milliseconds, large counters, scientific data). Choosing an appropriate size saves storage and can improve performance due to better cache utilization.

Arithmetic Operations: Integer division truncates (5/2 = 2, not 2.5). Use CAST or :: to convert to NUMERIC for precise division. Integer operations can overflow - adding two large INTEGERs may exceed the type's range causing an error. PostgreSQL detects overflow and raises an exception rather than silently wrapping around.

Integer Type Declaration Syntax
-- Column declarations
column_name SMALLINT
column_name INTEGER  -- or INT
column_name BIGINT

-- With constraints
age SMALLINT CHECK (age >= 0 AND age <= 150)
quantity INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0
user_id BIGINT PRIMARY KEY

🎯 Example 1: Basic Integer Columns

-- Create table with different integer types
CREATE TABLE product_inventory (
    product_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    quantity_in_stock SMALLINT NOT NULL CHECK (quantity_in_stock >= 0),
    total_units_sold BIGINT DEFAULT 0,
    reorder_threshold SMALLINT DEFAULT 10
);

-- Insert sample data
INSERT INTO product_inventory (product_id, quantity_in_stock, total_units_sold, reorder_threshold) 
VALUES 
    (2001, 125, 458923, 20),
    (2002, 45, 1250487, 15),
    (2003, 8, 89234567890, 10);

-- Query the data
SELECT 
    product_id,
    quantity_in_stock,
    total_units_sold,
    reorder_threshold,
    CASE 
        WHEN quantity_in_stock < reorder_threshold THEN 'REORDER NEEDED'
        ELSE 'SUFFICIENT STOCK'
    END AS stock_status
FROM product_inventory
ORDER BY quantity_in_stock;
Result:
 product_id | quantity_in_stock | total_units_sold | reorder_threshold | stock_status
------------+-------------------+------------------+-------------------+------------------
       2003 |                 8 |     89234567890 |                10 | REORDER NEEDED
       2002 |                45 |          1250487 |                15 | SUFFICIENT STOCK
       2001 |               125 |           458923 |                20 | SUFFICIENT STOCK
(3 rows)
Explanation: This example demonstrates the three integer types in a practical inventory scenario. SMALLINT used for quantity_in_stock (limited range 0-32,767 sufficient for most inventory), INTEGER for product_id (standard ID column), and BIGINT for total_units_sold (can accumulate to very large numbers over time). The CHECK constraint ensures non-negative stock quantities. CASE expression checks stock levels against thresholds. Each type chosen based on expected value range and storage efficiency.

🎯 Example 2: Integer Arithmetic

-- Demonstrate integer arithmetic operations
SELECT 
    15 + 7 AS addition,
    20 - 8 AS subtraction,
    6 * 9 AS multiplication,
    17 / 5 AS integer_division,          -- Truncates to 3
    17 % 5 AS modulo,                    -- Remainder is 2
    2 ^ 10 AS power,                     -- 2 to the 10th power
    |/ 64 AS square_root,                -- Square root
    17::NUMERIC / 5 AS precise_division; -- 3.4 with decimal
Result:
 addition | subtraction | multiplication | integer_division | modulo | power | square_root | precise_division
----------+-------------+----------------+------------------+--------+-------+-------------+------------------
       22 |          12 |             54 |                3 |      2 |  1024 |           8 |              3.4
(1 row)
Explanation: Integer arithmetic in PostgreSQL follows standard mathematical rules. Addition (+), subtraction (-), and multiplication (*) return integer results. Division (/) with two integers performs integer division, truncating any decimal portion (17/5 = 3, not 3.4). Modulo operator (%) returns the remainder. Power operator (^) raises number to exponent. Square root operator (|/) returns floating-point result. To get precise decimal division, cast at least one operand to NUMERIC using :: operator. Important: integer division truncation can surprise developers expecting decimal results - always cast when precision matters.

🎯 Example 3: Integer Range Limits

-- Demonstrate integer type limits
SELECT 
    32767::SMALLINT AS smallint_max,
    -32768::SMALLINT AS smallint_min,
    2147483647::INTEGER AS integer_max,
    -2147483648::INTEGER AS integer_min;

-- This will cause an overflow error (commented out to avoid error):
-- SELECT 32767::SMALLINT + 1;  -- ERROR: smallint out of range
-- SELECT 2147483647::INTEGER + 1;  -- ERROR: integer out of range

-- Safe way to handle large calculations
SELECT 
    32767::BIGINT + 1 AS safe_calculation,
    2147483647::BIGINT + 2147483647::BIGINT AS safe_large_sum;
Result:
 smallint_max | smallint_min | integer_max | integer_min
--------------+--------------+-------------+-------------
        32767 |       -32768 |  2147483647 | -2147483648
(1 row)

 safe_calculation | safe_large_sum
------------------+----------------
            32768 |     4294967294
(1 row)
Explanation: Each integer type has specific range limits: SMALLINT ±32K, INTEGER ±2.1 billion, BIGINT ±9.2 quintillion. Attempting arithmetic that exceeds these limits causes an overflow error (PostgreSQL raises exception rather than silent wraparound). The example shows maximum and minimum values for SMALLINT and INTEGER types. To prevent overflow, cast smaller types to larger types before arithmetic operations. This is essential in aggregate calculations (SUM, COUNT) where accumulated values might exceed original column type's range. Always consider potential overflow in financial calculations, counters, and cumulative statistics.

🎯 Example 4: Integer Types in E-Commerce Database

-- Query using integer columns from our e-commerce database
SELECT 
    o.order_id,                                    -- INTEGER
    o.customer_id,                                 -- INTEGER
    COUNT(oi.order_item_id) AS total_items,       -- BIGINT (COUNT returns BIGINT)
    SUM(oi.quantity) AS total_quantity,            -- BIGINT
    SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS order_total
FROM orders o
INNER JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
WHERE o.order_date >= '2026-01-01'
GROUP BY o.order_id, o.customer_id
HAVING SUM(oi.quantity) > 5
ORDER BY order_total DESC
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 order_id | customer_id | total_items | total_quantity | order_total
----------+-------------+-------------+----------------+-------------
     3007 |        1005 |           3 |             12 |      445.00
     3001 |        1001 |           2 |              6 |      157.94
     3006 |        1004 |           2 |              8 |      159.50
(3 rows)
Explanation: Real-world usage of integer types in e-commerce schema. order_id and customer_id use INTEGER (standard for primary/foreign keys). COUNT() aggregate function returns BIGINT even though counting fewer items - PostgreSQL ensures result type can handle large counts. SUM(quantity) aggregates SMALLINT values but returns BIGINT to prevent overflow. The HAVING clause filters groups with total_quantity > 5. ORDER BY sorts by calculated order_total. This demonstrates how PostgreSQL automatically handles type promotion in aggregate functions to prevent overflow, while maintaining storage efficiency in base tables.

🎯 Example 5: Integer Type Conversion and Casting

-- Demonstrate type casting and conversion
SELECT 
    '12345'::INTEGER AS string_to_int,
    12345::TEXT AS int_to_string,
    12.7::INTEGER AS float_to_int_truncate,
    CAST(12.7 AS INTEGER) AS float_to_int_cast,
    ROUND(12.7)::INTEGER AS float_to_int_rounded,
    '0xFF'::BIT(8)::INTEGER AS hex_to_int;

-- Type conversion in calculations
SELECT 
    product_id,
    product_name,
    price,
    (price * 1.15)::NUMERIC(10,2) AS price_with_tax,
    stock_quantity,
    (stock_quantity::REAL / 100 * 15)::INTEGER AS fifteen_percent
FROM products
WHERE product_id BETWEEN 2001 AND 2003;
Result:
 string_to_int | int_to_string | float_to_int_truncate | float_to_int_cast | float_to_int_rounded | hex_to_int
---------------+---------------+-----------------------+-------------------+----------------------+------------
         12345 | 12345         |                    12 |                12 |                   13 |        255
(1 row)

 product_id |    product_name     | price | price_with_tax | stock_quantity | fifteen_percent
------------+---------------------+-------+----------------+----------------+-----------------
       2001 | Wireless Mouse      | 32.99 |          37.94 |            120 |              18
       2002 | USB-C Cable         | 12.99 |          14.94 |             85 |              12
       2003 | Laptop Stand        | 49.99 |          57.49 |             45 |               6
(3 rows)
Explanation: Type conversion essential for data interchange and calculations. String literals cast to INTEGER using ::INTEGER or CAST() function. Floating-point to integer conversion truncates decimals unless ROUND() used first. TEXT to INTEGER useful for parsing input data.
