Date/Time input accepts multiple formats for flexibility. DATE accepts: '2026-01-26' (ISO), 'January 26, 2026', '01/26/2026' (depends on DateStyle). TIME accepts: '14:30:45', '2:30:45 PM'. TIMESTAMP combines both. PostgreSQL parses most reasonable formats but ISO 8601 (YYYY-MM-DD HH:MI:SS) recommended for unambiguous interchange.

🎯 Example 1: Various Input Formats

-- DATE input formats
SELECT 
    DATE '2026-01-26' AS iso_format,
    DATE '26-Jan-2026' AS abbreviated_month,
    DATE 'January 26, 2026' AS long_format,
    '2026-01-26'::DATE AS cast_syntax,
    TO_DATE('26/01/2026', 'DD/MM/YYYY') AS custom_format;

-- TIME input formats  
SELECT 
    TIME '14:30:45' AS military_time,
    TIME '2:30:45 PM' AS twelve_hour,
    TIME '14:30:45.123456' AS with_microseconds;

-- TIMESTAMP input
SELECT 
    TIMESTAMP '2026-01-26 14:30:45' AS space_separator,
    TIMESTAMP '2026-01-26T14:30:45' AS t_separator,
    TIMESTAMPTZ '2026-01-26 14:30:45+00' AS with_timezone,
    TIMESTAMPTZ '2026-01-26 14:30:45 UTC' AS timezone_name;
Result:
 iso_format | abbreviated_month | long_format |  cast_syntax | custom_format
------------+-------------------+-------------+--------------+---------------
 2026-01-26 | 2026-01-26        | 2026-01-26  | 2026-01-26   | 2026-01-26
(1 row)

 military_time | twelve_hour  | with_microseconds
---------------+--------------+-------------------
 14:30:45      | 14:30:45     | 14:30:45.123456
(1 row)

    space_separator      |    t_separator       |      with_timezone        |       timezone_name
-------------------------+----------------------+---------------------------+---------------------------
 2026-01-26 14:30:45     | 2026-01-26 14:30:45  | 2026-01-26 14:30:45+00    | 2026-01-26 14:30:45+00
(1 row)
Explanation: PostgreSQL accepts ISO 8601 format (YYYY-MM-DD), abbreviated months (Jan, Feb), spelled-out formats. Casting with :: or TYPE 'value' syntax both work. TO_DATE() parses custom formats using templates. TIME accepts 24-hour or 12-hour with AM/PM. TIMESTAMP accepts space or 'T' separator (ISO 8601). Timezone specified as offset (+00, -05) or name (UTC, PST). Recommendation: use ISO 8601 format in applications for unambiguous parsing across all locales.

8.5.2 Date/Time Output

Date/Time output controlled by DateStyle setting and TO_CHAR() formatting. Default output follows ISO format. TO_CHAR() provides extensive formatting options for custom display. Applications should format dates for user display rather than relying on database formatting for consistency across clients.

🎯 Example 1: Output Formatting with TO_CHAR

-- Various output formats using TO_CHAR
SELECT 
    order_date,
    TO_CHAR(order_date, 'YYYY-MM-DD') AS iso_format,
    TO_CHAR(order_date, 'DD/MM/YYYY') AS european_format,
    TO_CHAR(order_date, 'MM/DD/YYYY') AS us_format,
    TO_CHAR(order_date, 'Day, DD Month YYYY') AS long_format,
    TO_CHAR(order_date, 'Dy, Mon DD, YYYY') AS abbreviated,
    TO_CHAR(order_date, 'FMDay, FMMonth FMDD, YYYY') AS no_padding,
    TO_CHAR(order_date, 'HH24:MI:SS') AS time_only,
    TO_CHAR(order_date, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS') AS full_timestamp
FROM orders
WHERE order_id = 3001;
Result:
       order_date        | iso_format |  european_format  | us_format  |         long_format           |     abbreviated        |       no_padding           | time_only | full_timestamp
-------------------------+------------+-------------------+------------+-------------------------------+------------------------+----------------------------+-----------+---------------------
 2026-01-21 10:30:15     | 2026-01-21 | 21/01/2026        | 01/21/2026 | Wednesday, 21 January 2026    | Wed, Jan 21, 2026      | Wednesday, January 21, 2026| 10:30:15  | 2026-01-21 10:30:15
(1 row)
Explanation: TO_CHAR() formats dates using template patterns: YYYY (4-digit year), MM (month 01-12), DD (day 01-31), Day (weekday), Month (month name), HH24 (24-hour), MI (minutes), SS (seconds). FM prefix removes padding/blanks. Useful patterns: 'YYYY-MM-DD' (ISO), 'DD/MM/YYYY' (European), 'MM/DD/YYYY' (US), 'Day, Month DD, YYYY' (readable). Applications should format dates consistently for users while storing as DATE/TIMESTAMP internally.

8.5.3 Time Zones

Time zones critical for global applications. TIMESTAMPTZ stores UTC internally, displays in session timezone (controlled by timezone setting). AT TIME ZONE converts between zones. PostgreSQL uses IANA timezone database (America/New_York, Europe/London). Automatically handles DST transitions. Use TIMESTAMPTZ for absolute time points, TIMESTAMP for local times.

🎯 Example 1: Timezone Conversion Examples

-- Show current timezone
SHOW timezone;

-- Convert between timezones
WITH utc_time AS (
    SELECT TIMESTAMPTZ '2026-06-15 14:00:00 UTC' AS event_time
)
SELECT 
    event_time,
    event_time AT TIME ZONE 'America/New_York' AS ny,
    event_time AT TIME ZONE 'America/Los_Angeles' AS la,
    event_time AT TIME ZONE 'Europe/London' AS london,
    event_time AT TIME ZONE 'Asia/Tokyo' AS tokyo,
    event_time AT TIME ZONE 'Australia/Sydney' AS sydney
FROM utc_time;
Result:
 timezone
----------
 UTC
(1 row)

      event_time           |        ny           |        la           |      london         |       tokyo         |      sydney
---------------------------+---------------------+---------------------+---------------------+---------------------+---------------------
 2026-06-15 14:00:00+00    | 2026-06-15 10:00:00 | 2026-06-15 07:00:00 | 2026-06-15 15:00:00 | 2026-06-15 23:00:00 | 2026-06-16 00:00:00
(1 row)
Explanation: AT TIME ZONE converts TIMESTAMPTZ to timestamp at specified zone (removes timezone). NY is UTC-4 (June DST), LA is UTC-7, London is UTC+1 (BST), Tokyo is UTC+9, Sydney is UTC+10. Note Sydney crosses midnight. PostgreSQL automatically applies DST rules based on date. For scheduling meetings across timezones, store as TIMESTAMPTZ in UTC, display using AT TIME ZONE for each participant's timezone. Always use named timezones (America/New_York) not abbreviations (EST) - abbreviations ambiguous and don't handle DST.

8.5.4 Interval Input

INTERVAL input accepts various formats: SQL standard ('2 days 3 hours'), PostgreSQL ('2 days 3:00:00'), ISO 8601 ('P2DT3H'). Supports units: years, months, weeks, days, hours, minutes, seconds. INTERVAL useful for date arithmetic, age calculations, durations. Can be positive or negative.

🎯 Example 1: INTERVAL Input Formats

-- Various INTERVAL input formats
SELECT 
    INTERVAL '1 day' AS one_day,
    INTERVAL '2 weeks' AS two_weeks,
    INTERVAL '3 months' AS three_months,
    INTERVAL '4 years' AS four_years,
    INTERVAL '2 hours 30 minutes' AS two_and_half_hours,
    INTERVAL '1 day 2:30:45' AS mixed_format,
    INTERVAL '90 minutes' AS ninety_minutes,
    INTERVAL '-5 days' AS negative_interval,
    '3 days'::INTERVAL AS cast_syntax;
Result:
 one_day | two_weeks | three_months | four_years | two_and_half_hours |   mixed_format   | ninety_minutes | negative_interval | cast_syntax
---------+-----------+--------------+------------+--------------------+------------------+----------------+-------------------+-------------
 1 day   | 14 days   | 3 mons       | 4 years    | 02:30:00           | 1 day 02:30:45   | 01:30:00       | -5 days           | 3 days
(1 row)
Explanation: INTERVAL accepts human-readable durations. Supports singular or plural (day/days). Can combine units ('2 days 3 hours'). Time format HH:MI:SS also accepted. Months and years track calendar units (not converted to days). Minutes/hours converted to time format. Negative intervals subtract from dates. Use INTERVAL for date arithmetic: NOW() + INTERVAL '1 week', order_date - INTERVAL '30 days'. More readable than manual day calculations.

8.5.5 Interval Output

INTERVAL output displays as combination of years, months, days, and time. Controlled by IntervalStyle setting: 'postgres', 'postgres_verbose', 'sql_standard', 'iso_8601'. Can extract components with EXTRACT() or format with TO_CHAR(). Use JUSTIFY functions to normalize intervals.

🎯 Example 1: INTERVAL Operations and Formatting

-- INTERVAL operations
SELECT 
    AGE(TIMESTAMP '2000-01-01') AS age_from_2000,
    EXTRACT(YEAR FROM AGE(TIMESTAMP '2000-01-01')) AS years,
    EXTRACT(MONTH FROM AGE(TIMESTAMP '2000-01-01')) AS months,
    EXTRACT(DAY FROM AGE(TIMESTAMP '2000-01-01')) AS days,
    INTERVAL '30 days' + INTERVAL '5 hours' AS combined,
    INTERVAL '2 hours' * 3 AS multiplied,
    INTERVAL '10 days' / 2 AS divided,
    JUSTIFY_HOURS(INTERVAL '27 hours') AS justify_hours_example,
    JUSTIFY_DAYS(INTERVAL '35 days') AS justify_days_example;
Result:
    age_from_2000     | years | months | days |    combined     | multiplied |  divided  | justify_hours_example | justify_days_example
----------------------+-------+--------+------+-----------------+------------+-----------+-----------------------+----------------------
 26 years 25 days     |    26 |      0 |   25 | 30 days 05:00:00| 06:00:00   | 5 days    | 1 day 03:00:00        | 1 mon 5 days
(1 row)
Explanation: AGE() returns INTERVAL between timestamps. EXTRACT() retrieves components from intervals. INTERVAL arithmetic: addition combines intervals, multiplication/division scale intervals. JUSTIFY_HOURS() converts 24+ hours to days. JUSTIFY_DAYS() converts 30+ days to months (approximate). These functions normalize intervals for display. Use INTERVAL for: calculating age, measuring elapsed time, scheduling recurring events, time-based reporting. INTERVAL respects calendar complexities (varying month lengths, leap years).

8.6 Boolean Type

📖 About Boolean Type

The BOOLEAN type stores true/false values for logical operations. PostgreSQL boolean type has three states: TRUE, FALSE, and NULL (unknown). Storage: 1 byte per value. Boolean columns essential for flags, status indicators, feature toggles, and conditional logic.

Input Values: TRUE accepts: 'true', 't', 'yes', 'y', 'on', '1'. FALSE accepts: 'false', 'f', 'no', 'n', 'off', '0'. Case-insensitive. Output always displays as 't' or 'f' (or NULL). Boolean expressions support AND, OR, NOT operators and compare with standard relational operators.

Best Practices: Use BOOLEAN for binary states (is_active, is_deleted, email_verified). Set DEFAULT TRUE or FALSE to avoid NULL confusion. NULL means "unknown" - be careful with three-valued logic. Index boolean columns used in frequent WHERE clauses. Use CHECK constraint with BOOLEAN for data validation. Boolean columns make queries readable: WHERE is_active instead of WHERE status = 'active'.

BOOLEAN Type Syntax
-- Column declaration
column_name BOOLEAN
column_name BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE
column_name BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE

-- Input values for TRUE
TRUE, 't', 'true', 'y', 'yes', 'on', '1'

-- Input values for FALSE  
FALSE, 'f', 'false', 'n', 'no', 'off', '0'

-- Boolean expressions
WHERE column_name = TRUE
WHERE column_name              -- Same as = TRUE
WHERE NOT column_name          -- Same as = FALSE
WHERE column_name IS NULL

🎯 Example 1: Basic Boolean Operations

-- Create table with boolean columns
CREATE TABLE user_accounts (
    user_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    username VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    is_active BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE,
    email_verified BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
    is_admin BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
    newsletter_subscribed BOOLEAN
);

INSERT INTO user_accounts (username, is_active, email_verified, is_admin, newsletter_subscribed)
VALUES 
    ('alice', TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE),
    ('bob', 'yes', 'no', FALSE, 'yes'),     -- String inputs
    ('charlie', 't', 'f', FALSE, NULL),      -- Single char inputs
    ('diana', FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE);

-- Query with boolean conditions
SELECT 
    username,
    is_active,
    email_verified,
    is_admin,
    newsletter_subscribed,
    CASE WHEN is_admin THEN 'Administrator' ELSE 'User' END AS role
FROM user_accounts
WHERE is_active = TRUE
ORDER BY username;
Result:
 username | is_active | email_verified | is_admin | newsletter_subscribed |      role
----------+-----------+----------------+----------+-----------------------+----------------
 alice    | t         | t              | f        | t                     | User
 bob      | t         | f              | f        | t                     | User
 charlie  | t         | f              | f        |                       | User
(3 rows)
Explanation: BOOLEAN columns store binary states. Multiple input formats accepted: TRUE/'yes'/'t'/1 for true, FALSE/'no'/'f'/0 for false. Output displays as 't' or 'f'. WHERE is_active = TRUE filters active users (can omit = TRUE: WHERE is_active). NULL indicates unknown state (charlie hasn't set newsletter preference). CASE expressions use boolean values for branching logic. Boolean columns self-documenting - clearer than VARCHAR status codes. Common pattern: is_active flag for soft deletes, email_verified for account confirmation, is_admin for permissions.

🎯 Example 2: Boolean Logic and Operators

-- Boolean logic operations
SELECT 
    username,
    is_active,
    email_verified,
    is_admin,
    is_active AND email_verified AS active_and_verified,
    is_active OR is_admin AS active_or_admin,
    NOT is_active AS not_active,
    is_active AND (email_verified OR is_admin) AS complex_condition
FROM user_accounts
ORDER BY username;
Result:
 username | is_active | email_verified | is_admin | active_and_verified | active_or_admin | not_active | complex_condition
----------+-----------+----------------+----------+---------------------+-----------------+------------+-------------------
 alice    | t         | t              | f        | t                   | t               | f          | t
 bob      | t         | f              | f        | f                   | t               | f          | f
 charlie  | t         | f              | f        | f                   | t               | f          | f
 diana    | f         | f              | t        | f                   | t               | t          | f
(4 rows)
Explanation: Boolean operators: AND (both must be true), OR (at least one true), NOT (negates value). Operator precedence: NOT, then AND, then OR (use parentheses for clarity). Complex conditions combine operators: is_active AND (email_verified OR is_admin) checks if user active and either verified or admin. Three-valued logic: TRUE AND NULL = NULL, FALSE AND NULL = FALSE, TRUE OR NULL = TRUE. Always consider NULL cases when using boolean operators. Use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL to explicitly check for unknown state.

🎯 Example 3: Three-Valued Logic with NULL

-- Demonstrate three-valued logic
SELECT 
    username,
    newsletter_subscribed,
    newsletter_subscribed = TRUE AS explicitly_true,
    newsletter_subscribed = FALSE AS explicitly_false,
    newsletter_subscribed IS NULL AS is_null,
    newsletter_subscribed IS NOT NULL AS is_not_null,
    COALESCE(newsletter_subscribed, FALSE) AS default_to_false
FROM user_accounts
ORDER BY newsletter_subscribed NULLS LAST;
Result:
 username | newsletter_subscribed | explicitly_true | explicitly_false | is_null | is_not_null | default_to_false
----------+-----------------------+-----------------+------------------+---------+-------------+------------------
 diana    | f                     | f               | t                | f       | t           | f
 alice    | t                     | t               | f                | f       | t           | t
 bob      | t                     | t               | f                | f       | t           | t
 charlie  |                       |                 |                  | t       | f           | f
(4 rows)
Explanation: NULL in boolean context represents "unknown" state. Comparing NULL with = returns NULL (not FALSE). IS NULL checks explicitly for NULL. COALESCE() provides default value when NULL encountered. Three-valued logic can be tricky: WHERE column = TRUE excludes both FALSE and NULL rows. Use WHERE column IS TRUE to exclude NULL, or WHERE column IS NOT FALSE to include only TRUE. NULLS LAST in ORDER BY places NULLs at end. Best practice: set DEFAULT value on boolean columns to avoid NULL confusion in application logic.

🎯 Example 4: Boolean Aggregations

-- Aggregate boolean values
SELECT 
    COUNT(*) AS total_users,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE is_active) AS active_users,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE NOT is_active) AS inactive_users,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE email_verified) AS verified_users,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE is_admin) AS admin_count,
    BOOL_AND(is_active) AS all_active,
    BOOL_OR(email_verified) AS any_verified,
    ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE is_active) / COUNT(*), 2) AS active_percentage
FROM user_accounts;
Result:
 total_users | active_users | inactive_users | verified_users | admin_count | all_active | any_verified | active_percentage
-------------+--------------+----------------+----------------+-------------+------------+--------------+-------------------
           4 |            3 |              1 |              1 |           1 | f          | t            |             75.00
(1 row)
Explanation: COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE condition) counts rows meeting boolean condition. BOOL_AND() returns TRUE if all values TRUE (like AND across rows), FALSE otherwise. BOOL_OR() returns TRUE if any value TRUE (like OR across rows). Useful for validation: BOOL_AND(is_verified) checks all users verified. Percentage calculations: COUNT(WHERE condition) / COUNT(*) * 100. Boolean aggregations enable summary statistics: activation rates, verification rates, feature adoption metrics. NULL values excluded from aggregations unless explicitly checked.

🎯 Example 5: Boolean Indexes and Performance

-- Boolean columns benefit from partial indexes
CREATE INDEX idx_active_users ON user_accounts(user_id) WHERE is_active = TRUE;
CREATE INDEX idx_unverified_emails ON user_accounts(user_id) WHERE email_verified = FALSE;

-- Query using boolean filter (can use index)
SELECT username, is_active, email_verified
FROM user_accounts
WHERE is_active = TRUE
  AND email_verified = FALSE;

-- Update boolean values
UPDATE user_accounts
SET email_verified = TRUE
WHERE username = 'bob'
RETURNING username, email_verified;

-- Toggle boolean value
UPDATE user_accounts
SET is_active = NOT is_active
WHERE username = 'diana'
RETURNING username, is_active;
Result:
 username | is_active | email_verified
----------+-----------+----------------
 bob      | t         | f
(1 row)

 username | email_verified
----------+----------------
 bob      | t
(1 row)

 username | is_active
----------+-----------
 diana    | t
(1 row)
Explanation: Partial indexes on boolean columns efficient - index only rows where condition TRUE (smaller index). Common pattern: index WHERE is_active = TRUE since queries usually filter active records. Boolean updates straightforward: SET column = TRUE/FALSE. NOT operator toggles value. RETURNING clause confirms updated values. Boolean columns ideal for status tracking: soft deletes (is_deleted), feature flags (feature_enabled), workflow states (is_approved). Index columns used frequently in WHERE clauses for performance.

8.7 Enumerated Types

📖 About Enumerated Types

Enumerated types (ENUMs) are user-defined types with a static ordered set of values. Example: order_status ENUM can only be 'pending', 'processing', 'shipped', 'delivered'. ENUMs provide type safety - database rejects invalid values. More space-efficient than VARCHAR (stores 4-byte integer internally). Values have specific sort order.

Use Cases: ENUMs ideal for columns with fixed set of values: order status, priority levels, days of week, user roles, product categories. Advantages: data validation at database level, space efficiency, self-documenting schema, ordering enforcement. Disadvantages: harder to modify (adding values requires ALTER TYPE), not as flexible as CHECK constraint with VARCHAR.

Considerations: Choose ENUM when values rarely change and order matters. Use VARCHAR with CHECK constraint for frequently changing value sets. ENUMs don't support internationalization naturally (values are English strings). Cannot easily add/remove values without ALTER TYPE. Consider lookup tables (reference tables) for frequently changing value sets or when needing additional metadata per value.

8.7.1 Declaration of Enumerated Types

Creating ENUM types uses CREATE TYPE with AS ENUM clause. Once created, ENUM type used like built-in types. Values listed in desired sort order. Creating column with ENUM type ensures only listed values allowed. ENUMs are database-level objects visible to all schemas.

ENUM Type Creation Syntax
-- Create ENUM type
CREATE TYPE enum_name AS ENUM ('value1', 'value2', 'value3');

-- Use in table
CREATE TABLE table_name (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    status enum_name NOT NULL
);

-- Add values to existing ENUM (PostgreSQL 9.1+)
ALTER TYPE enum_name ADD VALUE 'new_value';
ALTER TYPE enum_name ADD VALUE 'new_value' BEFORE 'existing_value';
ALTER TYPE enum_name ADD VALUE 'new_value' AFTER 'existing_value';

🎯 Example 1: Creating and Using ENUM Types

-- Create ENUM types for e-commerce
CREATE TYPE order_status_enum AS ENUM (
    'pending',
    'processing', 
    'shipped',
    'delivered',
    'cancelled'
);

CREATE TYPE priority_level AS ENUM ('low', 'medium', 'high', 'urgent');

-- Create table using ENUMs
CREATE TABLE order_tracking (
    tracking_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    order_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    status order_status_enum NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending',
    priority priority_level DEFAULT 'medium',
    updated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

-- Insert data (only valid ENUM values accepted)
INSERT INTO order_tracking (order_id, status, priority)
VALUES 
    (3001, 'pending', 'high'),
    (3002, 'processing', 'medium'),
    (3003, 'shipped', 'low'),
    (3004, 'delivered', 'urgent');

-- This would error: invalid ENUM value
-- INSERT INTO order_tracking (order_id, status) VALUES (3005, 'invalid_status');

SELECT * FROM order_tracking ORDER BY priority DESC, status;
Result:
 tracking_id | order_id |   status    | priority |       updated_at
-------------+----------+-------------+----------+-------------------------
           4 |     3004 | delivered   | urgent   | 2026-01-26 10:30:15
           1 |     3001 | pending     | high     | 2026-01-26 10:30:15
           2 |     3002 | processing  | medium   | 2026-01-26 10:30:15
           3 |     3003 | shipped     | low      | 2026-01-26 10:30:15
(4 rows)
Explanation: CREATE TYPE AS ENUM defines custom enumeration. Values listed in order (important for sorting). Column declared with ENUM type name. Only values from ENUM definition accepted - invalid values cause error. DEFAULT value must be from ENUM. ORDER BY respects ENUM order: 'low' < 'medium' < 'high' < 'urgent'. ENUMs provide automatic validation - no need for CHECK constraints. Database enforces data integrity. Status transitions tracked with valid states only.

8.7.2 Ordering

ENUM ordering determined by declaration order, not alphabetical. First value listed is "smallest", last is "largest". Comparison operators (<, >, <=, >=, =, !=) work with ENUMs respecting this order. ORDER BY uses ENUM order. Cannot change order without dropping and recreating type.

🎯 Example 1: ENUM Ordering Examples

-- Demonstrate ENUM ordering
SELECT 
    order_id,
    status,
    priority,
    status > 'pending' AS beyond_pending,
    priority >= 'high' AS high_priority_or_above
FROM order_tracking
WHERE priority >= 'high'
ORDER BY status;
Result:
 order_id |  status   | priority | beyond_pending | high_priority_or_above
----------+-----------+----------+----------------+------------------------
     3001 | pending   | high     | f              | t
     3004 | delivered | urgent   | t              | t
(2 rows)
Explanation: ENUM comparison uses declaration order. 'pending' < 'processing' < 'shipped' < 'delivered' < 'cancelled' based on CREATE TYPE order. status > 'pending' filters progressed orders. priority >= 'high' includes 'high' and 'urgent'. ORDER BY status sorts by ENUM order (pending before delivered). This ordering semantic useful for workflow tracking: WHERE status >= 'shipped' finds shipped/delivered orders. Order must match business logic when creating ENUM.

8.7.3 Type Safety

ENUM type safety prevents invalid values at database level. Cannot insert strings not in ENUM definition. Cannot compare ENUMs of different types. Provides stronger guarantees than CHECK constraints. Applications cannot bypass validation.

🎯 Example 1: Type Safety Demonstrations

-- ENUM rejects invalid values (this would error if uncommented)
-- INSERT INTO order_tracking (order_id, status) VALUES (9999, 'invalid');
-- ERROR: invalid input value for enum order_status_enum: "invalid"

-- Must cast string to ENUM explicitly
INSERT INTO order_tracking (order_id, status, priority)
VALUES (3005, 'pending'::order_status_enum, 'low'::priority_level)
RETURNING tracking_id, order_id, status;

-- List all valid ENUM values
SELECT enumlabel AS valid_statuses
FROM pg_enum
WHERE enumtypid = 'order_status_enum'::regtype
ORDER BY enumsortorder;
Result:
 tracking_id | order_id |  status
-------------+----------+---------
           5 |     3005 | pending
(1 row)

 valid_statuses
----------------
 pending
 processing
 shipped
 delivered
 cancelled
(5 rows)
Explanation: ENUM type safety prevents typos and invalid data. Attempting to insert 'processing' (typo: extra 's') fails with clear error message. Explicit casting required when inserting string literals (::enum_type). pg_enum system catalog stores ENUM definitions. Query pg_enum to list valid values programmatically. Type safety advantage: catch errors at insert time vs. discovering bad data later. Applications can query pg_enum to populate dropdowns with valid values. ENUMs self-documenting - schema shows all possible values.

8.7.4 Implementation Details

ENUM implementation stores 4-byte integer internally (efficient). String labels stored once in system catalog. Comparison fast (integer comparison). Storage: 4 bytes per value regardless of string length. More efficient than VARCHAR when used repeatedly. Cannot drop ENUM values (would break data integrity). Adding values safe with ALTER TYPE ADD VALUE.

🎯 Example 1: ENUM Storage and Performance

-- Compare storage: ENUM vs VARCHAR
CREATE TABLE enum_test (id SERIAL, status order_status_enum);
CREATE TABLE varchar_test (id SERIAL, status VARCHAR(20));

INSERT INTO enum_test (status) 
SELECT 'processing' FROM generate_series(1, 1000);

INSERT INTO varchar_test (status)
SELECT 'processing' FROM generate_series(1, 1000);

-- Check table sizes
SELECT 
    pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size('enum_test')) AS enum_size,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size('varchar_test')) AS varchar_size,
    pg_column_size('processing'::order_status_enum) AS enum_value_bytes,
    pg_column_size('processing'::VARCHAR) AS varchar_value_bytes;
Result:
 enum_size | varchar_size | enum_value_bytes | varchar_value_bytes
-----------+--------------+------------------+---------------------
 64 kB     | 72 kB        |                4 |                  11
(1 row)
Explanation: ENUMs store as 4-byte integers internally (constant size). VARCHAR stores full string (10 bytes for 'processing' + 1 overhead). With 1000 rows, ENUM table smaller. Advantage increases with longer strings and more rows. ENUM comparisons faster (integer comparison vs. string comparison). Limitation: ALTER TYPE ADD VALUE must scan table to verify no conflicts. Cannot remove ENUM values once data exists. Trade-off: storage/performance efficiency vs. flexibility. Use ENUM for truly static value sets, lookup tables for dynamic ones.

8.8 Geometric Types

📖 About Geometric Types

Geometric types represent two-dimensional spatial objects: points, lines, line segments, boxes, paths, polygons, and circles. Useful for GIS applications, game development, computational geometry, and spatial queries. PostgreSQL provides rich set of geometric operators and functions for calculations (distance, intersection, containment).

Types Overview: POINT (x,y coordinates), LINE (infinite line), LSEG (line segment), BOX (rectangle), PATH (sequence of points - open or closed), POLYGON (closed path), CIRCLE (center point + radius). All types support indexing with GiST indexes for spatial queries. For advanced GIS, consider PostGIS extension.

8.8.1 Points

POINT type represents location in 2D space with (x,y) coordinates. Storage: 16 bytes. Input format: '(x,y)' or 'x,y'. Supports distance calculations, proximity searches, coordinate operations.

🎯 Example: Point Operations

-- Create table with points (store locations)
CREATE TABLE locations (
    location_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(100),
    coordinates POINT
);

INSERT INTO locations (name, coordinates) VALUES
    ('Warehouse A', POINT(10, 20)),
    ('Store B', '(30, 40)'),
    ('Office C', POINT(50, 10));

-- Calculate distances between points
SELECT 
    l1.name AS from_location,
    l2.name AS to_location,
    l1.coordinates,
    l2.coordinates,
    l1.coordinates <-> l2.coordinates AS distance
FROM locations l1, locations l2
WHERE l1.location_id < l2.location_id
ORDER BY distance;
Result:
 from_location | to_location | coordinates | coordinates |     distance
---------------+-------------+-------------+-------------+------------------
 Warehouse A   | Office C    | (10,20)     | (50,10)     | 41.2310562561766
 Warehouse A   | Store B     | (10,20)     | (30,40)     | 28.2842712474619
 Store B       | Office C    | (30,40)     | (50,10)     | 36.0555127546399
(3 rows)
Explanation: POINT stores 2D coordinates. <-> operator calculates Euclidean distance. Useful for proximity searches, nearest neighbor queries, delivery routing. GiST index on POINT columns accelerates spatial queries.

8.8.2 Lines

LINE type represents infinite line defined by equation Ax + By + C = 0. Storage: 32 bytes. Input: '{A,B,C}' or '[(x1,y1),(x2,y2)]' (two points determine line). Less commonly used than LSEG (line segments).

8.8.3 Line Segments

LSEG type represents finite line segment with start and end points. Storage: 32 bytes. Input: '[(x1,y1),(x2,y2)]'. Useful for routes, connections, boundaries between two points.

🎯 Example: Line Segment Operations

-- Create shipping routes
CREATE TABLE routes (
    route_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    route_name VARCHAR(50),
    path LSEG
);

INSERT INTO routes (route_name, path) VALUES
    ('Route 1', LSEG(POINT(0,0), POINT(10,10))),
    ('Route 2', '[(5,0),(5,15)]'),
    ('Route 3', LSEG(POINT(0,5), POINT(15,5)));

-- Calculate route lengths and check intersections
SELECT 
    r1.route_name,
    r1.path,
    LENGTH(r1.path) AS route_length,
    r1.path ?# LSEG(POINT(5,5), POINT(10,5)) AS intersects_horizontal
FROM routes r1;
Result:
 route_name |      path       |   route_length   | intersects_horizontal
------------+-----------------+------------------+-----------------------
 Route 1    | [(0,0),(10,10)] | 14.142135623731  | t
 Route 2    | [(5,0),(5,15)]  | 15               | t
 Route 3    | [(0,5),(15,5)]  | 15               | t
(3 rows)
Explanation: LSEG stores line segments. LENGTH() calculates segment length. ?# operator tests intersection. Useful for route planning, collision detection, boundary checking.

8.8.4 Boxes

BOX type represents rectangle defined by opposite corners. Storage: 32 bytes. Input: '((x1,y1),(x2,y2))' or 'x1,y1,x2,y2'. Corners automatically normalized (lower-left and upper-right stored). Common for bounding boxes, regions, map tiles.

🎯 Example: Box Operations

-- Define delivery zones as boxes
CREATE TABLE delivery_zones (
    zone_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    zone_name VARCHAR(50),
    area BOX
);

INSERT INTO delivery_zones (zone_name, area) VALUES
    ('Zone A', BOX(POINT(0,0), POINT(10,10))),
    ('Zone B', '((10,0),(20,10))'),
    ('Zone C', BOX(POINT(5,5), POINT(15,15)));

-- Check point containment and area calculations
SELECT 
    zone_name,
    area,
    width(area) AS zone_width,
    height(area) AS zone_height,
    area(area) AS zone_area,
    area @> POINT(7,7) AS contains_point_7_7,
    area && BOX(POINT(8,8), POINT(12,12)) AS overlaps_region
FROM delivery_zones;
Result:
 zone_name |       area        | zone_width | zone_height | zone_area | contains_point_7_7 | overlaps_region
-----------+-------------------+------------+-------------+-----------+--------------------+-----------------
 Zone A    | (10,10),(0,0)     |         10 |          10 |       100 | t                  | t
 Zone B    | (20,10),(10,0)    |         10 |          10 |       100 | f                  | t
 Zone C    | (15,15),(5,5)     |         10 |          10 |       100 | t                  | t
(3 rows)
Explanation: BOX defines rectangles. width() and height() return dimensions. area() calculates area. @> tests point containment. && tests box overlap. Useful for spatial indexing, region queries, map-based searches.

8.8.5 Paths

PATH type represents sequence of connected points. Can be open [(...)] or closed ((...)] (polygon). Storage: 16+16n bytes (n=number of points). Useful for routes, boundaries, complex shapes.

🎯 Example: Path Operations

-- Store delivery routes as paths
CREATE TABLE delivery_paths (
    path_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    description VARCHAR(100),
    route PATH
);

INSERT INTO delivery_paths (description, route) VALUES
    ('Open path', PATH '[(0,0),(10,10),(20,5)]'),
    ('Closed path', PATH '((0,0),(10,0),(10,10),(0,10))');

SELECT 
    description,
    route,
    npoints(route) AS point_count,
    isclosed(route) AS is_closed,
    length(route) AS path_length
FROM delivery_paths;
Result:
 description |           route                  | point_count | is_closed | path_length
-------------+----------------------------------+-------------+-----------+--------------
 Open path   | [(0,0),(10,10),(20,5)]           |           3 | f         | 25.622...
 Closed path | ((0,0),(10,0),(10,10),(0,10))    |           4 | t         | 40
(3 rows)
Explanation: PATH stores point sequences. Square brackets [] = open path, parentheses () = closed. npoints() counts vertices. isclosed() checks if closed. length() calculates total path length.

8.8.6 Polygons

POLYGON type represents closed path (same as closed PATH but specialized operations). Storage: 40+16n bytes. Input: '((x1,y1),...,(xn,yn))'. Useful for land parcels, coverage areas, boundaries.

🎯 Example: Polygon Operations

-- Define service coverage areas
CREATE TABLE coverage_areas (
    area_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    service_name VARCHAR(50),
    coverage POLYGON
);

INSERT INTO coverage_areas (service_name, coverage) VALUES
    ('Service A', POLYGON '((0,0),(10,0),(10,10),(0,10))'),
    ('Service B', '((5,5),(15,5),(15,15),(5,15))');

SELECT 
    service_name,
    coverage,
    npoints(coverage) AS vertices,
    area(coverage) AS coverage_area,
    center(coverage) AS center_point,
    coverage @> POINT(7,7) AS covers_point_7_7
FROM coverage_areas;
Result:
 service_name |            coverage                  | vertices | coverage_area | center_point | covers_point_7_7
--------------+--------------------------------------+----------+---------------+--------------+------------------
 Service A    | ((0,0),(10,0),(10,10),(0,10))        |        4 |           100 | (5,5)        | t
 Service B    | ((5,5),(15,5),(15,15),(5,15))        |        4 |           100 | (10,10)      | t
(4 rows)
Explanation: POLYGON represents closed areas. area() calculates enclosed area. center() finds centroid. @> tests point containment. Useful for geofencing, coverage analysis, territorial boundaries.

8.8.7 Circles

CIRCLE type represents circle with center point and radius. Storage: 24 bytes. Input: '<(x,y),r>' or '((x,y),r)'. Efficient for radius-based searches, proximity queries.

🎯 Example: Circle Operations

-- Define delivery radius for stores
CREATE TABLE store_coverage (
    store_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    store_name VARCHAR(50),
    delivery_range CIRCLE
);

INSERT INTO store_coverage (store_name, delivery_range) VALUES
    ('Store Downtown', CIRCLE(POINT(10,10), 5)),
    ('Store Uptown', '<(25,25),7>'),
    ('Store Midtown', CIRCLE '<(40,10),10>');

SELECT 
    store_name,
    delivery_range,
    center(delivery_range) AS store_location,
    radius(delivery_range) AS delivery_radius,
    diameter(delivery_range) AS delivery_diameter,
    area(delivery_range)::NUMERIC(10,2) AS coverage_area,
    delivery_range @> POINT(12,12) AS delivers_to_12_12,
    delivery_range && CIRCLE(POINT(15,15), 3) AS overlaps_with_test_circle
FROM store_coverage;
Result:
 store_name       |  delivery_range  | store_location | delivery_radius | delivery_diameter | coverage_area | delivers_to_12_12 | overlaps_with_test_circle
------------------+------------------+----------------+-----------------+-------------------+---------------+-------------------+---------------------------
 Store Downtown   | <(10,10),5>      | (10,10)        |               5 |                10 |         78.54 | t                 | t
 Store Uptown     | <(25,25),7>      | (25,25)        |               7 |                14 |        153.94 | f                 | f
 Store Midtown    | <(40,10),10>     | (40,10)        |              10 |                20 |        314.16 | f                 | f
(3 rows)
Explanation: CIRCLE stores center and radius. center() extracts center point. radius() and diameter() return dimensions. area() calculates circle area (πr²). @> tests if point inside circle. && tests circle overlap. Perfect for "find stores within X miles" queries with GiST indexes.

8.9 Network Address Types

📖 About Network Address Types

Network address types store IPv4, IPv6, and MAC addresses with built-in validation. Types: INET (host/network), CIDR (network specification), MACADDR (6-byte MAC), MACADDR8 (8-byte MAC/EUI-64). Advantages over text storage: automatic validation, subnet operations, indexing, sorting.

Use Cases: Logging IP addresses (web servers, security), network management (device inventory), access control (IP whitelists), MAC address tracking (hardware registration). PostgreSQL validates format on input, rejects invalid addresses.

8.9.1 inet

INET type stores IPv4 or IPv6 host address with optional netmask. Format: 'address' or 'address/prefix_bits'. Examples: '192.168.1.5', '2001:db8::8a2e:370:7334', '192.168.1.0/24'. Storage: 7-19 bytes (IPv4) or 19-43 bytes (IPv6). Supports host operations, subnet calculations, containment operators.

🎯 Example: INET Operations

-- Track user login IP addresses
CREATE TABLE user_logins (
    login_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id INTEGER,
    ip_address INET,
    login_time TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

INSERT INTO user_logins (user_id, ip_address) VALUES
    (1, '192.168.1.100'),
    (1, '192.168.1.101'),
    (2, '10.0.0.50'),
    (3, '2001:db8::1'),
    (1, '192.168.2.200');

-- Analyze login patterns and subnet matching
SELECT 
    user_id,
    ip_address,
    host(ip_address) AS host_only,
    family(ip_address) AS ip_version,
    ip_address << '192.168.1.0/24'::inet AS in_subnet_192_168_1,
    netmask(ip_address) AS default_netmask
FROM user_logins
WHERE user_id = 1
ORDER BY login_id;
Result:
 user_id |   ip_address    |    host_only    | ip_version | in_subnet_192_168_1 | default_netmask
---------+-----------------+-----------------+------------+---------------------+------------------
       1 | 192.168.1.100   | 192.168.1.100   |          4 | t                   | 255.255.255.255
       1 | 192.168.1.101   | 192.168.1.101   |          4 | t                   | 255.255.255.255
       1 | 192.168.2.200   | 192.168.2.200   |          4 | f                   | 255.255.255.255
(3 rows)
Explanation: INET stores IP addresses with validation. host() extracts IP without netmask. family() returns 4 (IPv4) or 6 (IPv6). << operator tests subnet containment (is left address within right network?). Useful for access control, geolocation, security logging.

8.9.2 cidr

CIDR type stores network specification (not host). Requires prefix length. Format: 'network_address/prefix_bits'. Example: '192.168.1.0/24' (valid), '192.168.1.5/24' (rejected - host bits must be zero). Storage: same as INET. Use CIDR for network definitions, INET for hosts.

🎯 Example: CIDR Network Management

-- Define network segments
CREATE TABLE network_segments (
    segment_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    segment_name VARCHAR(50),
    network CIDR,
    description TEXT
);

INSERT INTO network_segments (segment_name, network, description) VALUES
    ('Office LAN', '192.168.1.0/24', 'Main office network'),
    ('Guest WiFi', '10.20.0.0/16', 'Guest wireless access'),
    ('VPN Range', '172.16.0.0/12', 'Remote VPN connections'),
    ('IPv6 Block', '2001:db8::/32', 'IPv6 allocation');

-- Analyze network sizes and check IP membership
SELECT 
    segment_name,
    network,
    masklen(network) AS prefix_length,
    broadcast(network) AS broadcast_addr,
    hostmask(network) AS host_mask,
    '192.168.1.50'::inet << network AS contains_192_168_1_50,
    '10.20.5.100'::inet << network AS contains_10_20_5_100
FROM network_segments
ORDER BY segment_id;
Result:
 segment_name |     network      | prefix_length |  broadcast_addr   |     host_mask      | contains_192_168_1_50 | contains_10_20_5_100
--------------+------------------+---------------+-------------------+--------------------+-----------------------+----------------------
 Office LAN   | 192.168.1.0/24   |            24 | 192.168.1.255     | 0.0.0.255          | t                     | f
 Guest WiFi   | 10.20.0.0/16     |            16 | 10.20.255.255     | 0.0.255.255        | f                     | t
 VPN Range    | 172.16.0.0/12    |            12 | 172.31.255.255    | 0.15.255.255       | f                     | f
 IPv6 Block   | 2001:db8::/32    |            32 | 2001:db8:ffff:... | ::ffff:ffff:...    | f                     | f
(4 rows)
Explanation: CIDR validates network addresses (host bits must be 0). masklen() returns prefix length. broadcast() calculates broadcast address. hostmask() shows host portion mask. << tests if IP within network. Useful for network planning, DHCP management, routing tables.

8.9.3 inet vs. cidr

INET vs CIDR differences: INET accepts any host address with optional netmask ('192.168.1.5/24' OK). CIDR requires network address with host bits zero ('192.168.1.0/24' OK, '192.168.1.5/24' rejected). INET for logging actual hosts, CIDR for defining networks/subnets. Both support same operators, but CIDR enforces network specification rules.

🎯 Example: INET vs CIDR Comparison

-- Demonstrate difference
CREATE TABLE address_test (
    test_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    inet_column INET,
    cidr_column CIDR
);

-- These inserts show the difference
INSERT INTO address_test (inet_column, cidr_column) VALUES
    ('192.168.1.5/24', '192.168.1.0/24'),  -- INET allows host, CIDR needs network
    ('10.0.0.100', '10.0.0.0/8');

-- This would FAIL (uncomment to test):
-- INSERT INTO address_test (cidr_column) VALUES ('192.168.1.5/24');
-- ERROR: invalid cidr value: "192.168.1.5/24" - has bits set to right of mask

SELECT 
    inet_column,
    cidr_column,
    host(inet_column) AS inet_host,
    network(inet_column) AS inet_network,
    broadcast(cidr_column) AS cidr_broadcast,
    inet_column << cidr_column AS inet_in_cidr_network
FROM address_test;
Result:
   inet_column    |  cidr_column   |   inet_host   |  inet_network   | cidr_broadcast  | inet_in_cidr_network
------------------+----------------+---------------+-----------------+-----------------+----------------------
 192.168.1.5/24   | 192.168.1.0/24 | 192.168.1.5   | 192.168.1.0/24  | 192.168.1.255   | t
 10.0.0.100       | 10.0.0.0/8     | 10.0.0.100    | 10.0.0.100/32   | 10.255.255.255  | t
(2 rows)
Explanation: INET stores host addresses (can have host bits set). CIDR enforces network specification (host bits must be 0). network() function converts INET to CIDR representation. Use INET for actual device IPs, CIDR for subnet definitions, ACLs, routing rules.

8.9.4 macaddr

MACADDR type stores 6-byte MAC (Media Access Control) addresses. Format: 'XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX', 'XX-XX-XX-XX-XX-XX', 'XXXX.XXXX.XXXX'. Storage: 6 bytes. Used for network device tracking, hardware inventory, MAC-based authentication.

🎯 Example: MACADDR Device Tracking

-- Track network devices
CREATE TABLE network_devices (
    device_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    device_name VARCHAR(100),
    mac_address MACADDR,
    ip_address INET,
    device_type VARCHAR(50),
    registered_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

INSERT INTO network_devices (device_name, mac_address, ip_address, device_type) VALUES
    ('Server-01', '00:1B:44:11:3A:B7', '192.168.1.10', 'Server'),
    ('Laptop-Alice', '08-00-27-DC-C7-A6', '192.168.1.101', 'Laptop'),
    ('Printer-Floor2', '0800.27ab.cd12', '192.168.1.200', 'Printer'),
    ('Router-Main', 'AC:22:0B:12:34:56', '192.168.1.1', 'Router');

SELECT 
    device_name,
    mac_address,
    ip_address,
    device_type,
    trunc(mac_address) AS manufacturer_oui,
    mac_address - '00:00:00:00:00:01'::macaddr AS relative_offset
FROM network_devices
ORDER BY mac_address;
Result:
 device_name   |    mac_address    |   ip_address   | device_type |  manufacturer_oui | relative_offset
---------------+-------------------+----------------+-------------+-------------------+-----------------
 Server-01     | 00:1b:44:11:3a:b7 | 192.168.1.10   | Server      | 00:1b:44:00:00:00 |   1839328091830
 Laptop-Alice  | 08:00:27:dc:c7:a6 | 192.168.1.101  | Laptop      | 08:00:27:00:00:00 |   8796630171557
 Printer-Floor2| 08:00:27:ab:cd:12 | 192.168.1.200  | Printer     | 08:00:27:00:00:00 |   8796627024145
 Router-Main   | ac:22:0b:12:34:56 | 192.168.1.1    | Router      | ac:22:0b:00:00:00 | 189151848097877
(4 rows)
Explanation: MACADDR validates and stores MAC addresses in standard format. trunc() extracts manufacturer OUI (first 3 bytes). Arithmetic operators (-, +) work on MAC addresses. Useful for device inventory, MAC filtering, network access control (NAC).

8.9.5 macaddr8

MACADDR8 type stores 8-byte MAC addresses (EUI-64 format). Format: 'XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX'. Storage: 8 bytes. Used for IPv6 interface identifiers, modern network hardware with 64-bit addresses. Can cast 6-byte MACADDR to 8-byte MACADDR8 (inserts FF:FE in middle).

🎯 Example: MACADDR8 and EUI-64

-- Modern device registry with 8-byte MACs
CREATE TABLE modern_devices (
    device_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    device_name VARCHAR(100),
    mac64_address MACADDR8,
    ipv6_address INET
);

-- Insert native 8-byte MACs and converted 6-byte MACs
INSERT INTO modern_devices (device_name, mac64_address, ipv6_address) VALUES
    ('Device-A', '08:00:27:00:00:00:12:34', '2001:db8::a'),
    ('Device-B', macaddr8('AC:22:0B:12:34:56'), '2001:db8::b');  -- Converts 6-byte to 8-byte

SELECT 
    device_name,
    mac64_address,
    ipv6_address,
    set_masklen(host(ipv6_address)::inet, 64) AS ipv6_prefix
FROM modern_devices;
Result:
 device_name |      mac64_address        | ipv6_address |    ipv6_prefix
-------------+---------------------------+--------------+--------------------
 Device-A    | 08:00:27:00:00:00:12:34   | 2001:db8::a  | 2001:db8::a/64
 Device-B    | ac:22:0b:ff:fe:12:34:56   | 2001:db8::b  | 2001:db8::b/64
(2 rows)
Explanation: MACADDR8 stores 8-byte MAC addresses for modern hardware. macaddr8() function converts 6-byte MAC to 8-byte by inserting FF:FE (EUI-64 conversion). IPv6 link-local addresses often derived from EUI-64 MACs. Useful for IPv6 deployments, IoT devices with 64-bit identifiers.

8.10 Bit String Types

📖 About Bit String Types

Bit string types store strings of 1s and 0s. Two types: BIT(n) (fixed-length) and BIT VARYING(n) or VARBIT(n) (variable-length up to n bits). Input: B'101010' or X'2A' (hex). Useful for flags, permissions, compact binary data, bitmap indexes.

Operations: Bitwise AND (&), OR (|), XOR (#), NOT (~), shift (<<, >>), concatenation (||). Limited use cases compared to integer bit operations, but explicit bit-level storage when needed.

🎯 Example 1: User Permissions with Bit Strings

-- Store user permissions as bit flags
CREATE TABLE user_permissions (
    user_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    username VARCHAR(50),
    permissions BIT(8)  -- 8 permission flags
);

-- Permission bits: Read(0), Write(1), Delete(2), Admin(3), Export(4), Import(5), Share(6), Archive(7)
INSERT INTO user_permissions (username, permissions) VALUES
    ('alice', B'11110000'),   -- Read, Write, Delete, Admin
    ('bob', B'11000000'),     -- Read, Write only
    ('charlie', B'10100000'); -- Read, Delete only

-- Check specific permissions using bitwise operations
SELECT 
    username,
    permissions,
    get_bit(permissions, 0) = 1 AS has_read,
    get_bit(permissions, 1) = 1 AS has_write,
    get_bit(permissions, 3) = 1 AS has_admin,
    permissions & B'10000000' = B'10000000' AS read_check_bitwise
FROM user_permissions;
Result:
 username | permissions | has_read | has_write | has_admin | read_check_bitwise
----------+-------------+----------+-----------+-----------+--------------------
 alice    | 11110000    | t        | t         | t         | t
 bob      | 11000000    | t        | t         | f         | t
 charlie  | 10100000    | t        | f         | f         | t
(3 rows)
Explanation: BIT(n) stores fixed-length bit strings. get_bit(bits, position) extracts individual bit (0-indexed from left). & operator performs bitwise AND for permission checking. Compact storage for multiple boolean flags.

🎯 Example 2: Feature Flags with VARBIT

-- Variable-length feature flags
CREATE TABLE product_features (
    product_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name VARCHAR(50),
    enabled_features VARBIT(20)
);

INSERT INTO product_features (product_name, enabled_features) VALUES
    ('Basic Plan', B'10000'),
    ('Pro Plan', B'11111000'),
    ('Enterprise', B'11111111111');

SELECT 
    product_name,
    enabled_features,
    length(enabled_features) AS feature_count,
    enabled_features || B'01' AS add_features
FROM product_features;
Result:
 product_name | enabled_features | feature_count | add_features
--------------+------------------+---------------+--------------
 Basic Plan   | 10000            |             5 | 1000001
 Pro Plan     | 11111000         |             8 | 1111100001
 Enterprise   | 11111111111      |            11 | 1111111111101
(3 rows)
Explanation: VARBIT allows variable-length bit strings. length() returns number of bits. || concatenates bit strings. More flexible than fixed BIT(n) when bit count varies.

8.11 Text Search Types

📖 About Text Search Types

Text search types enable full-text search capabilities. Two types: tsvector (searchable document) and tsquery (search query). tsvector stores normalized lexemes with positions. tsquery stores search terms with operators (&, |, !, <>). Used with @@ match operator, GIN/GiST indexes for fast searching.

8.11.1 tsvector

tsvector type represents document in optimized form for text searching. Stores sorted list of distinct lexemes (normalized words). Created using to_tsvector() function with language configuration (e.g., 'english'). Removes stop words, applies stemming (running → run).

🎯 Example: Product Search with tsvector

-- Create products with searchable descriptions
CREATE TABLE products (
    product_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name VARCHAR(100),
    description TEXT,
    search_vector tsvector
);

INSERT INTO products (product_name, description) VALUES
    ('Wireless Mouse', 'Ergonomic wireless mouse with precision tracking'),
    ('Mechanical Keyboard', 'RGB mechanical keyboard for gaming and typing'),
    ('USB Cable', 'Durable USB-C charging cable');

-- Generate tsvector from text
UPDATE products 
SET search_vector = to_tsvector('english', product_name || ' ' || description);

SELECT 
    product_name,
    search_vector,
    to_tsvector('english', 'Running shoes for runners') AS example_stemming
FROM products
WHERE product_id = 1;
Result:
 product_name    |                    search_vector                        |     example_stemming
-----------------+---------------------------------------------------------+---------------------------
 Wireless Mouse  | 'ergonomic':1 'mous':3,5 'precis':7 'track':8 'wireless':2,4 | 'runner':4 'run':1 'shoe':2
(1 row)
Explanation: to_tsvector() converts text to searchable format. Applies stemming (mouse/mice → mous, running → run). Removes stop words (a, the, for). Numbers indicate word positions. Optimized for fast full-text search with GIN indexes.

8.11.2 tsquery

tsquery type represents text search query with operators. Created using to_tsquery() or plainto_tsquery(). Operators: & (AND), | (OR), ! (NOT), <-> (followed by). Use @@ operator to match tsvector against tsquery.

🎯 Example: Full-Text Search Queries

-- Search products using tsquery
SELECT 
    product_name,
    description,
    search_vector @@ to_tsquery('english', 'wireless & mouse') AS matches_wireless_and_mouse,
    search_vector @@ to_tsquery('english', 'keyboard | cable') AS matches_keyboard_or_cable,
    search_vector @@ plainto_tsquery('english', 'mechanical gaming') AS matches_plain_query,
    ts_rank(search_vector, to_tsquery('english', 'wireless')) AS relevance_score
FROM products
ORDER BY ts_rank(search_vector, to_tsquery('english', 'wireless')) DESC;
Result:
 product_name         | description                                     | matches_wireless_and_mouse | matches_keyboard_or_cable | matches_plain_query | relevance_score
----------------------+-------------------------------------------------+----------------------------+---------------------------+---------------------+-----------------
 Wireless Mouse       | Ergonomic wireless mouse with precision tracking| t                          | f                         | f                   | 0.0607927
 Mechanical Keyboard  | RGB mechanical keyboard for gaming and typing   | f                          | t                         | t                   | 0
 USB Cable            | Durable USB-C charging cable                    | f                          | t                         | f                   | 0
(3 rows)
Explanation: @@ operator matches tsvector against tsquery. to_tsquery() requires operator syntax. plainto_tsquery() accepts plain text (auto-adds & operators). ts_rank() calculates relevance score. Essential for building search engines, document retrieval, content discovery.

8.12 UUID Type

📖 About UUID Type

UUID (Universally Unique Identifier) stores 128-bit identifiers per RFC 4122. Format: 'a0eebc99-9c0b-4ef8-bb6d-6bb9bd380a11'. Storage: 16 bytes. Advantages: globally unique without central coordination, mergeable databases, distributed systems, public IDs (non-sequential). Generate with gen_random_uuid() (requires pgcrypto extension or built-in PostgreSQL 13+).

Use Cases: Primary keys in distributed systems, API tokens, session IDs, public identifiers (prevents enumeration attacks). Trade-off: larger than SERIAL/BIGSERIAL (16 vs 4/8 bytes), random UUIDs reduce index locality.

🎯 Example 1: UUID Primary Keys

-- Create table with UUID primary key
CREATE TABLE api_keys (
    key_id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    user_id INTEGER,
    key_name VARCHAR(100),
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    expires_at TIMESTAMP
);

INSERT INTO api_keys (user_id, key_name, expires_at) VALUES
    (1, 'Production API Key', CURRENT_TIMESTAMP + INTERVAL '1 year'),
    (2, 'Development Key', CURRENT_TIMESTAMP + INTERVAL '90 days'),
    (1, 'Mobile App Key', CURRENT_TIMESTAMP + INTERVAL '2 years');

SELECT 
    key_id,
    user_id,
    key_name,
    created_at,
    expires_at
FROM api_keys
ORDER BY created_at;
Result:
                key_id                | user_id |      key_name       |       created_at        |       expires_at
--------------------------------------+---------+---------------------+-------------------------+-------------------------
 a3f12b8c-7d4e-4f2a-9b1c-8e6d5a4c3b2a |       1 | Production API Key  | 2024-01-15 10:30:00     | 2025-01-15 10:30:00
 b7e45d9a-3c2f-4b8e-a1d6-7c9e2f4b5a8d |       2 | Development Key     | 2024-01-15 10:30:05     | 2024-04-14 10:30:05
 c9d23e7b-4a1c-4d8f-b2e9-6a7c3e5d4f1b |       1 | Mobile App Key      | 2024-01-15 10:30:10     | 2026-01-15 10:30:10
(3 rows)
Explanation: gen_random_uuid() generates version 4 (random) UUIDs automatically. UUID primary keys prevent ID enumeration (security benefit). Globally unique across databases (facilitates database merging, replication, distributed systems). Non-sequential (prevents exposing creation order).

🎯 Example 2: UUID vs SERIAL Comparison

-- Compare UUID and SERIAL characteristics
CREATE TABLE comparison_test (
    uuid_col UUID DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    serial_col SERIAL
);

INSERT INTO comparison_test DEFAULT VALUES;
INSERT INTO comparison_test DEFAULT VALUES;
INSERT INTO comparison_test DEFAULT VALUES;

SELECT 
    uuid_col,
    serial_col,
    pg_column_size(uuid_col) AS uuid_bytes,
    pg_column_size(serial_col) AS serial_bytes
FROM comparison_test;
Result:
              uuid_col                | serial_col | uuid_bytes | serial_bytes
--------------------------------------+------------+------------+--------------
 d4e8a7b3-9c2f-4e6d-a1b5-7c3e9f2d4a8b |          1 |         16 |            4
 e7f2b9d4-3a8c-4f1e-b6d9-2c7e4a5f3b1d |          2 |         16 |            4
 f3a8c1e7-4d2b-4a9f-c5e3-8b6d7f4e2a9c |          3 |         16 |            4
(3 rows)
Explanation: UUID storage: 16 bytes vs SERIAL: 4 bytes (BIGSERIAL: 8 bytes). UUID advantages: globally unique, unpredictable, distributed-friendly. SERIAL advantages: smaller storage, better index locality (sequential), human-readable. Choose UUID for distributed systems, public APIs. Choose SERIAL for single-database internal IDs.

8.13 XML Type

📖 About XML Type

XML type stores well-formed XML documents with validation. PostgreSQL checks XML syntax on input. Supports XPath queries, XSLT transformations, XML generation functions. Storage: variable (TOAST-able for large documents). Use for configuration files, SOAP APIs, document storage, data exchange.

8.13.1 Creating XML Values

Creating XML: xmlparse() for parsing strings, xmlelement() for building elements, xmlforest() for multiple elements, xmlconcat() for concatenation. PostgreSQL validates XML structure automatically.

🎯 Example: Creating and Storing XML

-- Store product catalog as XML
CREATE TABLE xml_products (
    product_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_data XML
);

INSERT INTO xml_products (product_data) VALUES
    (XMLPARSE(DOCUMENT '1Laptop999.99')),
    (xmlelement(name product,
        xmlelement(name id, 2),
        xmlelement(name name, 'Mouse'),
        xmlelement(name price, 29.99)
    ));

SELECT 
    product_id,
    product_data,
    xpath('/product/name/text()', product_data) AS product_name,
    xpath('/product/price/text()', product_data) AS price
FROM xml_products;
Result:
 product_id |                         product_data                           | product_name | price
------------+----------------------------------------------------------------+--------------+---------
          1 | 1Laptop999.99... | {Laptop}     | {999.99}
          2 | 2Mouse29.99...  | {Mouse}      | {29.99}
(2 rows)
Explanation: XMLPARSE() converts string to XML with validation. xmlelement() constructs XML elements programmatically. xpath() extracts data using XPath expressions. Returns array of XML values.

8.13.2 Encoding Handling

XML encoding: PostgreSQL stores XML in database encoding (typically UTF-8). Converts between client encoding and database encoding automatically. XML declaration encoding attribute handled during parsing/serialization.

8.13.3 Accessing XML Values

Accessing XML data: xpath() for XPath queries, xpath_exists() for existence checks, xml_is_well_formed() for validation, xmltable() for converting XML to rows.

🎯 Example: Querying XML Data

-- Query XML documents with XPath
SELECT 
    product_id,
    (xpath('/product/name/text()', product_data))[1]::text AS name,
    (xpath('/product/price/text()', product_data))[1]::text::numeric AS price,
    xpath_exists('/product/price[text() > 100]', product_data) AS is_expensive,
    xml_is_well_formed_document(product_data) AS is_valid
FROM xml_products;
Result:
 product_id |  name  |  price  | is_expensive | is_valid
------------+--------+---------+--------------+----------
          1 | Laptop | 999.99  | t            | t
          2 | Mouse  |  29.99  | f            | t
(2 rows)
Explanation: xpath() returns array; [1] extracts first element. xpath_exists() checks condition without returning data (efficient). ::text::numeric casts XML text to numeric. xml_is_well_formed_document() validates XML structure.

8.14 JSON Types

📖 About JSON Types

JSON types store JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) data. Two types: JSON (text storage, validates syntax) and JSONB (binary storage, decomposed, indexed). JSONB recommended for most cases: faster processing, indexing support (GIN), no whitespace preservation. JSON preserves original formatting.

Use Cases: API responses, configuration, flexible schemas, document storage, logging, NoSQL-like data. Rich operator set: -> for extraction, ->> for text, @> for containment, ? for key existence.

8.14.1 JSON Input and Output Syntax

JSON input: String literals containing valid JSON. PostgreSQL validates JSON syntax on input. Single quotes for string literal: '{"key": "value"}'. JSONB parses and stores in decomposed binary format. JSON stores as text with validation.

🎯 Example: JSON vs JSONB Storage

-- Compare JSON and JSONB
CREATE TABLE json_comparison (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    data_json JSON,
    data_jsonb JSONB
);

INSERT INTO json_comparison (data_json, data_jsonb) VALUES
    ('{"name": "Alice", "age": 30, "tags": ["admin", "user"]}',
     '{"name": "Alice", "age": 30, "tags": ["admin", "user"]}'),
    ('{"product":  "Laptop",   "price": 999.99}',  -- Extra spaces
     '{"product":  "Laptop",   "price": 999.99}');

SELECT 
    data_json,
    data_jsonb,
    pg_column_size(data_json) AS json_bytes,
    pg_column_size(data_jsonb) AS jsonb_bytes
FROM json_comparison;
Result:
                  data_json                        |                data_jsonb                    | json_bytes | jsonb_bytes
---------------------------------------------------+----------------------------------------------+------------+-------------
 {"name": "Alice", "age": 30, "tags": ["admin"...] | {"age": 30, "name": "Alice", "tags": ["a... |         57 |          95
 {"product":  "Laptop",   "price": 999.99}         | {"price": 999.99, "product": "Laptop"}       |         47 |          58
(2 rows)
Explanation: JSON preserves whitespace and key order. JSONB removes whitespace, reorders keys (alphabetically), stores binary format. JSONB larger due to decomposition overhead, but faster for queries. Use JSONB for querying/indexing, JSON for exact format preservation.

8.14.2 Designing JSON Documents

JSON document design: Balance between normalization (relational) and denormalization (document-oriented). Consider: query patterns, update frequency, data relationships, schema flexibility needs. Nested objects for closely related data. Arrays for collections. Keep documents reasonably sized (avoid huge arrays).

🎯 Example: Well-Designed JSON Documents

-- Good design: Structured user profiles with reasonable nesting
CREATE TABLE well_designed_profiles (
    user_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    username VARCHAR(50),
    profile JSONB
);

INSERT INTO well_designed_profiles (username, profile) VALUES
    ('alice', '{
        "personal": {"firstName": "Alice", "lastName": "Johnson", "email": "alice@example.com"},
        "settings": {"theme": "dark", "language": "en", "notifications": true},
        "metadata": {"created": "2024-01-01", "lastLogin": "2024-01-26", "loginCount": 42}
    }'),
    ('bob', '{
        "personal": {"firstName": "Bob", "lastName": "Smith", "email": "bob@example.com"},
        "settings": {"theme": "light", "language": "es", "notifications": false},
        "metadata": {"created": "2024-01-15", "lastLogin": "2024-01-25", "loginCount": 15}
    }');

-- Query nested structure efficiently
SELECT 
    username,
    profile->'personal'->>'firstName' AS first_name,
    profile->'settings'->>'theme' AS theme,
    (profile->'metadata'->>'loginCount')::INTEGER AS logins
FROM well_designed_profiles
WHERE profile->'settings'->>'language' = 'en';
Result:
 username | first_name | theme | logins
----------+------------+-------+--------
 alice    | Alice      | dark  |     42
(1 row)
Explanation: Organized JSON with logical grouping (personal, settings, metadata). Predictable structure aids queries. Balance: don't over-nest (hard to query), don't flatten everything (lose relationships). Design for common query patterns.

8.14.3 jsonb Containment and Existence

Containment operators: @> (contains), <@ (is contained by), ? (key exists), ?| (any key exists), ?& (all keys exist). Essential for querying JSON documents. GIN indexes accelerate these operations dramatically.

🎯 Example: JSON Operators and Extraction

-- Store user profiles with flexible schema
CREATE TABLE user_profiles (
    user_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    username VARCHAR(50),
    profile_data JSONB
);

INSERT INTO user_profiles (username, profile_data) VALUES
    ('alice', '{"email": "alice@example.com", "age": 30, "preferences": {"theme": "dark", "notifications": true}}'),
    ('bob', '{"email": "bob@example.com", "age": 25, "preferences": {"theme": "light"}}'),
    ('charlie', '{"email": "charlie@example.com", "skills": ["Python", "PostgreSQL", "Docker"]}');

-- Extract nested data with JSON operators
SELECT 
    username,
    profile_data->'email' AS email_json,
    profile_data->>'email' AS email_text,
    profile_data->'preferences'->>'theme' AS theme,
    profile_data->'skills'->0 AS first_skill,
    profile_data ? 'skills' AS has_skills,
    profile_data @> '{"age": 30}' AS is_age_30
FROM user_profiles;
Result:
 username |     email_json        |     email_text      | theme | first_skill | has_skills | is_age_30
----------+-----------------------+---------------------+-------+-------------+------------+-----------
 alice    | "alice@example.com"   | alice@example.com   | dark  | NULL        | f          | t
 bob      | "bob@example.com"     | bob@example.com     | light | NULL        | f          | f
 charlie  | "charlie@example.com" | charlie@example.com | NULL  | "Python"    | t          | f
(3 rows)
Explanation: -> returns JSON (with quotes). ->> returns text (no quotes). Chain operators for nested access. ->0 accesses array element. ? checks key existence. @> checks if left JSON contains right JSON. Essential for flexible schemas.

8.14.4 jsonb Indexing

JSONB indexing: GIN (Generalized Inverted Index) recommended for JSONB. Indexes all keys and values. Supports @>, ?, ?&, ?| operators. GIN index with jsonb_path_ops operator class for containment queries only (smaller, faster). B-tree indexes on expressions: (data->>'field') for specific field queries.

🎯 Example: JSON Aggregation and Construction

-- Aggregate data into JSON
SELECT 
    o.customer_id,
    c.customer_name,
    json_agg(json_build_object(
        'order_id', o.order_id,
        'order_date', o.order_date,
        'total', o.total_amount
    )) AS orders
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
WHERE c.customer_id <= 2
GROUP BY o.customer_id, c.customer_name;
Result:
 customer_id | customer_name |                                    orders
-------------+---------------+---------------------------------------------------------------------------
           1 | John Doe      | [{"order_id": 1, "order_date": "2024-01-01", "total": 1200.00}, ...]
           2 | Jane Smith    | [{"order_id": 2, "order_date": "2024-01-02", "total": 850.00}]
(2 rows)
Explanation: json_build_object() creates JSON object from key-value pairs. json_agg() aggregates rows into JSON array. Useful for API responses, nested data structures, denormalization.

🎯 Example: JSONB Indexing Performance

-- Create GIN index for JSONB queries
CREATE INDEX idx_profile_data ON user_profiles USING GIN (profile_data);

-- Efficient queries with index support
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT username, profile_data->'email' AS email
FROM user_profiles
WHERE profile_data @> '{"age": 30}';

-- Key existence query (also uses index)
SELECT username, profile_data->'preferences'->>'theme' AS theme
FROM user_profiles
WHERE profile_data ? 'preferences';
Result:
                                QUERY PLAN
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Bitmap Heap Scan on user_profiles  (cost=12.00..16.01 rows=1 ...)
   Recheck Cond: (profile_data @> '{"age": 30}'::jsonb)
   ->  Bitmap Index Scan on idx_profile_data  (cost=0.00..12.00 ...)
         Index Cond: (profile_data @> '{"age": 30}'::jsonb)
(4 rows)
Explanation: GIN (Generalized Inverted Index) indexes JSONB efficiently. Supports @>, ?, ?&, ?| operators. Dramatically improves query performance on large JSON datasets. Essential for production JSONB usage.

8.14.5 jsonb Subscripting

JSONB subscripting (PostgreSQL 14+): Array-style subscript notation for JSONB access and updates. Syntax: jsonb_column['key'] or jsonb_column['key']['nested']. Simpler than traditional -> operators for some use cases. Supports assignments in UPDATE statements.

🎯 Example: Subscript Notation

-- Using subscript notation (PostgreSQL 14+)
SELECT 
    username,
    profile_data['email'] AS email_subscript,
    profile_data['preferences']['theme'] AS theme_subscript,
    -- Compare with traditional operators
    profile_data->'email' AS email_operator,
    profile_data->'preferences'->'theme' AS theme_operator
FROM user_profiles
WHERE username = 'alice';

-- Update using subscript notation
UPDATE user_profiles
SET profile_data['preferences']['theme'] = '"auto"'
WHERE username = 'bob';
Result:
 username | email_subscript       | theme_subscript | email_operator        | theme_operator
----------+-----------------------+-----------------+-----------------------+----------------
 alice    | "alice@example.com"   | "dark"          | "alice@example.com"   | "dark"
(1 row)
Explanation: Subscript notation ['key'] is alternative to -> operator. Both return JSON values. More familiar syntax for developers from other languages. Available PostgreSQL 14+. Use whichever style team prefers.

8.14.6 Transforms

JSON Transforms: Convert between SQL types and JSON. json_populate_record() / jsonb_populate_record() convert JSON to table rows. json_to_record() for dynamic record types. json_each() / jsonb_each() expand JSON to key-value pairs. json_array_elements() expands arrays.

🎯 Example: JSON Transforms

-- Transform JSON to rows
SELECT * FROM jsonb_each('{"a": 1, "b": 2, "c": 3}'::jsonb);

-- Expand JSON array
SELECT * FROM jsonb_array_elements('[{"id": 1, "name": "Alice"}, {"id": 2, "name": "Bob"}]'::jsonb);

-- Populate record from JSON
CREATE TYPE person_type AS (name TEXT, age INTEGER, city TEXT);

SELECT * FROM jsonb_populate_record(NULL::person_type, 
    '{"name": "Alice", "age": 30, "city": "Boston"}'::jsonb);
Result:
-- jsonb_each result:
 key | value
-----+-------
 a   | 1
 b   | 2
 c   | 3

-- jsonb_array_elements result:
           value
----------------------------
 {"id": 1, "name": "Alice"}
 {"id": 2, "name": "Bob"}

-- jsonb_populate_record result:
  name  | age |  city
--------+-----+--------
 Alice  |  30 | Boston
Explanation: JSON transform functions convert between JSON and SQL types. jsonb_each() expands object to rows. jsonb_array_elements() expands array to rows. jsonb_populate_record() maps JSON to typed record. Essential for ETL, reporting, integration with relational queries.

8.14.7 jsonpath Type

JSONPath: Query language for JSON (similar to XPath for XML). PostgreSQL supports SQL/JSON path language. Functions: jsonb_path_exists(), jsonb_path_match(), jsonb_path_query(), jsonb_path_query_array(). Path expressions: $.key, $.array[*], $.**.nested (recursive), filters, comparisons.

🎯 Example 1: JSONPath Queries

-- Sample JSON document with nested structure
CREATE TABLE product_catalog (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_data JSONB
);

INSERT INTO product_catalog (product_data) VALUES
('{"name": "Laptop", "price": 999, "specs": {"cpu": "i7", "ram": 16}, "reviews": [{"rating": 5}, {"rating": 4}]}'),
('{"name": "Mouse", "price": 25, "specs": {"wireless": true}, "reviews": [{"rating": 4}, {"rating": 3}]}');

-- JSONPath queries
-- Simple path: $.key
SELECT jsonb_path_query(product_data, '$.name') AS product_name
FROM product_catalog;

-- Nested path
SELECT jsonb_path_query(product_data, '$.specs.cpu') AS cpu
FROM product_catalog WHERE jsonb_path_exists(product_data, '$.specs.cpu');

-- Array access with filter
SELECT product_data->>'name' AS product, 
       jsonb_path_query_array(product_data, '$.reviews[*].rating') AS all_ratings
FROM product_catalog;

-- Filter expression: find products with price < 100
SELECT product_data->>'name' AS affordable_products
FROM product_catalog
WHERE jsonb_path_exists(product_data, '$ ? (@.price < 100)');
Result:
-- Simple path result:
 product_name
--------------
 "Laptop"
 "Mouse"

-- Nested path result:
  cpu
------
 "i7"

-- Array access result:
 product |  all_ratings
---------+---------------
 Laptop  | [5, 4]
 Mouse   | [4, 3]

-- Filter result:
 affordable_products
---------------------
 Mouse
Explanation: JSONPath provides powerful querying with SQL/JSON path language. $.key accesses properties, [*] iterates arrays, ? (@.condition) filters. jsonb_path_query() returns first match, jsonb_path_query_array() returns all matches. jsonb_path_exists() checks path existence. More flexible than traditional operators for complex queries.

🎯 Example 2: Recursive JSONPath

-- Complex nested JSON structure
INSERT INTO product_catalog (product_data) VALUES
('{
    "category": "Electronics",
    "items": [
        {"name": "Phone", "details": {"price": 699}},
        {"name": "Charger", "details": {"price": 29}}
    ],
    "warehouse": {"location": {"city": "Boston", "prices": [599, 799]}}
}'::jsonb);

-- Recursive descent operator **: find all "price" keys at any level
SELECT jsonb_path_query_array(product_data, '$.**{price}') AS all_prices
FROM product_catalog
WHERE product_data->>'category' = 'Electronics';

-- Find all numeric values anywhere in structure
SELECT jsonb_path_query_array(product_data, 'strict $.** ? (@.type() == "number")') AS all_numbers
FROM product_catalog
WHERE product_data->>'category' = 'Electronics';
Result:
 all_prices
--------------
 [699, 29]

 all_numbers
---------------------
 [699, 29, 599, 799]
Explanation: ** (recursive descent) searches all levels of JSON structure. $.**{price} finds all objects with "price" key regardless of nesting. Type filters like @.type() == "number" select by type. Powerful for heterogeneous or deeply nested JSON when exact path unknown.

🎯 Example 3: JSONPath Array Operations

-- Working with arrays in JSONPath
SELECT 
    product_data->>'name' AS product,
    -- Get first review
    jsonb_path_query(product_data, '$.reviews[0]') AS first_review,
    -- Get last review
    jsonb_path_query(product_data, '$.reviews[last]') AS last_review,
    -- Get reviews with rating >= 4
    jsonb_path_query_array(product_data, '$.reviews[*] ? (@.rating >= 4)') AS good_reviews
FROM product_catalog
WHERE product_data ? 'reviews';
Result:
 product |   first_review   |   last_review    |      good_reviews
---------+------------------+------------------+------------------------
 Laptop  | {"rating": 5}    | {"rating": 4}    | [{"rating": 5}, {"rating": 4}]
 Mouse   | {"rating": 4}    | {"rating": 3}    | [{"rating": 4}]
(2 rows)
Explanation: JSONPath supports array indexing [0], [last], and filtering [*] ? (condition). Filters use @ for current element. Can combine multiple conditions with && and ||. Essential for querying arrays within JSON documents.

🎯 Example 4: JSONPath Comparison Operators

-- Using comparison operators in JSONPath
SELECT 
    product_data->>'name' AS product,
    product_data->>'price' AS price
FROM product_catalog
WHERE jsonb_path_exists(product_data, '$ ? (@.price >= 500 && @.price <= 1000)');

-- Find products with all reviews >= 4
SELECT product_data->>'name' AS highly_rated
FROM product_catalog
WHERE jsonb_path_exists(product_data, '$ ? (@.reviews[*].rating >= 4)');

-- Check for null values
SELECT product_data->>'name' AS product
FROM product_catalog
WHERE jsonb_path_exists(product_data, '$.specs.cpu ? (@ != null)');
Result:
-- Price range result:
 product | price
---------+-------
 Laptop  | 999
(1 row)

-- Highly rated result:
 highly_rated
--------------
(0 rows)

-- Has CPU result:
 product
---------
 Laptop
(1 row)
Explanation: JSONPath supports comparison operators: ==, !=, <, <=, >, >=. Combine with && (and), || (or). Use @ for current item in filter. Check for null with != null or == null. Powerful for complex filtering within JSON documents.

🎯 Example 5: JSONPath with Regular Expressions

-- Pattern matching in JSONPath
SELECT 
    product_data->>'name' AS product,
    product_data->>'specs' AS specs
FROM product_catalog
WHERE jsonb_path_exists(product_data, '$.name ? (@ like_regex "^L.*" flag "i")');

-- Check if any key matches pattern
SELECT id, product_data
FROM product_catalog
WHERE jsonb_path_exists(product_data, '$.** ? (@.type() == "string" && @ like_regex "wire.*")');
Result:
-- Name pattern result:
 product |              specs
---------+----------------------------------
 Laptop  | {"cpu": "i7", "ram": 16}
(1 row)

-- Contains "wire" result:
 id |                        product_data
----+------------------------------------------------------------
  2 | {"name": "Mouse", "specs": {"wireless": true}, ...}
(1 row)
Explanation: like_regex enables pattern matching in JSONPath. Flags: "i" (case-insensitive), "s" (dot matches newline). Combine with recursive ** to search entire document. Useful for fuzzy matching, validation, complex filtering when exact values unknown. 
-- Modify JSONB data in place
UPDATE user_profiles
SET profile_data = profile_data || '{"verified": true}'
WHERE username = 'alice';

-- Remove key
UPDATE user_profiles
SET profile_data = profile_data - 'age'
WHERE username = 'bob';

-- Set nested value
UPDATE user_profiles
SET profile_data = jsonb_set(profile_data, '{preferences,notifications}', 'false')
WHERE username = 'alice';

SELECT username, profile_data FROM user_profiles WHERE username IN ('alice', 'bob');
Result:
 username |                                          profile_data
----------+------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 alice    | {"age": 30, "email": "alice@example.com", "verified": true, "preferences": {"theme": "dark", "notifications": false}}
 bob      | {"email": "bob@example.com", "preferences": {"theme": "light"}}
(2 rows)
Explanation: || merges JSON objects (right overwrites left for duplicate keys). - removes key. jsonb_set() modifies nested values using path array. Enables schema-less updates without ALTER TABLE. Trade-off: less structure vs more flexibility.

8.15 Arrays

📖 About Arrays

Arrays store multiple values of same type in single column. Any built-in or user-defined type can be array. Declaration: type[] or ARRAY. Useful for tags, phone numbers, email addresses, multi-value attributes. Variable-length arrays, 1-based indexing (unlike most languages). Maximum dimensions: theoretically unlimited, practically limited by storage.

Operations: Array construction, element access, slicing, containment (@>), overlap (&&), concatenation (||), unnesting. GIN indexes support array operations efficiently.

8.15.1 Declaration of Array Types
