Array declaration: column_name type[] or column_name type ARRAY or type[size] (size optional, not enforced). Examples: INTEGER[], TEXT[], NUMERIC(10,2)[][]. Multi-dimensional arrays supported.

🎯 Example: Array Column Declaration

-- Create table with various array types
CREATE TABLE contact_info (
    contact_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(100),
    phone_numbers TEXT[],                    -- Variable-length array
    email_addresses VARCHAR(100) ARRAY,      -- Alternative syntax
    tags TEXT[],
    scores INTEGER[]
);

INSERT INTO contact_info (name, phone_numbers, email_addresses, tags, scores) VALUES
    ('Alice Johnson', 
     ARRAY['555-1234', '555-5678'],
     ARRAY['alice@work.com', 'alice@personal.com'],
     ARRAY['customer', 'premium', 'verified'],
     ARRAY[95, 88, 92]),
    ('Bob Smith',
     '{"555-9999"}',                         -- Array literal syntax
     '{"bob@example.com"}',
     '{"customer"}',
     '{85, 90, 87, 91}');

SELECT * FROM contact_info;
Result:
 contact_id |     name       |    phone_numbers     |          email_addresses             |           tags           |    scores
------------+----------------+----------------------+--------------------------------------+--------------------------+----------------
          1 | Alice Johnson  | {555-1234,555-5678}  | {alice@work.com,alice@personal.com}  | {customer,premium,verified} | {95,88,92}
          2 | Bob Smith      | {555-9999}           | {bob@example.com}                    | {customer}               | {85,90,87,91}
(2 rows)
Explanation: type[] declares array column. ARRAY[...] constructor creates arrays. '{...}' string literal also works. Arrays store multiple values without separate junction table. Denormalization trade-off: simpler structure vs less normalization.

8.15.2 Array Value Input

Array input formats: ARRAY[val1, val2, ...] constructor (recommended, handles escaping). '{val1, val2, ...}' string literal. Elements containing commas, quotes, braces need double quotes. NULL elements: ARRAY[1, NULL, 3] or '{1, NULL, 3}'.

8.15.3 Accessing Arrays

Array access: array[index] for single element (1-based!). array[start:end] for slice. array_length(array, dimension) for length. array_dims(array) for dimension info. cardinality(array) for total element count.

🎯 Example: Accessing Array Elements

-- Access array elements and get array info
SELECT 
    name,
    phone_numbers,
    phone_numbers[1] AS first_phone,           -- 1-based indexing!
    phone_numbers[2] AS second_phone,
    phone_numbers[1:2] AS first_two_phones,    -- Slicing
    array_length(phone_numbers, 1) AS phone_count,
    cardinality(scores) AS score_count,
    array_upper(scores, 1) AS max_index,
    array_lower(scores, 1) AS min_index
FROM contact_info
WHERE contact_id = 1;
Result:
 name          | phone_numbers        | first_phone | second_phone | first_two_phones     | phone_count | score_count | max_index | min_index
---------------+----------------------+-------------+--------------+----------------------+-------------+-------------+-----------+-----------
 Alice Johnson | {555-1234,555-5678}  | 555-1234    | 555-5678     | {555-1234,555-5678}  |           2 |           3 |         3 |         1
(1 row)
Explanation: PostgreSQL arrays use 1-based indexing (unlike C, Python). array[1] returns first element. array[1:2] returns slice (inclusive). array_length() and cardinality() return element count. array_upper()/array_lower() return bounds.

8.15.4 Modifying Arrays

Array modification: array_append(array, element) adds element to end. array_prepend(element, array) adds to start. array_cat(array1, array2) concatenates. array_remove(array, element) removes all occurrences. || operator for concatenation. Update specific index: UPDATE table SET arr[index] = value.

🎯 Example: Modifying Arrays

-- Modify arrays using various functions
UPDATE contact_info
SET tags = array_append(tags, 'vip')
WHERE name = 'Alice Johnson';

UPDATE contact_info
SET tags = array_prepend('active', tags)
WHERE name = 'Bob Smith';

SELECT 
    name,
    tags AS original_tags,
    array_append(tags, 'new_tag') AS with_append,
    array_remove(tags, 'customer') AS without_customer,
    tags || ARRAY['extra1', 'extra2'] AS concatenated,
    array_cat(tags, ARRAY['bulk1', 'bulk2']) AS with_cat
FROM contact_info
WHERE name = 'Alice Johnson';
Result:
 name          | original_tags                      | with_append                            | without_customer        | concatenated                               | with_cat
---------------+------------------------------------+----------------------------------------+-------------------------+--------------------------------------------+-------------------------------------------
 Alice Johnson | {customer,premium,verified,vip}    | {customer,premium,verified,vip,new_tag}| {premium,verified,vip}  | {customer,premium,verified,vip,extra1,extra2} | {customer,premium,verified,vip,bulk1,bulk2}
(1 row)
Explanation: array_append() adds to end. array_prepend() adds to beginning. array_remove() removes all matching elements. || and array_cat() concatenate arrays. These functions return new arrays (functional style) or can update columns directly.

8.15.5 Searching in Arrays

Array search operators: @> (contains), <@ (is contained by), && (overlaps), = (equality), array_position(array, element) finds index, ANY/ALL for element comparisons. GIN indexes accelerate array searches dramatically.

🎯 Example: Searching Arrays

-- Search for contacts using array operators
SELECT 
    name,
    tags,
    tags @> ARRAY['premium'] AS is_premium,
    tags @> ARRAY['vip', 'verified'] AS has_vip_and_verified,
    ARRAY['customer', 'premium'] <@ tags AS subset_check,
    tags && ARRAY['verified', 'admin'] AS has_verified_or_admin,
    array_position(tags, 'customer') AS customer_tag_position,
    'premium' = ANY(tags) AS premium_any_syntax
FROM contact_info;
Result:
 name          |              tags                      | is_premium | has_vip_and_verified | subset_check | has_verified_or_admin | customer_tag_position | premium_any_syntax
---------------+----------------------------------------+------------+----------------------+--------------+-----------------------+-----------------------+--------------------
 Alice Johnson | {customer,premium,verified,vip}        | t          | t                    | t            | t                     |                     1 | t
 Bob Smith     | {active,customer}                      | f          | f                    | f            | f                     |                     2 | f
(2 rows)
Explanation: @> tests if left array contains all elements of right array. <@ tests if left is subset of right. && tests if arrays share any elements. array_position() finds first occurrence (1-based, NULL if not found). ANY checks if value matches any array element. GIN indexes make these operations efficient on large datasets.

8.15.6 Array Input and Output Syntax

Array syntax rules: Output always '{elem1, elem2}' format. Input accepts ARRAY[...] or '{...}'. Elements with special chars (comma, brace, quote, whitespace, NULL) need double quotes. Quote literals: "NULL" (string), NULL (SQL NULL). Escape quotes with backslash: "\"quoted\"". Multi-dimensional: '{{1,2},{3,4}}'.

🎯 Example: Complex Array Syntax

-- Arrays with special characters and NULL handling
CREATE TABLE array_syntax_demo (
    demo_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    simple_array TEXT[],
    with_nulls INTEGER[],
    with_special_chars TEXT[],
    multidimensional INTEGER[][]
);

INSERT INTO array_syntax_demo VALUES
    (1, 
     ARRAY['simple', 'strings'],
     ARRAY[1, NULL, 3],
     ARRAY['text with, comma', 'text with "quotes"', 'NULL as string'],
     ARRAY[[1,2,3], [4,5,6]]);

SELECT 
    simple_array,
    with_nulls,
    with_nulls[2] AS null_element,
    with_special_chars,
    multidimensional,
    multidimensional[1][2] AS element_1_2,
    array_dims(multidimensional) AS dimensions
FROM array_syntax_demo;
Result:
 simple_array    | with_nulls  | null_element |           with_special_chars                        | multidimensional | element_1_2 | dimensions
-----------------+-------------+--------------+-----------------------------------------------------+------------------+-------------+-----------
 {simple,strings}| {1,NULL,3}  | NULL         | {"text with, comma","text with \"quotes\"","NULL... | {{1,2,3},{4,5,6}}|           2 | [1:2][1:3]
(1 row)
Explanation: NULL in array displayed as NULL (not quoted). Special characters automatically escaped in output (quotes doubled). Multi-dimensional arrays: [1][2] accesses row 1, column 2. array_dims() shows '[1:2][1:3]' meaning 2 rows, 3 columns (both 1-based). ARRAY constructor handles escaping automatically (preferred over '{...}' literals).

8.16 Composite Types

📖 About Composite Types

Composite types represent structure of row/record. Essentially custom data types combining multiple fields. Created with CREATE TYPE or automatically for each table. Useful for function return types, nested data structures, complex data modeling. Similar to structs/objects in programming languages.

Key Features: Named fields with types, nested composites allowed, can be stored in tables, used in function parameters/returns. Access fields with dot notation or parentheses syntax. ROW constructor creates composite values.

8.16.1 Declaration of Composite Types

Creating composite types: CREATE TYPE name AS (field1 type1, field2 type2, ...). Each table creation automatically creates composite type with same name. Use composite types for structured data without creating tables.

🎯 Example: Composite Type Declaration

-- Create custom composite types
CREATE TYPE address AS (
    street VARCHAR(100),
    city VARCHAR(50),
    state CHAR(2),
    zip_code VARCHAR(10)
);

CREATE TYPE full_name AS (
    first_name VARCHAR(50),
    middle_name VARCHAR(50),
    last_name VARCHAR(50)
);

-- Use composite types in table
CREATE TABLE employees (
    employee_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    name full_name,
    home_address address,
    work_address address,
    salary NUMERIC(10,2)
);

-- Table creation also creates composite type "employees"
SELECT 
    typname AS type_name,
    typtype AS type_kind
FROM pg_type
WHERE typname IN ('address', 'full_name', 'employees');
Result:
 type_name  | type_kind
------------+-----------
 address    | c
 full_name  | c
 employees  | c
(3 rows)
Explanation: CREATE TYPE AS (...) defines composite type. Each field has name and type. typtype='c' indicates composite. Every table automatically gets corresponding composite type. Can use composite types as column types, function parameters, return types.

8.16.2 Constructing Composite Values

Creating composite values: ROW(val1, val2, ...) constructor. Type casting: ROW(...)::type_name. Parenthesized syntax: '(val1,val2,...)'::type_name. Field order must match type definition. NULL values represented as NULL in field list.

🎯 Example: Composite Value Construction

-- Insert using ROW constructor
INSERT INTO employees (name, home_address, work_address, salary) VALUES
    (ROW('John', 'Q', 'Doe')::full_name,
     ROW('123 Main St', 'Boston', 'MA', '02101')::address,
     ROW('456 Business Ave', 'Boston', 'MA', '02102')::address,
     75000.00),
    (ROW('Jane', NULL, 'Smith'),  -- NULL middle name
     ('789 Oak Lane', 'Cambridge', 'MA', '02139')::address,
     ('456 Business Ave', 'Boston', 'MA', '02102')::address,
     82000.00);

SELECT 
    employee_id,
    name,
    home_address,
    salary
FROM employees;
Result:
 employee_id |       name        |                 home_address                | salary
-------------+-------------------+---------------------------------------------+----------
           1 | (John,Q,Doe)      | ("123 Main St",Boston,MA,02101)             | 75000.00
           2 | (Jane,,Smith)     | ("789 Oak Lane",Cambridge,MA,02139)         | 82000.00
(2 rows)
Explanation: ROW(...) creates composite value. ::type_name casts to specific composite type. Parenthesized '(...)' shorthand also works. NULL fields shown as empty between commas (Jane,,Smith). Composite values stored as single column, but contain multiple fields internally.

8.16.3 Accessing Composite Types

Field access: (column_name).field_name syntax (parentheses required to prevent ambiguity with table.column). Alternative: column_name.field_name when unambiguous. Can chain for nested composites: (address).city.

🎯 Example: Accessing Composite Fields

-- Access individual fields from composite types
SELECT 
    employee_id,
    (name).first_name AS first,           -- Parentheses required
    (name).last_name AS last,
    (home_address).city AS home_city,
    (home_address).state AS home_state,
    (work_address).street AS work_street,
    -- Without parentheses (works in some contexts):
    name.first_name AS alt_first,         -- May be ambiguous
    salary
FROM employees;
Result:
 employee_id | first | last  | home_city  | home_state |   work_street     | alt_first | salary
-------------+-------+-------+------------+------------+-------------------+-----------+----------
           1 | John  | Doe   | Boston     | MA         | 456 Business Ave  | John      | 75000.00
           2 | Jane  | Smith | Cambridge  | MA         | 456 Business Ave  | Jane      | 82000.00
(2 rows)
Explanation: (composite_column).field accesses nested field. Parentheses disambiguate composite_column from table name. Dot notation extracts individual fields. Can use in WHERE clauses, ORDER BY, anywhere expressions allowed.

8.16.4 Modifying Composite Types

Modifying composites: Update entire composite: SET column = ROW(...). Update individual field: SET column = ROW((column).field1, new_value, (column).field3, ...). ALTER TYPE ADD ATTRIBUTE / DROP ATTRIBUTE / ALTER ATTRIBUTE for type definition changes.

🎯 Example: Modifying Composite Values

-- Update entire composite value
UPDATE employees
SET home_address = ROW('999 New St', 'Springfield', 'MA', '01101')::address
WHERE employee_id = 1;

-- Update single field within composite (must reconstruct entire composite)
UPDATE employees
SET home_address = ROW(
    (home_address).street,
    (home_address).city,
    'CA',  -- Change only state
    (home_address).zip_code
)::address
WHERE employee_id = 2;

SELECT employee_id, (home_address).* FROM employees;
Result:
 employee_id |    street      |    city     | state | zip_code
-------------+----------------+-------------+-------+----------
           1 | 999 New St     | Springfield | MA    | 01101
           2 | 789 Oak Lane   | Cambridge   | CA    | 02139
(2 rows)
Explanation: Updating composite requires reconstructing entire value with ROW(). (composite_column).* expands all fields (similar to table.*). Limitation: can't update single field directly - must rebuild entire composite. Consider separate columns if frequent field updates needed.

8.16.5 Using Composite Types in Queries

Composite types in queries: WHERE conditions on fields: WHERE (column).field = value. Comparison operators work on entire composite (field-by-field). Can pass composites to functions, return from functions, join on composite columns.

🎯 Example: Querying with Composites

-- Query using composite fields
SELECT 
    employee_id,
    (name).first_name || ' ' || (name).last_name AS full_name,
    (home_address).city AS city,
    salary
FROM employees
WHERE (home_address).state = 'MA'
  AND (home_address).city IN ('Boston', 'Cambridge')
ORDER BY (name).last_name;

-- Compare entire composites
SELECT 
    e1.employee_id AS emp1,
    e2.employee_id AS emp2,
    e1.work_address AS address
FROM employees e1
JOIN employees e2 ON e1.work_address = e2.work_address
WHERE e1.employee_id < e2.employee_id;
Result:
 employee_id | full_name   | city      | salary
-------------+-------------+-----------+----------
           1 | John Q Doe  | Springfield| 75000.00

 emp1 | emp2 |                   address
------+------+-----------------------------------------------
    1 |    2 | ("456 Business Ave",Boston,MA,02102)
(2 rows)
Explanation: Can filter on composite fields in WHERE clause. Composite equality (=) compares all fields. Useful for finding employees sharing work address. Composite types reduce table proliferation for closely related data.

8.16.6 Composite Type Input and Output Syntax

Composite syntax: Output: (field1,field2,...) format. Fields with special chars quoted. NULL displayed as empty. Input: ROW(...) recommended or '(val1,val2,...)'::type_name. Escaping: double quotes for values with commas, parens, or quotes.

🎯 Example: Complex Composite Syntax

-- Nested composite types
CREATE TYPE contact_info AS (
    email VARCHAR(100),
    phone VARCHAR(20)
);

CREATE TYPE person_detail AS (
    full_name full_name,
    contact contact_info,
    notes TEXT
);

CREATE TABLE detailed_contacts (
    contact_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    details person_detail
);

INSERT INTO detailed_contacts (details) VALUES
    (ROW(
        ROW('Alice', 'M', 'Wonder')::full_name,
        ROW('alice@example.com', '555-0001')::contact_info,
        'Important client with special needs'
    )::person_detail);

SELECT 
    ((details).full_name).first_name AS first,
    ((details).contact).email AS email,
    (details).notes AS notes
FROM detailed_contacts;
Result:
 first |       email        |              notes
-------+--------------------+----------------------------------
 Alice | alice@example.com  | Important client with special...
(1 row)
Explanation: Composite types can be nested. ((column).field1).field2 accesses nested field (multiple levels of parentheses). ROW constructors nest for complex structures. Useful for complex data modeling, but consider JSON for highly nested/variable structures (more flexible).

8.17 Range Types

📖 About Range Types

Range types represent ranges of values: intervals between lower and upper bounds. Built-in types: INT4RANGE, INT8RANGE, NUMRANGE, TSRANGE (timestamp without timezone), TSTZRANGE (timestamp with timezone), DATERANGE. Bounds can be inclusive [x,y] or exclusive (x,y). Empty ranges, infinite ranges supported.

Use Cases: Scheduling (time slots, reservations), versioning (valid date ranges), inventory (price ranges), constraints (check availability). Powerful operators: overlap (&&), containment (@>, <@), adjacency (-|-), union (+), intersection (*), difference (-).

8.17.1 Built-in Range Types

Built-in range types: INT4RANGE (integer), INT8RANGE (bigint), NUMRANGE (numeric), DATERANGE (date), TSRANGE (timestamp without TZ), TSTZRANGE (timestamp with TZ). Also: multiranges (PostgreSQL 14+) for disjoint ranges. Can create custom range types with CREATE TYPE ... AS RANGE.

🎯 Example: Built-in Range Types

-- Demonstrate various range types
CREATE TABLE range_examples (
    example_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    description VARCHAR(100),
    int_range INT4RANGE,
    num_range NUMRANGE,
    date_range DATERANGE,
    timestamp_range TSTZRANGE
);

INSERT INTO range_examples (description, int_range, num_range, date_range, timestamp_range) VALUES
    ('Inclusive range', '[1,10]', '[0.0,100.0]', '[2024-01-01,2024-12-31]', '[2024-01-01 00:00:00+00,2024-01-01 23:59:59+00]'),
    ('Exclusive range', '(1,10)', '(0.0,100.0)', '(2024-01-01,2024-12-31)', '(2024-01-01 00:00:00+00,2024-01-01 23:59:59+00)'),
    ('Half-open range', '[1,10)', '[0.0,100.0)', '[2024-01-01,2024-12-31)', NULL),
    ('Unbounded range', '[1,)', '[0.0,)', '[2024-01-01,)', NULL);

SELECT 
    description,
    int_range,
    lower(int_range) AS int_lower,
    upper(int_range) AS int_upper,
    lower_inc(int_range) AS lower_inclusive,
    upper_inc(int_range) AS upper_inclusive
FROM range_examples;
Result:
 description      | int_range | int_lower | int_upper | lower_inclusive | upper_inclusive
------------------+-----------+-----------+-----------+-----------------+-----------------
 Inclusive range  | [1,11)    |         1 |        11 | t               | f
 Exclusive range  | [2,10)    |         2 |        10 | t               | f
 Half-open range  | [1,10)    |         1 |        10 | t               | f
 Unbounded range  | [1,)      |         1 |           | t               | f
(4 rows)
Explanation: Range notation: [ = inclusive, ( = exclusive, ) = exclusive, ] = inclusive. PostgreSQL normalizes ranges to canonical form ([x,y) for discrete types like integers). lower()/upper() extract bounds. lower_inc()/upper_inc() check if bounds inclusive. Unbounded (infinity) shown as empty.

8.17.2 Inclusive and Exclusive Bounds

Bound inclusivity: [a,b] = a ≤ x ≤ b (fully inclusive), (a,b) = a < x < b (fully exclusive), [a,b) = a ≤ x < b (left-inclusive), (a,b] = a < x ≤ b (right-inclusive). Discrete types (integers, dates) normalize to canonical [) form. Continuous types (numeric, timestamp) preserve input format.

🎯 Example: Bound Inclusivity

-- Show normalization of bounds
SELECT 
    '[1,10]'::int4range AS int_inclusive,        -- Normalizes to [1,11)
    '[1,10)'::int4range AS int_half_open,
    '[1.0,10.0]'::numrange AS num_inclusive,     -- Keeps [1.0,10.0]
    '[1.0,10.0)'::numrange AS num_half_open,
    '[2024-01-01,2024-01-31]'::daterange AS date_inclusive,  -- Normalizes to [2024-01-01,2024-02-01)
    isempty('[5,5)'::int4range) AS empty_range;
Result:
 int_inclusive | int_half_open | num_inclusive | num_half_open | date_inclusive                | empty_range
---------------+---------------+---------------+---------------+-------------------------------+-------------
 [1,11)        | [1,10)        | [1.0,10.0]    | [1.0,10.0)    | [2024-01-01,2024-02-01)       | t
(1 row)
Explanation: Discrete types (INT, DATE) have canonical [) form - [1,10] becomes [1,11) (includes 1-10). Continuous types (NUMERIC) preserve bounds as given. [x,x) is empty range (no values satisfy). Understanding normalization crucial for comparisons and overlaps.

8.17.3 Infinite (Unbounded) Ranges

Unbounded ranges: Lower unbound: (,upper] or (,upper). Upper unbound: [lower,) or (lower,). Fully unbounded: (,). Useful for "from date X onwards" or "up to date Y". lower_inf(range) and upper_inf(range) test for infinity.

🎯 Example: Unbounded Ranges

-- Membership tiers with date ranges
CREATE TABLE membership_tiers (
    tier_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    tier_name VARCHAR(50),
    valid_period DATERANGE
);

INSERT INTO membership_tiers (tier_name, valid_period) VALUES
    ('Legacy Tier', '[2020-01-01,2023-12-31)'),    -- Ended
    ('Current Tier', '[2024-01-01,)'),             -- No end date
    ('Future Tier', '[2025-01-01,)'),              -- Starts future
    ('Historical', '(,2020-01-01)');               -- No start (before 2020)

SELECT 
    tier_name,
    valid_period,
    lower(valid_period) AS starts,
    upper(valid_period) AS ends,
    lower_inf(valid_period) AS no_start,
    upper_inf(valid_period) AS no_end,
    valid_period @> CURRENT_DATE AS currently_valid
FROM membership_tiers;
Result:
 tier_name       | valid_period              | starts     | ends       | no_start | no_end | currently_valid
-----------------+---------------------------+------------+------------+----------+--------+-----------------
 Legacy Tier     | [2020-01-01,2023-12-31)   | 2020-01-01 | 2023-12-31 | f        | f      | f
 Current Tier    | [2024-01-01,)             | 2024-01-01 |            | f        | t      | t
 Future Tier     | [2025-01-01,)             | 2025-01-01 |            | f        | t      | f
 Historical      | (,2020-01-01)             |            | 2020-01-01 | t        | f      | f
(4 rows)
Explanation: [date,) represents "from date onwards" (infinite upper bound). (,date) represents "before date" (infinite lower bound). upper_inf()/lower_inf() check for infinity. @> tests if range contains value (CURRENT_DATE). Perfect for ongoing subscriptions, permanent records.

8.17.4 Range Input/Output

Range I/O syntax: Input: '[lower,upper]', '(lower,upper)', '[lower,upper)', '(lower,upper]', 'empty'. Whitespace allowed around bounds. Output: canonical form for discrete types, preserves input for continuous. Cast strings to range types: '...'::rangetype.

8.17.5 Constructing Ranges

Range construction: String literals: '[x,y]'::rangetype. Constructor functions: int4range(lower, upper, bounds), numrange(lower, upper, bounds). bounds parameter: '[]', '[)', '(]', '()'. Default: '[)' if omitted.

🎯 Example: Range Construction Methods

-- Various construction methods
SELECT 
    '[1,10]'::int4range AS string_literal,
    int4range(1, 10) AS default_bounds,          -- Defaults to [1,10)
    int4range(1, 10, '[]') AS fully_inclusive,   -- [1,11) after normalization
    int4range(1, 10, '[)') AS half_open,
    int4range(1, NULL) AS unbounded_upper,       -- [1,)
    numrange(1.5, 9.5, '()') AS exclusive_numeric,
    daterange('2024-01-01', '2024-12-31', '[]') AS date_range;
Result:
 string_literal | default_bounds | fully_inclusive | half_open | unbounded_upper | exclusive_numeric | date_range
----------------+----------------+-----------------+-----------+-----------------+-------------------+---------------------------
 [1,11)         | [1,10)         | [1,11)          | [1,10)    | [1,)            | (1.5,9.5)         | [2024-01-01,2025-01-01)
(1 row)
Explanation: Constructor functions (int4range, numrange, daterange) accept lower, upper, bounds parameters. Bounds string: '[]' (inclusive), '[)' (default, half-open), '(]', '()'. NULL bound creates infinite range. Discrete types normalize to canonical form.

8.17.6 Discrete Range Types

Discrete vs continuous ranges: Discrete (INT, DATE) have canonical form [) because successor function exists (1 → 2, 2024-01-01 → 2024-01-02). [1,10] normalized to [1,11). Continuous (NUMERIC, TIMESTAMP) keep original bounds - no canonical form.

8.17.7 Defining New Range Types

Custom range types: CREATE TYPE name AS RANGE (SUBTYPE = base_type, SUBTYPE_OPCLASS = operator_class). Optional: CANONICAL function for discrete types, SUBTYPE_DIFF function for indexing optimization. Example: create range of floats, custom enum types.

🎯 Example: Custom Range Type

-- Create custom range type for floating-point temperatures
CREATE TYPE floatrange AS RANGE (
    SUBTYPE = float8
);

CREATE TABLE temperature_logs (
    log_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    location VARCHAR(50),
    temp_range floatrange,
    recorded_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

INSERT INTO temperature_logs (location, temp_range) VALUES
    ('Warehouse A', floatrange(15.5, 25.5, '[)')),
    ('Warehouse B', floatrange(10.0, 30.0, '[]')),
    ('Freezer C', floatrange(-20.0, -5.0, '[)'));

SELECT 
    location,
    temp_range,
    lower(temp_range) AS min_temp,
    upper(temp_range) AS max_temp,
    temp_range @> 20.0::float8 AS includes_20_degrees
FROM temperature_logs;
Result:
 location    | temp_range    | min_temp | max_temp | includes_20_degrees
-------------+---------------+----------+----------+---------------------
 Warehouse A | [15.5,25.5)   |     15.5 |     25.5 | t
 Warehouse B | [10,30]       |       10 |       30 | t
 Freezer C   | [-20,-5)      |      -20 |       -5 | f
(3 rows)
Explanation: CREATE TYPE ... AS RANGE defines custom range type. SUBTYPE specifies base type (float8, custom types). Use custom ranges for domain-specific data (temperatures, weights, measurements). @> operator works on custom ranges too.

8.17.8 Indexing

Range indexing: GiST (Generalized Search Tree) and SP-GiST (Space-Partitioned GiST) indexes support range types. Accelerate overlap (&&), containment (@>, <@), adjacency (-|-) queries. Exclusion constraints with ranges prevent overlapping bookings/reservations.

🎯 Example: Range Indexing and Exclusion Constraints

-- Room reservation system with exclusion constraint
CREATE TABLE room_reservations (
    reservation_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    room_number INTEGER,
    reserved_period TSTZRANGE,
    guest_name VARCHAR(100),
    EXCLUDE USING GIST (room_number WITH =, reserved_period WITH &&)
);

-- Create GiST index for performance
CREATE INDEX idx_reserved_period ON room_reservations USING GIST (reserved_period);

-- Successful insertions (no overlap)
INSERT INTO room_reservations (room_number, reserved_period, guest_name) VALUES
    (101, '[2024-06-01 14:00:00+00, 2024-06-03 11:00:00+00)', 'Alice'),
    (101, '[2024-06-03 14:00:00+00, 2024-06-05 11:00:00+00)', 'Bob'),
    (102, '[2024-06-01 14:00:00+00, 2024-06-05 11:00:00+00)', 'Charlie');

-- This would FAIL (overlaps with Alice's reservation):
-- INSERT INTO room_reservations (room_number, reserved_period, guest_name)
-- VALUES (101, '[2024-06-02 10:00:00+00, 2024-06-04 10:00:00+00)', 'David');

SELECT 
    room_number,
    reserved_period,
    guest_name,
    lower(reserved_period) AS check_in,
    upper(reserved_period) AS check_out
FROM room_reservations
ORDER BY room_number, reserved_period;
Result:
 room_number |              reserved_period                | guest_name |      check_in          |      check_out
-------------+---------------------------------------------+------------+------------------------+------------------------
         101 | [2024-06-01 14:00:00+00,2024-06-03 11:00...) | Alice      | 2024-06-01 14:00:00+00 | 2024-06-03 11:00:00+00
         101 | [2024-06-03 14:00:00+00,2024-06-05 11:00...) | Bob        | 2024-06-03 14:00:00+00 | 2024-06-05 11:00:00+00
         102 | [2024-06-01 14:00:00+00,2024-06-05 11:00...) | Charlie    | 2024-06-01 14:00:00+00 | 2024-06-05 11:00:00+00
(3 rows)
Explanation: EXCLUDE USING GIST prevents overlapping ranges. room_number WITH = ensures same room. reserved_period WITH && prevents time overlap. GiST index accelerates range queries. Perfect for preventing double-booking in reservations, scheduling conflicts, resource allocation.

8.17.9 Constraints on Ranges

Range constraints: CHECK constraints: NOT isempty(range), lower(range) < upper(range). Exclusion constraints: EXCLUDE USING GIST (...) prevent overlaps. Unique constraints on ranges ensure no duplicates. Triggers for complex validation.

8.17.10 Range Operators and Functions

Range operators: @> (contains element/range), <@ (contained by), && (overlaps), << (strictly left), >> (strictly right), &< (not right), &> (not left), -|- (adjacent), + (union), * (intersection), - (difference). Functions: lower(), upper(), isempty(), lower_inc(), upper_inc(), lower_inf(), upper_inf(), range_merge().

🎯 Example: Range Operators

-- Demonstrate range operators
SELECT 
    '[1,10)'::int4range @> 5 AS contains_5,
    '[1,10)'::int4range @> '[3,7)'::int4range AS contains_subrange,
    '[1,10)'::int4range && '[5,15)'::int4range AS overlaps,
    '[1,10)'::int4range << '[20,30)'::int4range AS strictly_left_of,
    '[1,10)'::int4range -|- '[10,20)'::int4range AS adjacent,
    '[1,10)'::int4range + '[10,20)'::int4range AS union_ranges,
    '[1,15)'::int4range * '[10,20)'::int4range AS intersection,
    '[1,20)'::int4range - '[10,15)'::int4range AS difference;
Result:
 contains_5 | contains_subrange | overlaps | strictly_left_of | adjacent | union_ranges | intersection | difference
------------+-------------------+----------+------------------+----------+--------------+--------------+--------------
 t          | t                 | t        | t                | t        | [1,20)       | [10,15)      | [1,10)
(1 row)
Explanation: @> tests containment (element or subrange). && tests overlap. << and >> test ordering without overlap. -|- tests adjacency ([1,10) adjacent to [10,20)). + merges adjacent/overlapping ranges. * finds intersection. - finds difference. Rich operator set enables complex temporal/spatial queries.

8.18 Domain Types

📖 About Domain Types

Domain types are custom data types based on existing types with optional constraints. Similar to creating "subtypes" with validation rules. Syntax: CREATE DOMAIN name AS base_type [constraints]. Useful for enforcing business rules (email format, positive numbers, valid status codes) at database level. Domains centralize validation logic - define once, use everywhere.

Use Cases: Email addresses (format validation), postal codes (pattern matching), positive numbers (CHECK > 0), percentage values (0-100 range), status codes (limited enum-like values). Benefits: reusable constraints, self-documenting schema, centralized validation, type safety.

🎯 Example 1: Creating Basic Domains

-- Create domain types with constraints
CREATE DOMAIN email_address AS VARCHAR(255)
    CHECK (VALUE ~ '^[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Z|a-z]{2,}$');

CREATE DOMAIN positive_integer AS INTEGER
    CHECK (VALUE > 0);

CREATE DOMAIN percentage AS NUMERIC(5,2)
    CHECK (VALUE >= 0 AND VALUE <= 100);

CREATE DOMAIN us_postal_code AS CHAR(5)
    CHECK (VALUE ~ '^\d{5}$');

-- Use domains in table
CREATE TABLE customer_data (
    customer_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    email email_address,
    age positive_integer,
    discount_rate percentage,
    zip_code us_postal_code
);

-- Valid insertions
INSERT INTO customer_data (email, age, discount_rate, zip_code) VALUES
    ('john@example.com', 30, 15.50, '02101'),
    ('jane@company.org', 25, 10.00, '90210');

-- These would FAIL:
-- INSERT INTO customer_data (email, age) VALUES ('invalid-email', 30);  -- Invalid email format
-- INSERT INTO customer_data (email, age) VALUES ('test@test.com', -5);  -- Negative age
-- INSERT INTO customer_data (email, age, discount_rate) VALUES ('a@b.com', 30, 150);  -- Percentage > 100

SELECT * FROM customer_data;
Result:
 customer_id |        email        | age | discount_rate | zip_code
-------------+---------------------+-----+---------------+----------
           1 | john@example.com    |  30 |         15.50 | 02101
           2 | jane@company.org    |  25 |         10.00 | 90210
(2 rows)
Explanation: CREATE DOMAIN defines custom type with constraints. VALUE keyword refers to value being validated. Regular expression ~ operator validates email format. CHECK constraints enforce business rules. Domain constraints applied automatically on INSERT/UPDATE. Centralized validation prevents duplicate constraint definitions across tables.

🎯 Example 2: Domains with DEFAULT and NOT NULL

-- Domains with default values and NOT NULL
CREATE DOMAIN product_status AS VARCHAR(20)
    DEFAULT 'active'
    CHECK (VALUE IN ('active', 'inactive', 'discontinued', 'pending'));

CREATE DOMAIN non_empty_text AS TEXT
    NOT NULL
    CHECK (LENGTH(TRIM(VALUE)) > 0);

CREATE DOMAIN price_amount AS NUMERIC(10,2)
    CHECK (VALUE >= 0);

CREATE TABLE products_with_domains (
    product_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name non_empty_text,
    status product_status,  -- Will default to 'active'
    price price_amount
);

INSERT INTO products_with_domains (product_name, price) VALUES
    ('Laptop', 999.99),
    ('Mouse', 29.99);

INSERT INTO products_with_domains (product_name, status, price) VALUES
    ('Keyboard', 'discontinued', 79.99);

SELECT * FROM products_with_domains;
Result:
 product_id | product_name |    status     |  price
------------+--------------+---------------+---------
          1 | Laptop       | active        | 999.99
          2 | Mouse        | active        | 29.99
          3 | Keyboard     | discontinued  |  79.99
(3 rows)
Explanation: Domains can have DEFAULT values (applied when column omitted). NOT NULL constraint prevents NULL values. non_empty_text domain ensures no empty strings (LENGTH check). product_status limits values to predefined set. Domains encapsulate multiple constraints (type + validation + default).

🎯 Example 3: Domain Information and Management

-- Query domain information
SELECT 
    t.typname AS domain_name,
    pg_catalog.format_type(t.typbasetype, t.typtypmod) AS base_type,
    t.typnotnull AS not_null,
    t.typdefault AS default_value,
    c.conname AS constraint_name,
    pg_get_constraintdef(c.oid) AS constraint_definition
FROM pg_type t
LEFT JOIN pg_constraint c ON c.contypid = t.oid
WHERE t.typtype = 'd'
  AND t.typname IN ('email_address', 'positive_integer', 'product_status')
ORDER BY t.typname, c.conname;
Result:
 domain_name      | base_type          | not_null | default_value | constraint_name        | constraint_definition
------------------+--------------------+----------+---------------+------------------------+--------------------------------------------------
 email_address    | character varying  | f        |               | email_address_check    | CHECK ((VALUE::text ~ '^[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@...
 positive_integer | integer            | f        |               | positive_integer_check | CHECK ((VALUE > 0))
 product_status   | character varying  | f        | 'active'      | product_status_check   | CHECK ((VALUE::text = ANY (ARRAY['active'::...
(3 rows)
Explanation: pg_type catalog stores domain definitions. typtype='d' identifies domains. typbasetype shows underlying base type. Constraints stored in pg_constraint. pg_get_constraintdef() shows constraint logic. Query system catalogs to document domain rules.

🎯 Example 4: Altering and Dropping Domains

-- Alter domain to add/modify constraints
ALTER DOMAIN email_address 
    ADD CONSTRAINT email_length CHECK (LENGTH(VALUE) >= 5);

-- Drop constraint
ALTER DOMAIN email_address 
    DROP CONSTRAINT email_length;

-- Rename domain
ALTER DOMAIN us_postal_code RENAME TO zip_code;

-- Change default
ALTER DOMAIN product_status SET DEFAULT 'pending';

-- Drop domain (fails if in use)
-- DROP DOMAIN email_address;  -- ERROR: cannot drop domain email_address because other objects depend on it

-- Drop domain cascade (removes from all tables)
-- DROP DOMAIN email_address CASCADE;

SELECT typname FROM pg_type WHERE typtype = 'd' AND typname LIKE '%code%';
Result:
 typname
----------
 zip_code
(1 row)
Explanation: ALTER DOMAIN modifies existing domains. ADD CONSTRAINT adds validation rules. DROP CONSTRAINT removes them. RENAME changes domain name. SET DEFAULT changes default value. DROP DOMAIN removes domain (CASCADE required if columns use it). Domain changes affect all tables using that domain.

🎯 Example 5: Domains vs CHECK Constraints

-- Compare domain approach vs direct CHECK constraints
CREATE TABLE comparison_domain (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    email1 email_address,
    email2 email_address,
    email3 email_address
);

CREATE TABLE comparison_check (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    email1 VARCHAR(255) CHECK (email1 ~ '^[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Z|a-z]{2,}$'),
    email2 VARCHAR(255) CHECK (email2 ~ '^[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Z|a-z]{2,}$'),
    email3 VARCHAR(255) CHECK (email3 ~ '^[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Z|a-z]{2,}$')
);

SELECT 
    'Domain Approach' AS method,
    'Reusable, DRY, centralized' AS advantages,
    'Self-documenting type names' AS benefit
UNION ALL
SELECT 
    'CHECK Constraints' AS method,
    'More verbose, repeated logic' AS advantages,
    'No type abstraction' AS benefit;
Result:
 method             | advantages                    | benefit
--------------------+-------------------------------+---------------------------
 Domain Approach    | Reusable, DRY, centralized    | Self-documenting type names
 CHECK Constraints  | More verbose, repeated logic  | No type abstraction
(2 rows)
Explanation: Domains provide DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) approach - define constraint once, reuse everywhere. Column type "email_address" self-documents purpose. CHECK constraints require repeating logic for each column. Domains enable easier maintenance - change constraint once, applies to all uses. Trade-off: domains add abstraction layer vs direct constraints.

8.19 Object Identifier Types

📖 About Object Identifier Types

Object Identifier (OID) types reference system catalog objects. Types: OID (generic), REGPROC (function), REGPROCEDURE (function with args), REGOPER (operator), REGOPERATOR (operator with args), REGCLASS (table/view/sequence), REGTYPE (data type), REGROLE (role), REGNAMESPACE (schema), REGCONFIG (text search config), REGDICTIONARY (text search dictionary).

Use Cases: Referencing database objects by name (converted to OID internally), audit logging (track which tables modified), dynamic SQL (lookup table names), system queries (pg_catalog joins), extension development. More readable than raw OIDs - displays as name, stores as number.

🎯 Example 1: REGCLASS for Table References

-- Use regclass to reference tables
CREATE TABLE audit_log (
    log_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    table_name REGCLASS,
    action VARCHAR(20),
    logged_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

INSERT INTO audit_log (table_name, action) VALUES
    ('customers', 'INSERT'),
    ('orders', 'UPDATE'),
    ('products', 'DELETE');

-- Query table information using regclass
SELECT 
    log_id,
    table_name,
    table_name::oid AS table_oid,
    action,
    pg_relation_size(table_name) AS size_bytes,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(table_name)) AS size_readable
FROM audit_log;
Result:
 log_id | table_name | table_oid | action | size_bytes | size_readable
--------+------------+-----------+--------+------------+---------------
      1 | customers  |     16385 | INSERT |       8192 | 8192 bytes
      2 | orders     |     16392 | UPDATE |      16384 | 16 kB
      3 | products   |     16399 | DELETE |       8192 | 8192 bytes
(3 rows)
Explanation: REGCLASS stores table references as OID but displays as name. Automatically validates table exists on insert. ::oid cast reveals internal OID. Can use in functions expecting table names (pg_relation_size). More maintainable than storing table names as text - handles schema changes, prevents invalid references.

🎯 Example 2: REGPROC and Function References

-- Track function usage with regproc
CREATE TABLE function_usage_log (
    log_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    function_name REGPROC,
    call_count INTEGER DEFAULT 1,
    last_called TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

INSERT INTO function_usage_log (function_name) VALUES
    ('lower'),
    ('upper'),
    ('substring'),
    ('now');

SELECT 
    function_name,
    function_name::oid AS func_oid,
    pg_get_functiondef(function_name::oid) AS function_definition
FROM function_usage_log
WHERE function_name IN ('lower', 'upper')
LIMIT 2;
Result:
 function_name | func_oid | function_definition
---------------+----------+----------------------------------------------------
 lower         |    870   | CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION pg_catalog.lower(text)...
 upper         |    871   | CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION pg_catalog.upper(text)...
(2 rows)
Explanation: REGPROC stores function references by name. Resolves to OID internally. pg_get_functiondef() retrieves function source code using OID. Useful for auditing function calls, tracking dependencies, documentation generation.

🎯 Example 3: REGTYPE for Data Type References

-- Store data type information
CREATE TABLE column_metadata (
    meta_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    table_name REGCLASS,
    column_name VARCHAR(100),
    data_type REGTYPE
);

INSERT INTO column_metadata (table_name, column_name, data_type) VALUES
    ('customers', 'customer_id', 'integer'),
    ('customers', 'customer_name', 'varchar'),
    ('orders', 'order_date', 'timestamp'),
    ('products', 'price', 'numeric');

SELECT 
    table_name,
    column_name,
    data_type,
    data_type::oid AS type_oid,
    pg_type.typlen AS type_length,
    pg_type.typbyval AS passed_by_value
FROM column_metadata
JOIN pg_type ON data_type = pg_type.oid;
Result:
 table_name | column_name   | data_type           | type_oid | type_length | passed_by_value
------------+---------------+---------------------+----------+-------------+-----------------
 customers  | customer_id   | integer             |       23 |           4 | t
 customers  | customer_name | character varying   |     1043 |          -1 | f
 orders     | order_date    | timestamp without...|     1114 |           8 | t
 products   | price         | numeric             |     1700 |          -1 | f
(4 rows)
Explanation: REGTYPE references data types by name. Joins with pg_type catalog for detailed type information. typlen shows storage size (-1 = variable length). Useful for metadata queries, schema documentation, type analysis.

🎯 Example 4: REGROLE for User/Role References

-- Track database access by role
CREATE TABLE access_log (
    access_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    user_role REGROLE,
    access_time TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    action VARCHAR(50)
);

INSERT INTO access_log (user_role, action) VALUES
    (CURRENT_USER, 'Login'),
    (SESSION_USER, 'Query executed'),
    (CURRENT_USER, 'Logout');

SELECT 
    user_role,
    user_role::oid AS role_oid,
    action,
    access_time
FROM access_log;
Result:
 user_role | role_oid |     action      |       access_time
-----------+----------+-----------------+------------------------
 postgres  |       10 | Login           | 2026-01-26 10:00:00
 postgres  |       10 | Query executed  | 2026-01-26 10:05:00
 postgres  |       10 | Logout          | 2026-01-26 10:30:00
(3 rows)
Explanation: REGROLE stores role/user references. CURRENT_USER and SESSION_USER return REGROLE values. Validates role exists. Useful for audit trails, access logging, permission tracking. More reliable than storing username as text.

🎯 Example 5: OID Types Comparison

-- Compare different OID types
SELECT 
    'customers'::regclass AS table_ref,
    'customers'::regclass::oid AS table_oid,
    'lower'::regproc AS function_ref,
    'integer'::regtype AS type_ref,
    'public'::regnamespace AS schema_ref,
    CURRENT_USER::regrole AS role_ref;

-- Find all OID columns in system catalogs
SELECT 
    t.typname AS oid_type_name,
    t.typcategory,
    obj_description(t.oid, 'pg_type') AS description
FROM pg_type t
WHERE t.typname LIKE 'reg%'
ORDER BY t.typname;
Result:
 table_ref | table_oid | function_ref | type_ref | schema_ref | role_ref
-----------+-----------+--------------+----------+------------+----------
 customers |     16385 | lower        | integer  | public     | postgres

 oid_type_name  | typcategory | description
----------------+-------------+------------------------------------------
 regclass       | N           | registered class (table/view/sequence)
 regconfig      | N           | registered text search configuration
 regdictionary  | N           | registered text search dictionary
 regnamespace   | N           | registered namespace (schema)
 regoper        | N           | registered operator
 regoperator    | N           | registered operator with arguments
 regproc        | N           | registered procedure (function)
 regprocedure   | N           | registered procedure with arguments
 regrole        | N           | registered role
 regtype        | N           | registered type
(10 rows)
Explanation: REG* types provide human-readable references to database objects while storing efficient OIDs internally. Each type specialized for different object category. Display as names, compare as numbers. Essential for system programming, extensions, metadata management. Choose appropriate REG type for object category being referenced.

8.20 pg_lsn Type

📖 About pg_lsn Type

pg_lsn (Log Sequence Number) stores PostgreSQL Write-Ahead Log (WAL) positions. Format: 'XXX/YYYYYYYY' (8-byte unsigned integer, displayed as two hex numbers). Represents location in WAL stream. Used for replication, recovery, backup management. Comparison operators enable calculating WAL distance, checking replication lag.

Use Cases: Monitoring replication lag, tracking backup positions, WAL archiving, point-in-time recovery, measuring database activity. Essential for replication setups, high availability, disaster recovery planning.

🎯 Example 1: WAL Position Monitoring

-- Track WAL positions over time
CREATE TABLE wal_checkpoint (
    checkpoint_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    checkpoint_time TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    wal_position pg_lsn
);

-- Record current WAL position
INSERT INTO wal_checkpoint (wal_position) VALUES
    (pg_current_wal_lsn());

-- Simulate some activity and record again
INSERT INTO wal_checkpoint (wal_position) VALUES
    (pg_current_wal_lsn());

SELECT 
    checkpoint_id,
    checkpoint_time,
    wal_position,
    wal_position::text AS lsn_text
FROM wal_checkpoint;
Result:
 checkpoint_id |    checkpoint_time      | wal_position | lsn_text
---------------+-------------------------+--------------+--------------
             1 | 2026-01-26 10:00:00     | 0/1A2B3C4D   | 0/1A2B3C4D
             2 | 2026-01-26 10:05:00     | 0/1A2B5E8F   | 0/1A2B5E8F
(2 rows)
Explanation: pg_lsn stores WAL log sequence numbers. pg_current_wal_lsn() returns current WAL write position. Format X/Y where X and Y are hexadecimal. Monotonically increasing value. Each database modification advances WAL position. Essential for tracking database activity and replication.

🎯 Example 2: Calculating WAL Distance

-- Calculate WAL distance between checkpoints
SELECT 
    c1.checkpoint_id AS from_id,
    c2.checkpoint_id AS to_id,
    c1.wal_position AS from_lsn,
    c2.wal_position AS to_lsn,
    c2.wal_position - c1.wal_position AS bytes_written,
    pg_size_pretty((c2.wal_position - c1.wal_position)::bigint) AS size_written,
    c2.checkpoint_time - c1.checkpoint_time AS time_elapsed
FROM wal_checkpoint c1
CROSS JOIN wal_checkpoint c2
WHERE c1.checkpoint_id < c2.checkpoint_id;
Result:
 from_id | to_id | from_lsn   | to_lsn     | bytes_written | size_written | time_elapsed
---------+-------+------------+------------+---------------+--------------+--------------
       1 |     2 | 0/1A2B3C4D | 0/1A2B5E8F |          8770 | 8770 bytes   | 00:05:00
(1 row)
Explanation: Subtracting pg_lsn values calculates bytes of WAL generated. Useful for measuring database activity rate, estimating disk I/O, capacity planning. pg_size_pretty() formats bytes human-readable. Monitor WAL generation to optimize checkpoint frequency, archive settings.

🎯 Example 3: Replication Lag Monitoring

-- Monitor replication status (requires replication setup)
SELECT 
    client_addr AS replica_ip,
    state,
    sent_lsn,
    write_lsn,
    flush_lsn,
    replay_lsn,
    sent_lsn - replay_lsn AS replication_lag_bytes,
    pg_size_pretty((sent_lsn - replay_lsn)::bigint) AS lag_size,
    write_lag,
    flush_lag,
    replay_lag
FROM pg_stat_replication;
Result:
 replica_ip    | state     | sent_lsn   | write_lsn  | flush_lsn  | replay_lsn | replication_lag_bytes | lag_size    | write_lag | flush_lag | replay_lag
---------------+-----------+------------+------------+------------+------------+-----------------------+-------------+-----------+-----------+------------
 192.168.1.100 | streaming | 0/1A2B6F00 | 0/1A2B6F00 | 0/1A2B6F00 | 0/1A2B6E80 |                   128 | 128 bytes   | 00:00:00.01 | 00:00:00.02 | 00:00:00.03
(1 row)
Explanation: pg_stat_replication view shows replication status. sent_lsn = WAL sent to replica. replay_lsn = WAL applied on replica. Difference = replication lag in bytes. write_lag/flush_lag/replay_lag show time delays. Monitor these to ensure replicas stay current, detect replication issues.

🎯 Example 4: Backup Management with LSN

-- Track backup positions for point-in-time recovery
CREATE TABLE backup_catalog (
    backup_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    backup_name VARCHAR(100),
    backup_start_lsn pg_lsn,
    backup_stop_lsn pg_lsn,
    backup_time TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

-- Record backup (simulated)
INSERT INTO backup_catalog (backup_name, backup_start_lsn, backup_stop_lsn) VALUES
    ('daily-backup-2026-01-26', 
     pg_current_wal_lsn(), 
     pg_current_wal_lsn());

SELECT 
    backup_name,
    backup_start_lsn,
    backup_stop_lsn,
    backup_stop_lsn - backup_start_lsn AS backup_size_bytes,
    backup_time
FROM backup_catalog;
Result:
 backup_name              | backup_start_lsn | backup_stop_lsn | backup_size_bytes |     backup_time
--------------------------+------------------+-----------------+-------------------+---------------------
 daily-backup-2026-01-26  | 0/1A2B7000       | 0/1A2B7020      |                32 | 2026-01-26 11:00:00
(1 row)
Explanation: Recording backup LSN positions enables point-in-time recovery. backup_start_lsn marks where backup begins. backup_stop_lsn marks where it ends. Can restore to any LSN between these points using WAL archives. Essential for disaster recovery, regulatory compliance, database migration.

🎯 Example 5: LSN Comparison and Ordering

-- Demonstrate pg_lsn comparison operators
SELECT 
    '0/1000000'::pg_lsn < '0/2000000'::pg_lsn AS less_than,
    '0/2000000'::pg_lsn > '0/1000000'::pg_lsn AS greater_than,
    '0/1A2B3C4D'::pg_lsn = '0/1A2B3C4D'::pg_lsn AS equal,
    '0/1A2B3C4D'::pg_lsn <> '0/1A2B3C4E'::pg_lsn AS not_equal,
    '0/2000000'::pg_lsn - '0/1000000'::pg_lsn AS difference_bytes,
    GREATEST('0/1000000'::pg_lsn, '0/2000000'::pg_lsn) AS max_lsn,
    LEAST('0/1000000'::pg_lsn, '0/2000000'::pg_lsn) AS min_lsn;
Result:
 less_than | greater_than | equal | not_equal | difference_bytes | max_lsn    | min_lsn
-----------+--------------+-------+-----------+------------------+------------+------------
 t         | t            | t     | t         |         16777216 | 0/2000000  | 0/1000000
(1 row)
Explanation: pg_lsn supports comparison operators (<, >, =, <=, >=, <>). Subtraction yields byte difference. GREATEST/LEAST find max/min LSNs. LSNs always increase - earlier events have smaller LSNs. Essential for temporal queries, replication monitoring, recovery planning.

8.21 Pseudo-Types

📖 About Pseudo-Types

Pseudo-types are special placeholder types used in function declarations. Cannot be used as column types. Types: ANY, ANYELEMENT, ANYARRAY, ANYNONARRAY, ANYENUM, ANYRANGE, RECORD, TRIGGER, EVENT_TRIGGER, VOID, INTERNAL, CSTRING. Enable polymorphic functions (work with multiple types), trigger functions, internal functions.

Common Uses: ANY* family for generic functions accepting any type. RECORD for dynamic row types. TRIGGER for trigger functions. VOID for functions with no return value. These types essential for system functions, extensions, generic utilities.

🎯 Example 1: ANYELEMENT for Polymorphic Functions

-- Create generic function using ANYELEMENT
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION first_element(ANYARRAY)
RETURNS ANYELEMENT AS $$
    SELECT $1[1];
$$ LANGUAGE SQL;

-- Works with any array type
SELECT 
    first_element(ARRAY[1, 2, 3]) AS first_int,
    first_element(ARRAY['a', 'b', 'c']) AS first_text,
    first_element(ARRAY[1.5, 2.5, 3.5]) AS first_numeric;
Result:
 first_int | first_text | first_numeric
-----------+------------+---------------
         1 | a          |          1.5
(1 row)
Explanation: ANYELEMENT represents "any specific type". ANYARRAY parameter accepts any array type. Return type ANYELEMENT matches element type of input array. Single function works with INTEGER[], TEXT[], NUMERIC[], etc. Polymorphism without code duplication.

🎯 Example 2: RECORD for Dynamic Row Types

-- Function returning dynamic record structure
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION get_table_sample(table_name regclass)
RETURNS SETOF RECORD AS $$
BEGIN
    RETURN QUERY EXECUTE format('SELECT * FROM %s LIMIT 3', table_name);
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Must specify column structure when calling
SELECT * FROM get_table_sample('customers') 
    AS t(customer_id int, customer_name varchar, email varchar, phone varchar);
Result:
 customer_id | customer_name | email             | phone
-------------+---------------+-------------------+-------------
           1 | John Doe      | john@example.com  | 555-0001
           2 | Jane Smith    | jane@example.com  | 555-0002
           3 | Bob Johnson   | bob@example.com   | 555-0003
(3 rows)
Explanation: RECORD pseudo-type represents row with unknown structure. Useful for dynamic SQL where column structure determined at runtime. Caller must specify column definitions using AS clause. SETOF RECORD returns multiple rows. Essential for generic utility functions.

🎯 Example 3: VOID for Side-Effect Functions

-- Function with VOID return (performs action, returns nothing)
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION log_message(message TEXT)
RETURNS VOID AS $$
BEGIN
    RAISE NOTICE 'LOG: %', message;
    -- Could insert into audit table here
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Call void function
SELECT log_message('Database operation completed');

-- VOID functions typically called for side effects
DO $$
BEGIN
    PERFORM log_message('Batch process started');
    -- More operations...
    PERFORM log_message('Batch process completed');
END $$;
Result:
NOTICE:  LOG: Database operation completed
 log_message
-------------
(empty result)

NOTICE:  LOG: Batch process started
NOTICE:  LOG: Batch process completed
(execution successful)
Explanation: VOID indicates function returns no value (like void in C/Java). Called for side effects (logging, updates, notifications). Use PERFORM in pl/pgsql to call VOID functions (SELECT discards result). Common for utility functions, triggers, maintenance procedures.

🎯 Example 4: TRIGGER Pseudo-Type

-- Trigger function must return TRIGGER type
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION audit_trigger_function()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
    IF TG_OP = 'INSERT' THEN
        RAISE NOTICE 'Inserted row with ID: %', NEW.customer_id;
    ELSIF TG_OP = 'UPDATE' THEN
        RAISE NOTICE 'Updated row ID: % (old: %, new: %)', 
                     NEW.customer_id, OLD.customer_name, NEW.customer_name;
    ELSIF TG_OP = 'DELETE' THEN
        RAISE NOTICE 'Deleted row with ID: %', OLD.customer_id;
    END IF;
    
    RETURN NEW;  -- For INSERT/UPDATE, return NULL for DELETE
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Attach trigger to table
CREATE TRIGGER customers_audit_trigger
AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE ON customers
FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION audit_trigger_function();
Result:
-- After INSERT:
NOTICE:  Inserted row with ID: 11

-- After UPDATE:
NOTICE:  Updated row ID: 11 (old: John, new: Jonathan)

-- After DELETE:
NOTICE:  Deleted row with ID: 11
Explanation: TRIGGER pseudo-type required for trigger functions. Provides special variables: TG_OP (operation), NEW (new row), OLD (old row), TG_TABLE_NAME, etc. RETURN NEW for INSERT/UPDATE, RETURN OLD for DELETE (or NULL to abort). Cannot be used outside trigger context.

🎯 Example 5: Pseudo-Type Summary

-- Query all pseudo-types in the system
SELECT 
    t.typname AS pseudo_type,
    t.typtype,
    CASE t.typtype
        WHEN 'p' THEN 'Pseudo-type'
        ELSE 'Other'
    END AS type_category,
    obj_description(t.oid, 'pg_type') AS description
FROM pg_type t
WHERE t.typtype = 'p'
ORDER BY t.typname;
Result:
 pseudo_type      | typtype | type_category | description
------------------+---------+---------------+----------------------------------------------
 any              | p       | Pseudo-type   | Pseudo-type for ANY parameter
 anyelement       | p       | Pseudo-type   | Pseudo-type for ANY element type
 anyarray         | p       | Pseudo-type   | Pseudo-type for ANY array type
 anynonarray      | p       | Pseudo-type   | Pseudo-type for ANY non-array type
 anyenum          | p       | Pseudo-type   | Pseudo-type for ANY enum type
 anyrange         | p       | Pseudo-type   | Pseudo-type for ANY range type
 cstring          | p       | Pseudo-type   | C-style string
 event_trigger    | p       | Pseudo-type   | Pseudo-type for event trigger functions
 internal         | p       | Pseudo-type   | Pseudo-type for internal use
 record           | p       | Pseudo-type   | Pseudo-type for unspecified row type
 trigger          | p       | Pseudo-type   | Pseudo-type for trigger functions
 void             | p       | Pseudo-type   | Pseudo-type for functions with no return
(12 rows)
Explanation: Pseudo-types (typtype='p') exist only for function signatures, not data storage. ANY* family enables polymorphic functions. RECORD for dynamic structures. TRIGGER/EVENT_TRIGGER for hooks. VOID for side-effect functions. INTERNAL/CSTRING for system internals. Essential PostgreSQL feature enabling flexible, reusable function design. Cannot create columns with pseudo-types - use only in function declarations.

Chapter 9: Functions and Operators

📖 About Functions and Operators

PostgreSQL provides a rich set of functions and operators for data manipulation, computation, and analysis. This chapter covers logical operators, mathematical functions, string operations, date/time manipulation, pattern matching, aggregation, window functions, and much more.

Understanding these functions is essential for effective SQL queries, data transformation, and building powerful database applications. Each section includes practical examples using our E-commerce database.

9.1 Logical Operators

Logical operators: Boolean logic operators AND, OR, NOT. Used in WHERE clauses, conditional expressions. Three-valued logic with NULL (TRUE, FALSE, NULL). Operator precedence: NOT > AND > OR. Use parentheses for clarity.

Logical Operators Syntax
-- AND: Both conditions must be TRUE
condition1 AND condition2

-- OR: At least one condition must be TRUE
condition1 OR condition2

-- NOT: Negates a condition
NOT condition

🎯 Example 1: AND Operator

-- Find high-value orders from specific customer
SELECT 
    order_id,
    customer_id,
    order_date,
    total_amount,
    status
FROM orders
WHERE customer_id = 1 
  AND total_amount > 1000
ORDER BY order_date DESC;
Result:
 order_id | customer_id | order_date | total_amount | status
----------+-------------+------------+--------------+----------
        1 |           1 | 2024-01-01 |      1200.00 | completed
(1 row)
Explanation: AND operator requires both conditions to be TRUE. Query returns only orders matching customer_id=1 AND total_amount>1000. Both conditions must be satisfied.

🎯 Example 2: OR Operator

-- Find orders that are either pending or processing
SELECT 
    order_id,
    customer_id,
    order_date,
    total_amount,
    status
FROM orders
WHERE status = 'pending' OR status = 'processing'
ORDER BY order_date;
Result:
 order_id | customer_id | order_date | total_amount |  status
----------+-------------+------------+--------------+-----------
        3 |           3 | 2024-01-03 |       500.00 | pending
        4 |           1 | 2024-01-04 |       350.00 | processing
(2 rows)
Explanation: OR operator returns TRUE if at least one condition is TRUE. Query returns orders with status='pending' OR status='processing'. Alternative syntax: WHERE status IN ('pending', 'processing').

🎯 Example 3: NOT Operator

-- Find products NOT in Electronics category
SELECT 
    product_id,
    product_name,
    category,
    price
FROM products
WHERE NOT category = 'Electronics'
  AND price < 100
ORDER BY price;
Result:
 product_id | product_name  |  category | price
------------+---------------+-----------+-------
          9 | Office Chair  | Furniture | 89.99
          6 | Water Bottle  | Sports    | 15.99
(2 rows)
Explanation: NOT negates condition. WHERE NOT category = 'Electronics' equivalent to WHERE category != 'Electronics'. Combined with AND for multiple conditions. Use parentheses for complex expressions.

🎯 Example 4: NULL Logic

-- Three-valued logic demonstration with NULL
SELECT 
    TRUE AND NULL AS and_null,      -- Result: NULL
    FALSE AND NULL AS and_false,    -- Result: FALSE
    TRUE OR NULL AS or_true,        -- Result: TRUE
    FALSE OR NULL AS or_null,       -- Result: NULL
    NOT NULL AS not_null;           -- Result: NULL
Result:
 and_null | and_false | or_true | or_null | not_null
----------+-----------+---------+---------+----------
          | f         | t       |         |
(1 row)
Explanation: NULL represents unknown. TRUE AND NULL = NULL (unknown if both true). FALSE AND NULL = FALSE (one already false). TRUE OR NULL = TRUE (one already true). FALSE OR NULL = NULL (unknown if any true). NOT NULL = NULL (unknown negated still unknown).

🎯 Example 5: Complex Logical Expressions

-- Operator precedence: NOT > AND > OR
SELECT 
    order_id,
    customer_id,
    total_amount,
    status,
    CASE 
        WHEN (status = 'completed' OR status = 'shipped') AND total_amount > 500 
            THEN 'High-value completed'
        WHEN NOT status = 'cancelled' AND total_amount < 200 
            THEN 'Low-value active'
        ELSE 'Other'
    END AS order_category
FROM orders
ORDER BY order_id
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 order_id | customer_id | total_amount |  status   |   order_category
----------+-------------+--------------+-----------+----------------------
        1 |           1 |      1200.00 | completed | High-value completed
        2 |           2 |       850.00 | shipped   | High-value completed
        3 |           3 |       500.00 | pending   | Other
        4 |           1 |       350.00 | processing| Other
        5 |           4 |      1500.00 | completed | High-value completed
(5 rows)
Explanation: Logical operators follow precedence: NOT evaluates first, then AND, then OR. Use parentheses to override precedence and clarify intent. Complex expressions in CASE statements common for categorization. Essential for filtering and business logic.

9.2 Comparison Functions and Operators

Comparison operators: Compare values and return boolean (TRUE/FALSE). Standard operators: = (equal), != or <> (not equal), < (less than), <= (less or equal), > (greater than), >= (greater or equal). NULL comparisons require IS NULL / IS NOT NULL. BETWEEN for range checks. IS DISTINCT FROM for NULL-safe comparison.

Comparison Operators
-- Standard comparison
value1 = value2          -- Equal
value1 <> value2         -- Not equal (also !=)
value1 < value2          -- Less than
value1 <= value2         -- Less than or equal
value1 > value2          -- Greater than
value1 >= value2         -- Greater than or equal

-- NULL handling
value IS NULL            -- Check for NULL
value IS NOT NULL        -- Check for non-NULL
value1 IS DISTINCT FROM value2  -- NULL-safe comparison

-- Range checking
value BETWEEN low AND high      -- Inclusive range
value NOT BETWEEN low AND high  -- Outside range

🎯 Example 1: Basic Comparisons

-- Various comparison operators
SELECT 
    product_id,
    product_name,
    price,
    price = 999.99 AS exact_match,
    price <> 999.99 AS not_equal,
    price < 100 AS under_100,
    price >= 1000 AS expensive
FROM products
WHERE product_id <= 5
ORDER BY product_id;
Result:
 product_id | product_name | price  | exact_match | not_equal | under_100 | expensive
------------+--------------+--------+-------------+-----------+-----------+-----------
          1 | Laptop       | 999.99 | t           | f         | f         | f
          2 | Smartphone   | 699.99 | f           | t         | f         | f
          3 | Headphones   |  49.99 | f           | t         | t         | f
          4 | Monitor      | 299.99 | f           | t         | f         | f
          5 | Keyboard     |  79.99 | f           | t         | t         | f
(5 rows)
Explanation: Comparison operators return boolean values (t/f). = checks equality, <> checks inequality. <, <=, >, >= compare numeric/date values. All comparisons can be used in WHERE clauses or SELECT expressions.

🎯 Example 2: BETWEEN Operator

-- BETWEEN for range queries
SELECT 
    order_id,
    customer_id,
    order_date,
    total_amount
FROM orders
WHERE total_amount BETWEEN 500 AND 1000
  AND order_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'
ORDER BY total_amount;
Result:
 order_id | customer_id | order_date | total_amount
----------+-------------+------------+--------------
        3 |           3 | 2024-01-03 |       500.00
        2 |           2 | 2024-01-02 |       850.00
(2 rows)
Explanation: BETWEEN is inclusive on both ends. BETWEEN 500 AND 1000 equivalent to >= 500 AND <= 1000. Works with numbers, dates, strings. More readable than separate >= and <= operators. NOT BETWEEN for exclusive range.

🎯 Example 3: NULL Comparisons

-- NULL requires special operators
SELECT 
    employee_id,
    first_name,
    last_name,
    manager_id,
    manager_id IS NULL AS is_top_manager,
    manager_id IS NOT NULL AS has_manager
FROM employees
WHERE employee_id <= 5
ORDER BY employee_id;
Result:
 employee_id | first_name | last_name | manager_id | is_top_manager | has_manager
-------------+------------+-----------+------------+----------------+-------------
           1 | John       | Manager   |            | t              | f
           2 | Jane       | Developer |          1 | f              | t
           3 | Bob        | Designer  |          1 | f              | t
           4 | Alice      | Analyst   |          2 | f              | t
           5 | Charlie    | Support   |          2 | f              | t
(5 rows)
Explanation: NULL = NULL returns NULL (not TRUE). Use IS NULL to check for NULL. IS NOT NULL checks for non-NULL. NULL represents missing/unknown value. Regular comparison operators (=, <>) don't work with NULL. Top-level managers have NULL manager_id.

🎯 Example 4: IS DISTINCT FROM

-- NULL-safe comparison with IS DISTINCT FROM
CREATE TEMP TABLE comparison_test (
    id INT,
    value1 INT,
    value2 INT
);

INSERT INTO comparison_test VALUES
(1, 10, 10),
(2, 10, 20),
(3, NULL, NULL),
(4, NULL, 10);

SELECT 
    id,
    value1,
    value2,
    value1 = value2 AS regular_equal,
    value1 IS NOT DISTINCT FROM value2 AS null_safe_equal,
    value1 IS DISTINCT FROM value2 AS null_safe_not_equal
FROM comparison_test
ORDER BY id;
Result:
 id | value1 | value2 | regular_equal | null_safe_equal | null_safe_not_equal
----+--------+--------+---------------+-----------------+---------------------
  1 |     10 |     10 |               | t               | f
  2 |     10 |     20 | f             | f               | t
  3 |        |        |               | t               | f
  4 |        |     10 |               | f               | t
(4 rows)
Explanation: IS DISTINCT FROM treats NULL as comparable value. NULL IS NOT DISTINCT FROM NULL returns TRUE. NULL IS DISTINCT FROM 10 returns TRUE. Regular = returns NULL when either operand is NULL. Useful for NULL-aware equality checks in joins, grouping, or UNIQUE constraints.

🎯 Example 5: Comparison with Subqueries

-- Comparison with aggregate results
SELECT 
    product_id,
    product_name,
    price,
    CASE 
        WHEN price > (SELECT AVG(price) FROM products) THEN 'Above Average'
        WHEN price < (SELECT AVG(price) FROM products) THEN 'Below Average'
        ELSE 'Average'
    END AS price_category
FROM products
WHERE category = 'Electronics'
ORDER BY price DESC
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 product_id | product_name  | price  | price_category
------------+---------------+--------+----------------
          1 | Laptop        | 999.99 | Above Average
          2 | Smartphone    | 699.99 | Above Average
          4 | Monitor       | 299.99 | Below Average
          5 | Keyboard      |  79.99 | Below Average
          3 | Headphones    |  49.99 | Below Average
(5 rows)
Explanation: Comparison operators work with scalar subqueries (returning single value). Common pattern: compare column to aggregate (AVG, MAX, MIN). Subquery evaluated once, result compared to each row. Useful for categorizing data relative to statistics.

9.3 Mathematical Functions and Operators

Mathematical functions: PostgreSQL provides comprehensive math operations. Basic arithmetic: +, -, *, / (division), % (modulo), ^ (exponentiation). Functions: abs() (absolute value), ceil()/floor() (rounding), round() (decimal rounding), trunc() (truncation), sqrt() (square root), power(), exp(), ln()/log(), trigonometric functions (sin, cos, tan), random() (0-1), and more.

Common Mathematical Functions
-- Arithmetic operators
+ (addition), - (subtraction), * (multiplication), / (division)
% (modulo), ^ (exponentiation), |/ (square root), ||/ (cube root)

-- Rounding functions
round(numeric, decimals)      -- Round to decimals
ceil(numeric)                 -- Round up
floor(numeric)                -- Round down
trunc(numeric, decimals)      -- Truncate

-- Mathematical functions
abs(numeric)                  -- Absolute value
sqrt(numeric)                 -- Square root
power(base, exponent)         -- Exponentiation
exp(numeric)                  -- e^x
ln(numeric)                   -- Natural logarithm
log(numeric)                  -- Base-10 logarithm

-- Trigonometric
sin(radians), cos(radians), tan(radians)
asin(), acos(), atan(), atan2(y, x)

-- Random
random()                      -- Random 0-1
random() * (max - min) + min  -- Random in range

🎯 Example 1: Basic Arithmetic

-- Arithmetic operations on order data
SELECT 
    order_id,
    total_amount,
    total_amount * 0.10 AS tax_10_percent,
    total_amount + (total_amount * 0.10) AS amount_with_tax,
    total_amount / 12 AS monthly_payment,
    total_amount % 100 AS remainder_mod_100
FROM orders
WHERE order_id <= 5
ORDER BY order_id;
Result:
 order_id | total_amount | tax_10_percent | amount_with_tax | monthly_payment | remainder_mod_100
----------+--------------+----------------+-----------------+-----------------+-------------------
        1 |      1200.00 |         120.00 |         1320.00 |          100.00 |              0.00
        2 |       850.00 |          85.00 |          935.00 |           70.83 |             50.00
        3 |       500.00 |          50.00 |          550.00 |           41.67 |              0.00
        4 |       350.00 |          35.00 |          385.00 |           29.17 |             50.00
        5 |      1500.00 |         150.00 |         1650.00 |          125.00 |              0.00
(5 rows)
Explanation: Basic arithmetic operators: * (multiplication), + (addition), / (division), % (modulo). Modulo (%) returns remainder after division. NUMERIC type preserves precision. Use parentheses for order of operations. Common for calculations, tax, discounts, installments.

🎯 Example 2: Rounding Functions

-- Different rounding strategies
SELECT 
    product_name,
    price AS original_price,
    round(price) AS rounded,
    round(price, 1) AS one_decimal,
    ceil(price) AS rounded_up,
    floor(price) AS rounded_down,
    trunc(price, 1) AS truncated
FROM products
WHERE category = 'Electronics'
ORDER BY price DESC
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 product_name  | original_price | rounded | one_decimal | rounded_up | rounded_down | truncated
---------------+----------------+---------+-------------+------------+--------------+-----------
 Laptop        |         999.99 |    1000 |       999.0 |       1000 |          999 |     999.9
 Smartphone    |         699.99 |     700 |       699.0 |        700 |          699 |     699.9
 Monitor       |         299.99 |     300 |       299.0 |        300 |          299 |     299.9
 Keyboard      |          79.99 |      80 |        79.0 |         80 |           79 |      79.9
 Headphones    |          49.99 |      50 |        49.0 |         50 |           49 |      49.9
(5 rows)
Explanation: round() rounds to nearest integer or specified decimals. ceil() always rounds up. floor() always rounds down. trunc() removes decimals without rounding. Choose based on business requirements (pricing, tax calculations, reports).

🎯 Example 3: Power and Root Functions

-- Exponential and root operations
SELECT 
    value,
    sqrt(value) AS square_root,
    |/ value AS square_root_operator,
    power(value, 2) AS squared,
    power(value, 3) AS cubed,
    value ^ 2 AS squared_operator,
    ||/ value AS cube_root
FROM (VALUES (4), (9), (16), (25), (100)) AS t(value)
ORDER BY value;
Result:
 value | square_root | square_root_operator | squared | cubed  | squared_operator | cube_root
-------+-------------+----------------------+---------+--------+------------------+-----------
     4 |           2 |                    2 |      16 |     64 |               16 |  1.587401
     9 |           3 |                    3 |      81 |    729 |               81 |  2.080084
    16 |           4 |                    4 |     256 |   4096 |              256 |  2.519842
    25 |           5 |                    5 |     625 |  15625 |              625 |  2.924018
   100 |          10 |                   10 |   10000 | 1000000|            10000 |  4.641589
(5 rows)
Explanation: sqrt() or |/ calculates square root. power(base, exp) or ^ raises to power. ||/ calculates cube root. Useful for geometric calculations, statistical formulas, compound interest, distance calculations (Pythagorean theorem).

🎯 Example 4: Logarithmic and Exponential Functions

-- Logarithms and exponential growth
SELECT 
    value,
    ln(value) AS natural_log,
    log(value) AS base_10_log,
    exp(1) AS e_constant,
    exp(ln(value)) AS identity_exp_ln,
    power(10, log(value)) AS identity_power_log
FROM (VALUES (1), (10), (100), (1000)) AS t(value)
ORDER BY value;
Result:
 value | natural_log | base_10_log | e_constant | identity_exp_ln | identity_power_log
-------+-------------+-------------+------------+-----------------+--------------------
     1 |    0.000000 |    0.000000 |   2.718282 |        1.000000 |           1.000000
    10 |    2.302585 |    1.000000 |   2.718282 |       10.000000 |          10.000000
   100 |    4.605170 |    2.000000 |   2.718282 |      100.000000 |         100.000000
  1000 |    6.907755 |    3.000000 |   2.718282 |     1000.000000 |        1000.000000
(5 rows)
Explanation: ln() is natural logarithm (base e). log() is base-10 logarithm. exp() raises e to power. Logarithms useful for growth calculations, scaling, decibels, pH. exp(ln(x)) = x identity. Common in scientific, financial calculations.

🎯 Example 5: Random Numbers and Trigonometry

-- Generate random data and trigonometric values
SELECT 
    -- Random numbers
    random() AS random_0_1,
    random() * 100 AS random_0_100,
    floor(random() * 100 + 1)::int AS random_int_1_100,
    
    -- Trigonometric functions (45 degrees = π/4 radians)
    degrees(pi()/4) AS angle_degrees,
    sin(pi()/4) AS sine_45deg,
    cos(pi()/4) AS cosine_45deg,
    tan(pi()/4) AS tangent_45deg,
    pi() AS pi_constant;
Result:
   random_0_1    | random_0_100 | random_int_1_100 | angle_degrees | sine_45deg | cosine_45deg | tangent_45deg | pi_constant
-----------------+--------------+------------------+---------------+------------+--------------+---------------+-------------
 0.4782...       |    47.82...  |               73 |            45 |   0.707107 |     0.707107 |      1.000000 |    3.141593
(1 row)
Explanation: random() generates float 0-1. Scale and shift for ranges: random() * (max-min) + min. floor() converts to integer. Trigonometric functions use radians. pi() returns π. degrees()/radians() convert angles. Useful for sampling, simulations, geometry, game development.

9.4 String Functions and Operators

String functions: Powerful text manipulation capabilities. Concatenation (||, concat()), case conversion (upper(), lower(), initcap()), substring extraction (substring(), left(), right()), length (length(), char_length()), trimming (trim(), ltrim(), rtrim()), padding (lpad(), rpad()), position finding (position(), strpos()), replacement (replace(), overlay()), splitting (split_part(), string_to_array()), and formatting (format()).

Common String Functions
-- Concatenation
string1 || string2                    -- Concatenate
concat(str1, str2, ...)               -- Concatenate with NULL handling

-- Case conversion
upper(string), lower(string), initcap(string)

-- Substring
substring(string FROM start FOR length)
left(string, n), right(string, n)

-- Length and trimming
length(string), char_length(string)
trim([LEADING|TRAILING|BOTH] chars FROM string)
ltrim(string, chars), rtrim(string, chars)

-- Padding
lpad(string, length, fill), rpad(string, length, fill)

-- Search and replace
position(substring IN string), strpos(string, substring)
replace(string, from, to)
overlay(string PLACING replacement FROM start FOR length)

-- Splitting
split_part(string, delimiter, field_number)
string_to_array(string, delimiter)

🎯 Example 1: String Concatenation and Case Conversion

-- Build full names and format text
SELECT 
    first_name,
    last_name,
    first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name,
