          7 | Coffee Maker   | Appliances
          8 | Microwave      | Appliances
(3 rows)
Explanation: ANY/SOME test if expression operator relation holds for at least one array element. ANY and SOME are identical - use either. Works with all comparison operators. = ANY(array) similar to IN. > ANY means "greater than minimum". <> ANY means "not equal to at least one" (usually true). Use for flexible matching conditions.

9.25.4 ALL (array)

ALL: expression operator ALL(array). Returns true if comparison is true for all array elements. Returns true for empty arrays. Works with =, <>, <, <=, >, >=. Useful for "matches all of" logic, range checking. <> ALL(array) equivalent to NOT IN. > ALL finds values greater than maximum.

🎯 Example 1: ALL with Greater Than

-- Products priced higher than all listed thresholds
SELECT 
    product_id,
    product_name,
    price
FROM products
WHERE price > ALL(ARRAY[50, 100, 200])
ORDER BY price DESC
LIMIT 3;
Result:
 product_id | product_name | price
------------+--------------+--------
          1 | Laptop       | 999.99
          2 | Smartphone   | 699.99
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 2: ALL with Not Equal (NOT IN equivalent)

-- Products not matching any of these IDs
SELECT 
    product_id,
    product_name,
    category
FROM products
WHERE product_id <> ALL(ARRAY[1, 2, 3, 5])
ORDER BY product_id
LIMIT 4;
Result:
 product_id |  product_name  |  category
------------+----------------+------------
          4 | Desk Chair     | Furniture
          6 | Running Shoes  | Sports
          7 | Coffee Maker   | Appliances
          8 | Microwave      | Appliances
(4 rows)

🎯 Example 3: ALL with Less Than

-- Products cheaper than all luxury items
SELECT 
    product_id,
    product_name,
    price
FROM products
WHERE price < ALL(ARRAY[500.00, 700.00, 1000.00])
ORDER BY price DESC
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 product_id |  product_name  | price
------------+----------------+--------
          4 | Desk Chair     | 199.99
          6 | Running Shoes  | 89.99
          7 | Coffee Maker   | 79.99
          8 | Microwave      | 149.99
          9 | Basketball     | 29.99
(5 rows)

🎯 Example 4: ALL with Equality (rarely useful)

-- Check if value equals all array elements (all same)
SELECT 
    'Electronics' = ALL(ARRAY['Electronics', 'Electronics']) AS all_electronics,
    'Electronics' = ALL(ARRAY['Electronics', 'Sports']) AS mixed_categories;
Result:
 all_electronics | mixed_categories
-----------------+------------------
 t               | f
(1 row)

🎯 Example 5: ALL with Empty Array

-- ALL returns true for empty arrays
SELECT 
    100 > ALL(ARRAY[]::int[]) AS greater_than_empty,
    100 < ALL(ARRAY[]::int[]) AS less_than_empty,
    'test' = ALL(ARRAY[]::text[]) AS equals_empty;
Result:
 greater_than_empty | less_than_empty | equals_empty
--------------------+-----------------+--------------
 t                  | t               | t
(1 row)
Explanation: ALL requires expression operator relation to hold for every array element. Returns true for empty arrays (vacuous truth). > ALL means "greater than maximum". < ALL means "less than minimum". = ALL means "equal to all" (rarely useful). <> ALL equivalent to NOT IN. Use for range validation, exclusion filters.

9.25.5 Row Constructor Comparison

Row constructor comparison: (expr1, expr2, ...) operator (expr1, expr2, ...). Lexicographic ordering: compares first elements, if equal compares second, etc. Operators: =, <>, <, <=, >, >=. Useful for multi-column ordering, compound comparisons, pagination cursors.

🎯 Example: Row Constructor Comparison

-- Row constructor comparisons
SELECT 
    order_id,
    order_date,
    total_amount
FROM orders
WHERE (order_date, order_id) > (DATE '2024-01-02', 2)
ORDER BY order_date, order_id
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 order_id | order_date | total_amount
----------+------------+--------------
        3 | 2024-01-03 |       500.00
        4 | 2024-01-04 |       350.00
        5 | 2024-01-05 |      1500.00
        6 | 2024-01-06 |       250.00
        7 | 2024-01-07 |       750.00
(5 rows)
Explanation: Row constructors compared lexicographically (like dictionary ordering). (date, id) > (cutoff_date, cutoff_id) compares date first, if equal compares id. Essential for keyset pagination (avoiding OFFSET), multi-column range queries, compound ordering.

9.25.6 Composite Type Comparison

Composite type comparison: Compare entire rows as composite types. Equality (=, <>) compares all fields. Ordering (<, >, <=, >=) uses field-by-field lexicographic comparison. IS NOT DISTINCT FROM for NULL-safe comparison. Useful for comparing complex objects, detecting changes, row equality.

🎯 Example: Composite Type Comparison

-- Composite row comparison
SELECT 
    o1.order_id AS order1,
    o2.order_id AS order2,
    ROW(o1.customer_id, o1.total_amount) = ROW(o2.customer_id, o2.total_amount) AS same_customer_amount,
    o1 IS DISTINCT FROM o2 AS rows_different
FROM orders o1, orders o2
WHERE o1.order_id = 1 AND o2.order_id IN (1, 2);
Result:
 order1 | order2 | same_customer_amount | rows_different
--------+--------+----------------------+----------------
      1 |      1 | t                    | f
      1 |      2 | f                    | t
(2 rows)
Explanation: ROW() creates composite value. Compare entire records with =, <>. IS DISTINCT FROM handles NULLs (NULL = NULL is FALSE, but NULL IS NOT DISTINCT FROM NULL is TRUE). Useful for detecting changes, comparing snapshots, equality tests on complex types.

9.26 Set Returning Functions

Set returning functions (SRFs): Functions returning multiple rows. generate_series() (numeric/date sequences), unnest() (expand arrays), json_each/jsonb_each() (expand JSON), regexp_matches() (all regex matches), generate_subscripts() (array indices). Use in FROM clause or WITH ORDINALITY for row numbers. Essential for generating test data, expanding arrays, sequence generation.

🎯 Example: Set Returning Functions

-- Various set returning functions
-- Generate series
SELECT * FROM generate_series(1, 5) AS n;

-- Generate date series
SELECT * FROM generate_series(
    DATE '2024-01-01', 
    DATE '2024-01-05', 
    INTERVAL '1 day'
) AS date_series;

-- Unnest array
SELECT unnest(ARRAY['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']) AS fruit;

-- With ordinality (row numbers)
SELECT * FROM unnest(ARRAY['a', 'b', 'c']) WITH ORDINALITY AS t(letter, position);
Result:
-- generate_series result:
 n
---
 1
 2
 3
 4
 5

-- date series result:
 date_series
-------------
 2024-01-01
 2024-01-02
 2024-01-03
 2024-01-04
 2024-01-05

-- unnest result:
  fruit
---------
 apple
 banana
 cherry

-- with ordinality result:
 letter | position
--------+----------
 a      |        1
 b      |        2
 c      |        3
Explanation: generate_series() creates sequences (numbers, dates, timestamps). unnest() expands arrays to rows. WITH ORDINALITY adds row numbers. Use for test data generation, calendar tables, expanding denormalized data, joining with sequences. Essential for data generation, analysis, ETL processes.

9.27 System Information Functions and Operators

System information functions: Query database metadata, session info, privileges, catalog data. Functions grouped by category: session info, access control, schema visibility, catalog inspection, object addressing, comments, validation, transactions, versions. Essential for administration, security auditing, metadata queries, dynamic SQL generation.

9.27.1 Session Information Functions

Session information: current_database(), current_schema(), current_user, session_user, user (current user), inet_client_addr() (client IP), inet_server_addr() (server IP), pg_backend_pid() (process ID), version() (PostgreSQL version). Essential for auditing, logging, connection tracking, multi-tenant systems.

🎯 Example: Session Information

-- Session metadata
SELECT 
    current_database() AS database_name,
    current_schema() AS default_schema,
    current_user AS current_user_name,
    session_user AS session_user_name,
    inet_client_addr() AS client_ip,
    inet_server_addr() AS server_ip,
    pg_backend_pid() AS backend_process_id,
    version() AS postgres_version;
Result:
 database_name | default_schema | current_user_name | session_user_name | client_ip  | server_ip  | backend_process_id | postgres_version
---------------+----------------+-------------------+-------------------+------------+------------+--------------------+------------------
 mydb          | public         | postgres          | postgres          | 127.0.0.1  | 127.0.0.1  | 12345              | PostgreSQL 17.0...
(1 row)
Explanation: Session functions provide runtime context. current_user shows effective user (respects SET ROLE). session_user shows authenticated user. inet_client_addr() useful for auditing. pg_backend_pid() for identifying processes. version() returns full version string. Essential for multi-tenant apps, audit trails, connection monitoring.

9.27.2 Access Privilege Inquiry Functions

🎯 Example: Privilege Checks

-- Check privileges
SELECT 
    has_table_privilege('postgres', 'products', 'SELECT') AS can_select_products,
    has_database_privilege(current_user, current_database(), 'CREATE') AS can_create_tables,
    has_schema_privilege('public', 'USAGE') AS can_use_public_schema;
Result:
 can_select_products | can_create_tables | can_use_public_schema
---------------------+-------------------+-----------------------
 t                   | t                 | t
(1 row)
Explanation: has_*_privilege() functions check permissions. has_table_privilege() for table access. has_database_privilege() for database operations. has_schema_privilege() for schema usage. Use for dynamic authorization, feature toggles based on permissions, security validation.

9.27.3 Schema Visibility Inquiry Functions

🎯 Example: Schema Visibility

-- Check object visibility
SELECT 
    pg_table_is_visible('products'::regclass) AS products_visible,
    pg_function_is_visible('upper'::regproc) AS upper_function_visible,
    current_schemas(true) AS search_path_with_implicit,
    current_schemas(false) AS search_path_explicit;
Result:
 products_visible | upper_function_visible | search_path_with_implicit | search_path_explicit
------------------+------------------------+---------------------------+----------------------
 t                | t                      | {pg_catalog,public}       | {public}
(1 row)
Explanation: pg_*_is_visible() checks if object in search path. current_schemas() returns schema search order. Implicit includes system schemas (pg_catalog). Use for name resolution debugging, dynamic object lookup, understanding query behavior.

9.27.4 System Catalog Information Functions

🎯 Example: Catalog Queries

-- Query system catalog info
SELECT 
    format_type('int4'::regtype, NULL) AS int_type_name,
    pg_get_userbyid(10) AS user_name_by_oid,
    pg_typeof(123) AS literal_type,
    pg_typeof(ARRAY[1,2,3]) AS array_type,
    col_description('products'::regclass, 1) AS column_1_comment;
Result:
 int_type_name | user_name_by_oid | literal_type | array_type | column_1_comment
---------------+------------------+--------------+------------+------------------
 integer       | postgres         | integer      | integer[]  | Product ID
(1 row)
Explanation: format_type() formats type names. pg_get_userbyid() resolves OIDs to names. pg_typeof() returns expression type. col_description() retrieves column comments. Essential for metadata browsing, documentation generation, introspection.

9.27.5 Object Information and Addressing

🎯 Example: Object Addressing

-- Object identification
SELECT 
    'products'::regclass AS table_oid,
    'products'::regclass::oid AS numeric_oid,
    pg_relation_filepath('products'::regclass) AS file_path,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size('products'::regclass)) AS total_size;
Result:
 table_oid | numeric_oid |    file_path    | total_size
-----------+-------------+-----------------+------------
 products  |       16385 | base/16384/16385| 8192 bytes
(1 row)
Explanation: regclass/regproc types convert names to OIDs. pg_relation_filepath() shows physical file. pg_total_relation_size() includes indexes, TOAST. Use for space analysis, physical location tracking, performance monitoring.

9.27.6 Comment Information Functions

Comment information: Functions to retrieve comments/descriptions on database objects. obj_description(oid, catalog_name) gets comment on object. col_description(table_oid, column_number) gets column comment. shobj_description(oid, catalog_name) for shared objects. Comments stored in pg_description catalog. Useful for documentation, metadata retrieval.

🎯 Example 1: Table Comments

-- Get table comments
SELECT 
    c.relname AS table_name,
    obj_description(c.oid, 'pg_class') AS table_comment
FROM pg_class c
WHERE c.relname IN ('products', 'customers', 'orders')
    AND c.relkind = 'r'
ORDER BY c.relname;
Result:
 table_name | table_comment
------------+-------------------------------
 customers  | Customer information table
 orders     | Order header table
 products   | Product catalog
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 2: Column Comments

-- Get column descriptions
SELECT 
    a.attnum AS column_number,
    a.attname AS column_name,
    col_description('products'::regclass, a.attnum) AS column_comment
FROM pg_attribute a
WHERE a.attrelid = 'products'::regclass
    AND a.attnum > 0
    AND NOT a.attisdropped
ORDER BY a.attnum
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 column_number |  column_name  |      column_comment
---------------+---------------+---------------------------
             1 | product_id    | Unique product identifier
             2 | product_name  | Product display name
             3 | category      | Product category
             4 | price         | Product price in USD
             5 | stock         | Available inventory
(5 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Function Comments

-- Get comments on functions
SELECT 
    p.proname AS function_name,
    pg_get_function_identity_arguments(p.oid) AS arguments,
    obj_description(p.oid, 'pg_proc') AS function_comment
FROM pg_proc p
WHERE p.proname LIKE 'array%'
LIMIT 3;
Result:
  function_name  |  arguments   |       function_comment
-----------------+--------------+-------------------------------
 array_append    | anyarray, anyelement | Append element to array
 array_cat       | anyarray, anyarray  | Concatenate arrays
 array_length    | anyarray, integer   | Get array dimension length
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 4: Database Comments

-- Get database comment
SELECT 
    d.datname AS database_name,
    shobj_description(d.oid, 'pg_database') AS database_comment
FROM pg_database d
WHERE d.datname = current_database();
Result:
 database_name |      database_comment
---------------+----------------------------
 ecommerce     | E-commerce application DB
(1 row)

🎯 Example 5: Index Comments

-- Get index comments
SELECT 
    i.indexname AS index_name,
    i.tablename AS table_name,
    obj_description((quote_ident(i.schemaname) || '.' || quote_ident(i.indexname))::regclass, 'pg_class') AS index_comment
FROM pg_indexes i
WHERE i.tablename = 'products'
LIMIT 3;
Result:
       index_name        | table_name |       index_comment
-------------------------+------------+---------------------------
 products_pkey           | products   | Primary key index
 products_category_idx   | products   | Category search index
 products_name_idx       | products   | Product name search
(3 rows)
Explanation: Comment functions retrieve documentation stored on database objects. obj_description() for regular objects, shobj_description() for shared objects (databases, roles), col_description() for columns. Comments set via COMMENT ON command. Use for self-documenting databases, generating documentation, metadata discovery.

9.27.7 Data Validity Checking Functions

Data validity: Functions to verify data integrity and structure. pg_column_is_updatable() checks if column can be updated. pg_relation_is_updatable() checks if relation is updatable. format_type() formats type name for display. Useful for permissions checking, view updatability, type introspection, validation logic.

🎯 Example 1: Check Column Updatability

-- Check if columns are updatable
SELECT 
    column_name,
    pg_column_is_updatable('products'::regclass, attnum, false) AS is_updatable
FROM information_schema.columns c
JOIN pg_attribute a ON a.attname = c.column_name AND a.attrelid = 'products'::regclass
WHERE table_name = 'products'
ORDER BY ordinal_position
LIMIT 5;
Result:
  column_name  | is_updatable
---------------+--------------
 product_id    | t
 product_name  | t
 category      | t
 price         | t
 stock         | t
(5 rows)

🎯 Example 2: Check Relation Updatability

-- Check if relation allows updates
SELECT 
    c.relname AS relation_name,
    c.relkind AS kind,
    pg_relation_is_updatable(c.oid, false) AS updatable_flags,
    (pg_relation_is_updatable(c.oid, false) & 4) > 0 AS allows_update,
    (pg_relation_is_updatable(c.oid, false) & 8) > 0 AS allows_insert
FROM pg_class c
WHERE c.relname IN ('products', 'order_summary_view')
ORDER BY c.relname;
Result:
 relation_name     | kind | updatable_flags | allows_update | allows_insert
-------------------+------+-----------------+---------------+---------------
 order_summary_view| v    |               0 | f             | f
 products          | r    |              28 | t             | t
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Format Type Names

-- Format data types for display
SELECT 
    a.attname AS column_name,
    format_type(a.atttypid, a.atttypmod) AS formatted_type,
    pg_catalog.format_type(a.atttypid, NULL) AS base_type
FROM pg_attribute a
WHERE a.attrelid = 'products'::regclass
    AND a.attnum > 0
    AND NOT a.attisdropped
ORDER BY a.attnum
LIMIT 5;
Result:
  column_name  | formatted_type        | base_type
---------------+-----------------------+-----------
 product_id    | integer               | integer
 product_name  | character varying(100)| character varying
 category      | character varying(50) | character varying
 price         | numeric(10,2)         | numeric
 stock         | integer               | integer
(5 rows)

🎯 Example 4: Check View Updatability Detail

-- Detailed updatability check for views
WITH view_check AS (
    SELECT 
        c.relname,
        pg_relation_is_updatable(c.oid, true) AS flags
    FROM pg_class c
    WHERE c.relkind = 'v' AND c.relname LIKE '%_view'
    LIMIT 1
)
SELECT 
    relname,
    (flags & 4) > 0 AS updatable,
    (flags & 8) > 0 AS insertable,
    (flags & 16) > 0 AS deletable
FROM view_check;
Result:
      relname       | updatable | insertable | deletable
--------------------+-----------+------------+-----------
 order_summary_view | f         | f          | f
(1 row)

🎯 Example 5: Column Type Details

-- Get detailed type information
SELECT 
    'varchar(100)'::regtype AS type_name,
    format_type('varchar(100)'::regtype, NULL) AS formatted_no_mod,
    format_type('varchar(100)'::regtype, 104) AS formatted_with_mod;
Result:
    type_name        |  formatted_no_mod   | formatted_with_mod
---------------------+---------------------+------------------------
 character varying   | character varying   | character varying(100)
(1 row)
Explanation: Validity functions check data structure capabilities. pg_relation_is_updatable() returns bitmap: 4=UPDATE, 8=INSERT, 16=DELETE. pg_column_is_updatable() for specific columns. format_type() shows type with modifiers (length, precision). Use for permission systems, ORM tools, schema introspection, updatable view detection.

9.27.8 Transaction ID and Snapshot Information Functions

Transaction tracking: Functions for transaction ID and snapshot information. txid_current() gets current transaction ID. pg_current_xact_id() in newer versions. pg_current_snapshot() gets current snapshot. Useful for MVCC understanding, replication lag monitoring, transaction tracking, debugging visibility issues.

🎯 Example 1: Current Transaction ID

-- Get current transaction information
BEGIN;
SELECT 
    pg_current_xact_id() AS current_xact_id,
    pg_current_xact_id_if_assigned() AS xact_id_if_assigned,
    pg_backend_pid() AS backend_pid;
COMMIT;
Result:
 current_xact_id | xact_id_if_assigned | backend_pid
-----------------+---------------------+-------------
             745 |                 745 |        1234
(1 row)

🎯 Example 2: Snapshot Information

-- Get current snapshot details
SELECT 
    pg_current_snapshot() AS snapshot_info,
    pg_snapshot_xmin(pg_current_snapshot()) AS xmin,
    pg_snapshot_xmax(pg_current_snapshot()) AS xmax;
Result:
  snapshot_info  | xmin | xmax
-----------------+------+------
 745:745:        |  745 |  745
(1 row)

🎯 Example 3: Transaction ID Visibility

-- Check transaction visibility
SELECT 
    pg_current_xact_id() AS my_xact,
    pg_visible_in_snapshot(pg_current_xact_id() - 10, pg_current_snapshot()) AS old_visible,
    pg_visible_in_snapshot(pg_current_xact_id() + 10, pg_current_snapshot()) AS future_visible;
Result:
 my_xact | old_visible | future_visible
---------+-------------+----------------
     745 | t           | f
(1 row)

🎯 Example 4: Transaction Age

-- Check transaction age for table rows
SELECT 
    product_id,
    product_name,
    age(xmin) AS xmin_age,
    xmin::text::bigint AS transaction_id
FROM products
WHERE product_id <= 3
ORDER BY product_id;
Result:
 product_id | product_name | xmin_age | transaction_id
------------+--------------+----------+----------------
          1 | Laptop       |       45 |            700
          2 | Smartphone   |       42 |            703
          3 | Headphones   |       40 |            705
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 5: Snapshot Comparison

-- Compare snapshots
WITH snap1 AS (
    SELECT pg_current_snapshot() AS s1
),
snap2 AS (
    SELECT pg_current_snapshot() AS s2
)
SELECT 
    s1,
    s2,
    pg_snapshot_xmin(s1) AS s1_xmin,
    pg_snapshot_xmax(s2) AS s2_xmax
FROM snap1, snap2;
Result:
       s1        |       s2        | s1_xmin | s2_xmax
-----------------+-----------------+---------+---------
 745:745:        | 746:746:        |     745 |     746
(1 row)
Explanation: Transaction ID functions track MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control). pg_current_xact_id() gets current transaction number. Snapshots determine row visibility. xmin shows transaction that created row. age() calculates transaction age (distance from current). Use for debugging visibility, monitoring wraparound, replication monitoring, understanding MVCC.

9.27.9 Committed Transaction Information Functions

Commit tracking: Functions to query transaction commit information. pg_xact_commit_timestamp() gets commit timestamp. pg_last_committed_xact() gets last committed transaction. Requires track_commit_timestamp = on. Useful for audit trails, replication monitoring, determining data freshness, forensic analysis.

🎯 Example 1: Transaction Commit Timestamp

-- Get commit timestamp for transaction (requires track_commit_timestamp=on)
SELECT 
    pg_current_xact_id() AS current_xact,
    pg_xact_commit_timestamp(pg_current_xact_id() - 5) AS commit_time_5_ago;
Result:
 current_xact |     commit_time_5_ago
--------------+----------------------------
          745 | 2026-01-26 14:30:25.123+00
(1 row)

🎯 Example 2: Last Committed Transaction

-- Get information about last committed transaction
SELECT 
    (pg_last_committed_xact()).xid AS last_xid,
    (pg_last_committed_xact()).timestamp AS last_commit_time;
Result:
 last_xid |      last_commit_time
----------+----------------------------
      744 | 2026-01-26 14:35:10.456+00
(1 row)

🎯 Example 3: Row Creation Time

-- Estimate when rows were created using xmin
SELECT 
    product_id,
    product_name,
    xmin AS creating_xact,
    pg_xact_commit_timestamp(xmin::text::bigint) AS created_at
FROM products
WHERE product_id <= 3
ORDER BY product_id;
Result:
 product_id | product_name | creating_xact |         created_at
------------+--------------+---------------+----------------------------
          1 | Laptop       |           700 | 2026-01-26 10:00:00.000+00
          2 | Smartphone   |           703 | 2026-01-26 10:05:15.234+00
          3 | Headphones   |           705 | 2026-01-26 10:10:30.567+00
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 4: Recent Transaction Activity

-- Find recent committed transactions
WITH recent_xacts AS (
    SELECT 
        generate_series(
            (pg_last_committed_xact()).xid - 5,
            (pg_last_committed_xact()).xid
        ) AS xid
)
SELECT 
    xid,
    pg_xact_commit_timestamp(xid) AS commit_time,
    EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (now() - pg_xact_commit_timestamp(xid))) AS seconds_ago
FROM recent_xacts
WHERE pg_xact_commit_timestamp(xid) IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY xid DESC
LIMIT 3;
Result:
 xid |         commit_time        | seconds_ago
-----+----------------------------+-------------
 744 | 2026-01-26 14:35:10.456+00 |        5.23
 743 | 2026-01-26 14:35:05.123+00 |       10.56
 742 | 2026-01-26 14:35:00.789+00 |       15.89
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 5: Check Commit Tracking Status

-- Verify commit tracking is enabled
SELECT 
    name,
    setting,
    short_desc
FROM pg_settings
WHERE name = 'track_commit_timestamp';
Result:
          name           | setting |           short_desc
-------------------------+---------+--------------------------------
 track_commit_timestamp  | on      | Collects transaction commit time
(1 row)
Explanation: Commit tracking functions require track_commit_timestamp configuration. pg_xact_commit_timestamp() returns when transaction committed. pg_last_committed_xact() shows most recent commit. Can determine row creation time via xmin. Overhead from tracking - enable only when needed. Use for audit logging, data lineage, replication lag calculation, forensics.

9.27.10 Control Data Functions

Control data: Functions to access cluster control information from pg_control file. pg_control_checkpoint() returns checkpoint information. pg_control_system() returns system identifier info. pg_control_init() returns initialization parameters. pg_control_recovery() returns recovery state. Critical for monitoring, disaster recovery, replication setup.

🎯 Example 1: Checkpoint Information

-- Get current checkpoint information
SELECT 
    checkpoint_lsn,
    redo_lsn,
    checkpoint_time,
    timeline_id
FROM pg_control_checkpoint();
Result:
 checkpoint_lsn |  redo_lsn   |      checkpoint_time       | timeline_id
----------------+-------------+----------------------------+-------------
 0/1A2B3C4D     | 0/1A2B3C00  | 2026-01-26 14:30:00.000+00 |           1
(1 row)

🎯 Example 2: System Identifier

-- Get system identifier and catalog version
SELECT 
    system_identifier,
    pg_control_version,
    catalog_version_no,
    data_page_checksum_version
FROM pg_control_system();
Result:
 system_identifier | pg_control_version | catalog_version_no | data_page_checksum_version
-------------------+--------------------+--------------------+----------------------------
 7123456789012345  |               1300 |          202401261 |                          1
(1 row)

🎯 Example 3: Initialization Parameters

-- Get cluster initialization info
SELECT 
    max_data_alignment,
    database_block_size,
    blocks_per_segment,
    wal_block_size,
    max_identifier_length
FROM pg_control_init();
Result:
 max_data_alignment | database_block_size | blocks_per_segment | wal_block_size | max_identifier_length
--------------------+---------------------+--------------------+----------------+-----------------------
                  8 |                8192 |             131072 |           8192 |                    63
(1 row)

🎯 Example 4: Recovery State

-- Check recovery information
SELECT 
    min_recovery_end_lsn,
    min_recovery_end_timeline,
    backup_start_lsn,
    backup_end_lsn
FROM pg_control_recovery();
Result:
 min_recovery_end_lsn | min_recovery_end_timeline | backup_start_lsn | backup_end_lsn
----------------------+---------------------------+------------------+----------------
 0/0                  |                         0 | 0/0              | 0/0
(1 row)

🎯 Example 5: Complete Control File Overview

-- Comprehensive control file status
SELECT 
    'Checkpoint' AS info_type,
    checkpoint_lsn::text AS value
FROM pg_control_checkpoint()
UNION ALL
SELECT 
    'System ID',
    system_identifier::text
FROM pg_control_system()
UNION ALL
SELECT 
    'Block Size',
    database_block_size::text
FROM pg_control_init();
Result:
  info_type  |      value
-------------+------------------
 Checkpoint  | 0/1A2B3C4D
 System ID   | 7123456789012345
 Block Size  | 8192
(3 rows)
Explanation: Control data functions read pg_control file (cluster metadata). Checkpoint info shows WAL positions. System identifier uniquely identifies cluster. Init parameters show compile-time constants. Recovery state for standby servers. Essential for replication setup, backup validation, disaster recovery planning, performance tuning.

9.27.11 Version Information Functions

🎯 Example: Version Functions

-- Version details
SELECT 
    version() AS full_version,
    current_setting('server_version') AS version_number,
    current_setting('server_version_num')::int AS version_num_int,
    pg_postmaster_start_time() AS server_start_time,
    pg_conf_load_time() AS config_load_time;
Result:
           full_version            | version_number | version_num_int |   server_start_time    |    config_load_time
---------------------------------------+----------------+-----------------+------------------------+------------------------
 PostgreSQL 17.0 on x86_64-pc-linux..  | 17.0           |          170000 | 2026-01-26 10:00:00+00 | 2026-01-26 10:00:01+00
(1 row)
Explanation: version() returns full server info. server_version setting for numeric version. server_version_num for programmatic comparison. pg_postmaster_start_time() shows last restart. Essential for compatibility checks, monitoring uptime, version-specific behavior.

9.27.12 WAL Summarization Information Functions

WAL summary: Functions for Write-Ahead Log summary information. pg_wal_summary_contents() examines WAL summary files. pg_get_wal_summarizer_state() shows summarizer status. Useful for incremental backup, understanding WAL activity, monitoring write patterns, replication diagnostics. Available in PostgreSQL 17+.

🎯 Example 1: WAL Summarizer State

-- Check WAL summarizer status
SELECT 
    summarized_tli AS timeline,
    summarized_lsn AS last_summarized_lsn,
    pending_lsn AS pending_until_lsn,
    summarizer_pid
FROM pg_get_wal_summarizer_state();
Result:
 timeline | last_summarized_lsn | pending_until_lsn | summarizer_pid
----------+---------------------+-------------------+----------------
        1 | 0/1A2B3C4D          | 0/1A2B4000        |           5678
(1 row)

🎯 Example 2: WAL Summary File List

-- List WAL summary files
SELECT 
    name,
    size,
    modification AS last_modified
FROM pg_ls_waldir()
WHERE name LIKE '%.summary'
ORDER BY modification DESC
LIMIT 3;
Result:
           name           |  size  |     last_modified
--------------------------+--------+------------------------
 000000010000000000000005.summary |   4096 | 2026-01-26 14:00:00
 000000010000000000000004.summary |   4096 | 2026-01-26 13:50:00
 000000010000000000000003.summary |   4096 | 2026-01-26 13:40:00
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Current WAL Position

-- Get current WAL location for comparison
SELECT 
    pg_current_wal_lsn() AS current_lsn,
    pg_walfile_name(pg_current_wal_lsn()) AS current_wal_file,
    pg_wal_lsn_diff(
        pg_current_wal_lsn(),
        (SELECT summarized_lsn FROM pg_get_wal_summarizer_state())
    ) AS bytes_unsummarized;
Result:
 current_lsn  |     current_wal_file      | bytes_unsummarized
--------------+---------------------------+--------------------
 0/1A2B4500   | 000000010000000000000006  |               1283
(1 row)

🎯 Example 4: WAL Activity Rate

-- Calculate WAL generation rate
WITH wal_stats AS (
    SELECT 
        pg_current_wal_lsn() AS current_lsn,
        pg_postmaster_start_time() AS start_time
)
SELECT 
    current_lsn,
    pg_size_pretty(
        pg_wal_lsn_diff(current_lsn, '0/0')
    ) AS total_wal_generated,
    pg_size_pretty(
        pg_wal_lsn_diff(current_lsn, '0/0')::numeric / 
        EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (now() - start_time))
    ) AS wal_per_second
FROM wal_stats;
Result:
 current_lsn  | total_wal_generated | wal_per_second
--------------+---------------------+----------------
 0/1A2B4500   | 26 MB               | 1456 bytes
(1 row)

🎯 Example 5: WAL Summary Configuration

-- Check WAL summarization settings
SELECT 
    name,
    setting,
    unit,
    short_desc
FROM pg_settings
WHERE name LIKE '%wal_summar%'
ORDER BY name;
Result:
          name           | setting | unit |          short_desc
-------------------------+---------+------+-------------------------------
 wal_summarizer          | on      |      | Enables WAL summarization
 wal_summary_keep_time   | 10      | d    | Days to retain WAL summaries
(2 rows)
Explanation: WAL summary functions support incremental backup (PostgreSQL 17+). pg_get_wal_summarizer_state() shows background process status. Summary files track changed blocks for efficient incremental backups. pg_wal_lsn_diff() calculates WAL distances. Use for backup planning, monitoring WAL generation rate, incremental backup setup, replication lag analysis.

9.28 System Administration Functions

System administration functions: Database management operations. Configuration: current_setting(), set_config(). Signaling: pg_cancel_backend(), pg_terminate_backend(). Backup/Recovery: pg_start_backup(), pg_stop_backup(). Replication: pg_create_physical_replication_slot(). Maintenance: pg_relation_size(), pg_stat_reset(). Advisory locks: pg_advisory_lock(). Requires superuser or appropriate privileges.

9.28.1 Configuration Settings Functions

Configuration functions: current_setting(name) gets parameter value. set_config(name, value, is_local) sets parameter. pg_reload_conf() reloads config files. Scope: session, transaction, local. Essential for runtime configuration, feature flags, dynamic tuning.

🎯 Example: Configuration Management

-- Configuration operations
SELECT 
    current_setting('max_connections') AS max_connections,
    current_setting('work_mem') AS work_mem,
    set_config('work_mem', '16MB', false) AS new_work_mem_session,
    set_config('work_mem', '32MB', true) AS new_work_mem_transaction;
Result:
 max_connections | work_mem | new_work_mem_session | new_work_mem_transaction
-----------------+----------+----------------------+--------------------------
 100             | 4MB      | 16MB                 | 32MB
(1 row)
Explanation: current_setting() reads configuration. set_config() modifies settings (session or transaction scope). false = session-wide, true = transaction-only. Use for dynamic tuning, temporary performance adjustments, debugging. Some parameters require superuser.

9.28.2 Server Signaling Functions

Signaling functions: pg_cancel_backend(pid) cancels query. pg_terminate_backend(pid) terminates session. pg_reload_conf() reloads config. Requires superuser or pg_signal_backend role. Essential for killing runaway queries, connection management, graceful shutdown.

🎯 Example: Backend Management

-- View active backends (for demonstration)
SELECT 
    pid,
    usename,
    datname,
    state,
    query_start,
    left(query, 50) AS query_preview
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE state = 'active'
  AND pid <> pg_backend_pid()  -- Exclude current session
ORDER BY query_start
LIMIT 3;

-- To cancel: SELECT pg_cancel_backend(pid);
-- To terminate: SELECT pg_terminate_backend(pid);
Result:
  pid  | usename  | datname |  state | query_start         | query_preview
-------+----------+---------+--------+---------------------+-------------------
 12346 | app_user | mydb    | active | 2026-01-26 14:30:00 | SELECT * FROM large_table WHERE...
(1 row)
Explanation: pg_stat_activity shows active connections. pg_cancel_backend() gracefully cancels query (like Ctrl+C). pg_terminate_backend() forcibly disconnects. Use for killing runaway queries, freeing locks, connection cleanup. Requires appropriate privileges.

9.28.3 Backup Control Functions

Backup control: Functions for managing physical backups. pg_backup_start() begins backup (formerly pg_start_backup). pg_backup_stop() completes backup. pg_switch_wal() forces WAL segment switch. pg_walfile_name() converts LSN to filename. Essential for hot backups, PITR setup, backup scripts, disaster recovery.

🎯 Example 1: Backup Start and Stop

-- Initiate a backup (requires superuser or pg_backup role)
SELECT 
    pg_backup_start('daily_backup', false) AS backup_start_lsn,
    pg_current_wal_lsn() AS current_lsn;
Result:
 backup_start_lsn | current_lsn
------------------+-------------
 0/1A2B3C4D       | 0/1A2B3C50
(1 row)

🎯 Example 2: Complete Backup Process

-- Complete backup workflow
BEGIN;
SELECT pg_backup_start('my_backup') AS start_lsn;
-- (Copy data files here)
SELECT 
    lsn AS stop_lsn,
    labelfile,
    spcmapfile
FROM pg_backup_stop(false);
COMMIT;
Result:
    stop_lsn    | labelfile | spcmapfile
----------------+-----------+------------
 0/1A2B4000     | START...  | (null)
(1 row)

🎯 Example 3: WAL Segment Management

-- Force WAL segment switch and get filename
SELECT 
    pg_switch_wal() AS new_segment_lsn,
    pg_walfile_name(pg_switch_wal()) AS new_wal_filename;
Result:
 new_segment_lsn |       new_wal_filename
-----------------+---------------------------
 0/2000000       | 000000010000000000000002
(1 row)

🎯 Example 4: Calculate WAL Files for Backup

-- Determine WAL files needed for backup
WITH backup_info AS (
    SELECT 
        '0/1A2B3C4D'::pg_lsn AS start_lsn,
        '0/1A2B4000'::pg_lsn AS stop_lsn
)
SELECT 
    pg_walfile_name(start_lsn) AS first_wal_file,
    pg_walfile_name(stop_lsn) AS last_wal_file,
    pg_wal_lsn_diff(stop_lsn, start_lsn) AS bytes_between
FROM backup_info;
Result:
      first_wal_file       |       last_wal_file       | bytes_between
---------------------------+---------------------------+---------------
 000000010000000000000001  | 000000010000000000000001  |          1331
(1 row)

🎯 Example 5: Check Backup Status

-- Verify if backup is in progress
SELECT 
    pg_is_in_backup() AS backup_in_progress,
    pg_backup_start_time() AS backup_started_at,
    pg_current_wal_lsn() AS current_position;
Result:
 backup_in_progress |   backup_started_at    | current_position
--------------------+------------------------+------------------
 f                  | NULL                   | 0/1A2B4500
(1 row)
Explanation: Backup functions coordinate hot backups. pg_backup_start() creates checkpoint, returns LSN. Copy data files between start/stop. pg_backup_stop() returns backup label with metadata. pg_switch_wal() forces new segment for archiving. pg_walfile_name() converts LSN to filename. Essential for reliable backup procedures without downtime.

9.28.4 Recovery Control Functions

Recovery control: Functions for managing recovery and promotion. pg_is_in_recovery() checks if standby mode. pg_promote() promotes standby to primary. pg_wal_replay_pause()/pg_wal_replay_resume() control replay. pg_last_wal_receive_lsn() shows received WAL. Critical for failover, replication management, controlled promotion.

🎯 Example 1: Check Recovery Status

-- Determine if server is in recovery
SELECT 
    pg_is_in_recovery() AS is_standby,
    CASE 
        WHEN pg_is_in_recovery() THEN pg_last_wal_receive_lsn()
        ELSE pg_current_wal_lsn()
    END AS wal_position,
    pg_is_wal_replay_paused() AS replay_paused;
Result:
 is_standby | wal_position | replay_paused
------------+--------------+---------------
 f          | 0/1A2B4500   | f
(1 row)

🎯 Example 2: Standby Replication Lag

-- Calculate replication lag on standby
SELECT 
    pg_last_wal_receive_lsn() AS received_lsn,
    pg_last_wal_replay_lsn() AS replayed_lsn,
    pg_wal_lsn_diff(
        pg_last_wal_receive_lsn(),
        pg_last_wal_replay_lsn()
    ) AS replay_lag_bytes,
    pg_last_xact_replay_timestamp() AS last_replay_time;
Result:
 received_lsn | replayed_lsn | replay_lag_bytes |   last_replay_time
--------------+--------------+------------------+------------------------
 0/1A2B4500   | 0/1A2B4400   |              256 | 2026-01-26 14:35:00+00
(1 row)

🎯 Example 3: Pause and Resume Recovery

-- Pause WAL replay (on standby)
SELECT pg_wal_replay_pause();

-- Check status
SELECT 
    pg_is_wal_replay_paused() AS is_paused,
    pg_last_wal_replay_lsn() AS paused_at_lsn;

-- Resume replay
SELECT pg_wal_replay_resume();
Result:
 is_paused | paused_at_lsn
-----------+---------------
 t         | 0/1A2B4400
(1 row)

🎯 Example 4: Recovery Timeline Information

-- Get recovery timeline details
SELECT 
    pg_current_wal_lsn() AS current_lsn,
    (SELECT timeline_id FROM pg_control_checkpoint()) AS timeline,
    pg_postmaster_start_time() AS server_started;
Result:
 current_lsn  | timeline |     server_started
--------------+----------+------------------------
 0/1A2B4500   |        1 | 2026-01-26 10:00:00+00
(1 row)

🎯 Example 5: Promote Standby (Critical Operation)

-- Promote standby to primary (use with caution!)
-- Only run on standby servers during failover
SELECT 
    pg_is_in_recovery() AS is_standby,
    CASE 
        WHEN pg_is_in_recovery() THEN 'Ready to promote'
        ELSE 'Already primary'
    END AS promotion_status;

-- To actually promote (commented for safety):
-- SELECT pg_promote();
Result:
 is_standby | promotion_status
------------+------------------
 f          | Already primary
(1 row)
Explanation: Recovery functions manage standby servers. pg_is_in_recovery() true on standbys. pg_last_wal_replay_lsn() shows applied WAL. Pause replay to create consistent snapshot. pg_promote() converts standby to primary (irreversible). Monitor replication lag with LSN diff. Essential for high availability, disaster recovery, planned failovers.

9.28.5 Snapshot Synchronization Functions

Snapshot sync: Functions for exporting and importing transaction snapshots. pg_export_snapshot() exports current snapshot. SET TRANSACTION SNAPSHOT imports it. Enables consistent reads across sessions, parallel data loading, coordinated backups. Snapshot identifier format: epochno-sessionid-seqno.

🎯 Example 1: Export Snapshot

-- Export snapshot in session 1
BEGIN TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;
SELECT pg_export_snapshot() AS snapshot_id;
Result:
       snapshot_id
-----------------------
 00000003-00000001-1
(1 row)

🎯 Example 2: Import Snapshot (Different Session)

-- Use exported snapshot in session 2
BEGIN TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;
SET TRANSACTION SNAPSHOT '00000003-00000001-1';

-- Now this session sees same data as session 1
SELECT count(*) FROM products;
Result:
 count
-------
    10
(1 row)

🎯 Example 3: Coordinated Parallel Export

-- Session 1: Start transaction and export
BEGIN TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;
SELECT 
    txid_current() AS transaction_id,
    pg_export_snapshot() AS snapshot_id,
    now() AS export_time;
Result:
 transaction_id |    snapshot_id     |       export_time
----------------+--------------------+------------------------
            750 | 00000003-00000001-1| 2026-01-26 14:40:00+00
(1 row)

🎯 Example 4: Snapshot for Consistent Backup

-- Export snapshot for pg_dump coordination
BEGIN TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL REPEATABLE READ;
SELECT 
    pg_export_snapshot() AS snapshot_for_dump,
    pg_current_wal_lsn() AS wal_position;

-- External tool can use: pg_dump --snapshot=00000003-00000001-1
Result:
  snapshot_for_dump  | wal_position
---------------------+--------------
 00000003-00000001-1 | 0/1A2B4500
(1 row)

🎯 Example 5: Snapshot Lifetime

-- Check transaction isolation level
SHOW transaction_isolation;

-- Export snapshot (only works in REPEATABLE READ or SERIALIZABLE)
SELECT 
    current_setting('transaction_isolation') AS isolation_level,
    CASE 
        WHEN current_setting('transaction_isolation') IN ('repeatable read', 'serializable')
        THEN 'Can export snapshot'
        ELSE 'Need REPEATABLE READ or SERIALIZABLE'
    END AS snapshot_capability;
Result:
 isolation_level  |  snapshot_capability
------------------+----------------------
 read committed   | Need REPEATABLE READ or SERIALIZABLE
(1 row)
Explanation: Snapshot export enables consistent reads across sessions. pg_export_snapshot() generates identifier valid until exporting transaction ends. Other sessions import with SET TRANSACTION SNAPSHOT. Requires REPEATABLE READ or SERIALIZABLE isolation. Use for parallel pg_dump, consistent multi-table exports, coordinated reporting, distributed transactions across connections.

9.28.6 Replication Management Functions

Replication management: Functions for replication slots and origins. pg_create_physical_replication_slot() creates physical slot. pg_create_logical_replication_slot() for logical. pg_drop_replication_slot() removes slots. pg_replication_slot_advance() moves slot forward. Essential for streaming replication, logical decoding, preventing WAL deletion.

🎯 Example 1: List Replication Slots

-- View existing replication slots
SELECT 
    slot_name,
    slot_type,
    database,
    active,
    restart_lsn,
    confirmed_flush_lsn
FROM pg_replication_slots
ORDER BY slot_name;
Result:
  slot_name  | slot_type | database | active | restart_lsn | confirmed_flush_lsn
-------------+-----------+----------+--------+-------------+---------------------
 standby_01  | physical  | NULL     | t      | 0/1A2B4000  | NULL
 logical_01  | logical   | mydb     | t      | 0/1A2B4200  | 0/1A2B4400
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 2: Create Physical Replication Slot

-- Create slot for physical replication (standby)
SELECT 
    slot_name,
    lsn
FROM pg_create_physical_replication_slot('standby_02');
Result:
  slot_name  |     lsn
-------------+-------------
 standby_02  | 0/1A2B4500
(1 row)

🎯 Example 3: Create Logical Replication Slot

-- Create slot for logical replication
SELECT 
    slot_name,
    lsn
FROM pg_create_logical_replication_slot('logical_02', 'pgoutput');
Result:
  slot_name  |     lsn
-------------+-------------
 logical_02  | 0/1A2B4600
(1 row)

🎯 Example 4: Check Replication Lag

-- Calculate WAL retention for slots
SELECT 
    slot_name,
    slot_type,
    pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn) AS lag_bytes,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn)) AS lag_size,
    active
FROM pg_replication_slots
ORDER BY lag_bytes DESC;
Result:
  slot_name  | slot_type | lag_bytes | lag_size | active
-------------+-----------+-----------+----------+--------
 standby_01  | physical  |       500 | 500 bytes| t
 logical_01  | logical   |       300 | 300 bytes| t
 standby_02  | physical  |         0 | 0 bytes  | f
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 5: Drop Replication Slot

-- Remove inactive replication slot
SELECT pg_drop_replication_slot('standby_02');

-- Verify deletion
SELECT count(*) AS remaining_slots
FROM pg_replication_slots;
Result:
 remaining_slots
-----------------
               2
(1 row)
Explanation: Replication slots prevent WAL deletion until consumed. Physical slots for streaming replication (standbys). Logical slots for logical replication/decoding. restart_lsn shows oldest needed WAL. Inactive slots can cause disk space issues. pg_replication_slot_advance() fast-forwards without reading data. Essential for reliable replication, preventing data loss, managing WAL retention.

9.28.7 Database Object Management Functions

Object management: Functions for database object operations. pg_relation_size() gets table size. pg_total_relation_size() includes indexes and TOAST. pg_database_size() for database size. pg_tablespace_size() for tablespace. pg_size_pretty() formats bytes. Essential for space monitoring, capacity planning, identifying large objects.

🎯 Example 1: Table Size Information

-- Get detailed size breakdown for tables
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    tablename,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(schemaname||'.'||tablename)) AS table_size,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(schemaname||'.'||tablename)) AS total_size,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_indexes_size(schemaname||'.'||tablename)) AS indexes_size
FROM pg_tables
WHERE schemaname = 'public'
ORDER BY pg_total_relation_size(schemaname||'.'||tablename) DESC
LIMIT 3;
Result:
 schemaname | tablename  | table_size | total_size | indexes_size
------------+------------+------------+------------+--------------
 public     | order_items| 256 kB     | 512 kB     | 256 kB
 public     | orders     | 128 kB     | 256 kB     | 128 kB
 public     | customers  | 96 kB      | 192 kB     | 96 kB
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 2: Database Size

-- Get size of current and all databases
SELECT 
    datname AS database_name,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_database_size(datname)) AS size,
    pg_database_size(datname) AS size_bytes
FROM pg_database
WHERE datname = current_database()
   OR datname IN ('postgres', 'template1')
ORDER BY pg_database_size(datname) DESC;
Result:
 database_name |  size   | size_bytes
---------------+---------+------------
 ecommerce     | 15 MB   |   15728640
 postgres      | 8425 kB |    8626176
 template1     | 7833 kB |    8021504
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Index Size Analysis

-- Find largest indexes
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    tablename,
    indexname,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS index_size
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
ORDER BY pg_relation_size(indexrelid) DESC
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 schemaname |  tablename  |       indexname        | index_size
------------+-------------+------------------------+------------
 public     | order_items | order_items_pkey       | 128 kB
 public     | orders      | orders_pkey            | 64 kB
 public     | customers   | customers_pkey         | 48 kB
 public     | products    | products_pkey          | 32 kB
 public     | order_items | order_items_order_idx  | 64 kB
(5 rows)

🎯 Example 4: Tablespace Usage

-- Get tablespace sizes
SELECT 
    spcname AS tablespace_name,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_tablespace_size(spcname)) AS size,
    pg_tablespace_location(oid) AS location
FROM pg_tablespace
ORDER BY pg_tablespace_size(spcname) DESC;
Result:
 tablespace_name |  size   |           location
-----------------+---------+------------------------------
 pg_default      | 25 MB   |
 pg_global       | 512 kB  |
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 5: TOAST Table Size

-- Check TOAST storage for large values
SELECT 
    t.tablename,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(t.tablename::regclass)) AS table_size,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(t.tablename::regclass) - 
                   pg_relation_size(t.tablename::regclass)) AS overhead_size,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(t.tablename::regclass)) AS total_size
FROM pg_tables t
WHERE schemaname = 'public'
ORDER BY pg_total_relation_size(t.tablename::regclass) DESC
LIMIT 3;
Result:
 tablename   | table_size | overhead_size | total_size
-------------+------------+---------------+------------
 order_items | 256 kB     | 256 kB        | 512 kB
 orders      | 128 kB     | 128 kB        | 256 kB
 customers   | 96 kB      | 96 kB         | 192 kB
(3 rows)
Explanation: Size functions measure storage usage. pg_relation_size() measures main table fork. pg_indexes_size() measures all indexes. pg_total_relation_size() includes table, indexes, TOAST, and FSM/VM. pg_size_pretty() formats bytes human-readable. Use for capacity planning, identifying bloat, monitoring growth, optimizing storage. Regular monitoring prevents disk space issues.

9.28.8 Index Maintenance Functions

Index maintenance: Functions for index health and maintenance. pg_stat_user_indexes tracks index usage. pg_index_column_has_property() checks index properties. brin_summarize_new_values() updates BRIN indexes. brin_desummarize_range() removes BRIN summaries. Essential for index optimization, detecting unused indexes, BRIN maintenance.

🎯 Example 1: Index Usage Statistics

-- Find unused indexes
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    tablename,
    indexname,
    idx_scan AS times_used,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS index_size
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE idx_scan = 0
    AND schemaname = 'public'
ORDER BY pg_relation_size(indexrelid) DESC
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 schemaname | tablename |      indexname       | times_used | index_size
------------+-----------+----------------------+------------+------------
 public     | products  | products_stock_idx   |          0 | 32 kB
 public     | customers | customers_email_idx  |          0 | 24 kB
(2 rows)

🎯 Example 2: Index Scan Efficiency

-- Compare index vs sequential scans
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    tablename,
    indexname,
    idx_scan AS index_scans,
    idx_tup_read AS tuples_read,
    idx_tup_fetch AS tuples_fetched,
    CASE 
        WHEN idx_scan > 0 THEN round(idx_tup_fetch::numeric / idx_scan, 2)
        ELSE 0
    END AS avg_tuples_per_scan
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE schemaname = 'public'
ORDER BY idx_scan DESC
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 schemaname | tablename |       indexname        | index_scans | tuples_read | tuples_fetched | avg_tuples_per_scan
------------+-----------+------------------------+-------------+-------------+----------------+---------------------
 public     | products  | products_pkey          |         250 |         250 |            250 |                1.00
 public     | orders    | orders_customer_idx    |         120 |         480 |            450 |                3.75
 public     | order_items| order_items_order_idx |         100 |         350 |            320 |                3.20
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 3: Check Index Properties

-- Examine index column properties
SELECT 
    'products_pkey'::regclass AS index_name,
    pg_index_column_has_property('products_pkey'::regclass, 1, 'asc') AS is_asc,
    pg_index_column_has_property('products_pkey'::regclass, 1, 'nulls_first') AS nulls_first,
    pg_index_column_has_property('products_pkey'::regclass, 1, 'orderable') AS is_orderable,
    pg_indexam_has_property((SELECT i.relam FROM pg_class i WHERE i.relname = 'products_pkey'), 'can_unique') AS supports_unique;
Result:
  index_name   | is_asc | nulls_first | is_orderable | supports_unique
---------------+--------+-------------+--------------+-----------------
 products_pkey | t      | f           | t            | t
(1 row)

🎯 Example 4: Index Bloat Estimate

-- Estimate index bloat
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    tablename,
    indexname,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS index_size,
    idx_scan AS scans,
    idx_tup_read AS tuples_read,
    idx_tup_fetch AS tuples_fetched
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE schemaname = 'public'
ORDER BY pg_relation_size(indexrelid) DESC
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 schemaname | tablename   |       indexname        | index_size | scans | tuples_read | tuples_fetched
------------+-------------+------------------------+------------+-------+-------------+----------------
 public     | order_items | order_items_pkey       | 128 kB     |   250 |         250 |            250
 public     | order_items | order_items_order_idx  | 64 kB      |   100 |         350 |            320
 public     | orders      | orders_pkey            | 64 kB      |   200 |         200 |            200
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 5: Index Access Method Properties

-- Check index AM capabilities
SELECT 
    am.amname AS access_method,
    pg_indexam_has_property(am.oid, 'can_unique') AS supports_unique,
    pg_indexam_has_property(am.oid, 'can_multi_col') AS supports_multi_column,
    pg_indexam_has_property(am.oid, 'can_order') AS supports_ordering
FROM pg_am am
WHERE am.amtype = 'i'
ORDER BY am.amname;
Result:
 access_method | supports_unique | supports_multi_column | supports_ordering
---------------+-----------------+-----------------------+-------------------
 btree         | t               | t                     | t
 hash          | f               | t                     | f
 brin          | f               | t                     | f
 gin           | f               | t                     | f
(4 rows)
Explanation: Index maintenance functions monitor and optimize indexes. pg_stat_user_indexes shows usage patterns - unused indexes waste space. pg_index_column_has_property() checks sorting, NULLs handling. Compare idx_scan to seq_scan in pg_stat_user_tables. Low idx_scan suggests index not beneficial. Regular monitoring prevents unused index accumulation, guides REINDEX decisions.

9.28.9 Generic File Access Functions

File access: Functions to read/write server files. pg_read_file() reads text file. pg_read_binary_file() for binary. pg_ls_dir() lists directory. pg_stat_file() gets file metadata. Restricted to superuser and pg_read_server_files role. Essential for log analysis, configuration checks, backup verification.

🎯 Example 1: List Directory Contents

-- List files in data directory (requires superuser)
SELECT 
    name,
    size,
    modification
FROM pg_ls_dir('base') AS name,
     LATERAL pg_stat_file('base/' || name) AS f
ORDER BY modification DESC
LIMIT 5;
Result:
  name  |  size   |      modification
--------+---------+------------------------
 16384  | 1048576 | 2026-01-26 14:00:00+00
 16385  |  524288 | 2026-01-26 13:55:00+00
 1      |  262144 | 2026-01-26 10:00:00+00
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 2: Read Configuration File

-- Read part of postgresql.conf
SELECT pg_read_file('postgresql.conf', 0, 200) AS config_excerpt;
Result:
              config_excerpt
--------------------------------------------
 # PostgreSQL configuration file
 # ----------------------------
 
 listen_addresses = '*'
 port = 5432
 max_connections = 100
(1 row)

🎯 Example 3: File Metadata

-- Get detailed file information
SELECT 
    size AS file_size_bytes,
    pg_size_pretty(size) AS file_size,
    modification AS last_modified,
    isdir AS is_directory,
    (SELECT setting FROM pg_settings WHERE name = 'data_directory') || '/postgresql.conf' AS file_path
FROM pg_stat_file('postgresql.conf');
Result:
 file_size_bytes | file_size |     last_modified      | is_directory |          file_path
-----------------+-----------+------------------------+--------------+--------------------------------
            8192 | 8192 bytes| 2026-01-26 10:00:00+00 | f            | /var/lib/postgresql/data/postgresql.conf
(1 row)

🎯 Example 4: List WAL Files

-- List WAL segments
SELECT 
    name AS wal_file,
    size,
    modification AS created_at
FROM pg_ls_waldir()
ORDER BY modification DESC
LIMIT 3;
Result:
          wal_file           |   size    |      created_at
-----------------------------+-----------+------------------------
 000000010000000000000006    | 16777216  | 2026-01-26 14:35:00+00
 000000010000000000000005    | 16777216  | 2026-01-26 14:25:00+00
 000000010000000000000004    | 16777216  | 2026-01-26 14:15:00+00
(3 rows)

🎯 Example 5: Check Log File Size

-- Get current log file information
WITH log_settings AS (
    SELECT 
        current_setting('log_directory') AS log_dir,
        current_setting('log_filename') AS log_pattern
)
SELECT 
    log_dir,
    log_pattern,
    (SELECT pg_size_pretty(sum(size)) 
     FROM pg_ls_logdir() 
     WHERE name LIKE '%.log') AS total_log_size;
FROM log_settings;
Result:
 log_dir |     log_pattern      | total_log_size
---------+----------------------+----------------
 log     | postgresql-%Y-%m-%d.log | 25 MB
(1 row)
Explanation: File access functions operate on server filesystem. pg_read_file() for text, pg_read_binary_file() for binary. pg_ls_dir() lists directory, pg_stat_file() gets metadata. Restricted to data_directory and log_directory trees for security. pg_ls_waldir() and pg_ls_logdir() convenience functions. Essential for monitoring logs, verifying backups, configuration auditing. Requires appropriate permissions.

9.28.10 Advisory Lock Functions

Advisory locks: Application-level locks not tied to database objects. pg_advisory_lock(key) acquires lock. pg_advisory_unlock(key) releases. pg_try_advisory_lock(key) non-blocking. Session or transaction scope. Essential for distributed coordination, preventing duplicate jobs, resource synchronization.

🎯 Example: Advisory Locks

-- Advisory lock usage
SELECT 
    pg_try_advisory_lock(123456) AS lock_acquired,
    pg_advisory_lock(123457) AS blocking_lock,
    pg_advisory_unlock(123456) AS lock_released;

-- Check locks
SELECT * FROM pg_locks WHERE locktype = 'advisory' LIMIT 3;
Result:
 lock_acquired | blocking_lock | lock_released
---------------+---------------+---------------
 t             |               | t

-- pg_locks result:
 locktype | pid   | mode            | granted
----------+-------+-----------------+---------
 advisory | 12345 | ExclusiveLock   | t
(1 row)
Explanation: Advisory locks provide application-level coordination. pg_advisory_lock() blocks until acquired. pg_try_advisory_lock() returns immediately (false if unavailable). pg_advisory_unlock() releases. Use for preventing duplicate cron jobs, coordinating batch processes, mutual exclusion. Released automatically at session end.

9.29 Trigger Functions

Trigger functions: Special variables in trigger functions. NEW (new row data), OLD (old row data), TG_OP (operation: INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE), TG_TABLE_NAME (table name), TG_WHEN (BEFORE/AFTER), TG_LEVEL (ROW/STATEMENT). Language: PL/pgSQL. Essential for auditing, validation, derived data, complex business rules.

🎯 Example: Trigger Function

-- Create trigger function
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION audit_trigger()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
    INSERT INTO audit_log(table_name, operation, old_data, new_data, changed_at)
    VALUES (TG_TABLE_NAME, TG_OP, row_to_json(OLD), row_to_json(NEW), now());
    RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Create trigger
CREATE TRIGGER products_audit
AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE ON products
FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION audit_trigger();
Result:
CREATE FUNCTION
CREATE TRIGGER
Explanation: Trigger functions use special variables. NEW contains new row (INSERT/UPDATE). OLD contains old row (UPDATE/DELETE). TG_OP identifies operation. TG_TABLE_NAME for generic triggers. Return NEW/OLD to proceed, NULL to abort. Essential for audit trails, validation, maintaining derived data.

9.30 Event Trigger Functions

Event triggers: Fire on DDL events (CREATE, ALTER, DROP). Functions: pg_event_trigger_ddl_commands() (commands executed), pg_event_trigger_dropped_objects() (dropped objects), pg_event_trigger_table_rewrite_oid() (rewritten table). Events: ddl_command_start, ddl_command_end, table_rewrite, sql_drop. Essential for DDL auditing, schema change tracking, preventing unauthorized changes.

9.30.1 Capturing Changes at Command End

🎯 Example: DDL Auditing

-- Event trigger for DDL auditing
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION ddl_audit()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE
    obj record;
BEGIN
    FOR obj IN SELECT * FROM pg_event_trigger_ddl_commands()
    LOOP
        RAISE NOTICE 'DDL: % on %', obj.command_tag, obj.object_identity;
    END LOOP;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER audit_ddl ON ddl_command_end
EXECUTE FUNCTION ddl_audit();
Result:
CREATE FUNCTION
CREATE EVENT TRIGGER
Explanation: Event triggers monitor DDL operations. pg_event_trigger_ddl_commands() returns executed commands. Fire on events: ddl_command_start, ddl_command_end, sql_drop. Use for audit trails, preventing schema changes, synchronizing development environments. Requires superuser to create.

9.30.2 Processing Objects Dropped by a DDL Command

Dropped objects: pg_event_trigger_dropped_objects() returns objects dropped by DROP commands. Available in sql_drop event triggers. Returns object identity, type, flags. Useful for dependency tracking, cascading cleanup, auditing deletions, preventing accidental drops.

🎯 Example 1: Track Dropped Objects

-- Event trigger to log dropped objects
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION log_dropped_objects()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE
    obj record;
BEGIN
    FOR obj IN SELECT * FROM pg_event_trigger_dropped_objects()
    LOOP
        RAISE NOTICE 'Dropped % %: %',
            obj.object_type,
            obj.object_identity,
            CASE WHEN obj.normal THEN 'normal' ELSE 'cascade' END;
    END LOOP;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER track_drops ON sql_drop
EXECUTE FUNCTION log_dropped_objects();
Result:
CREATE FUNCTION
CREATE EVENT TRIGGER

🎯 Example 2: Test Drop Detection

-- Create and drop test table to trigger event
CREATE TABLE test_drop_table (id int, name text);
CREATE INDEX test_drop_idx ON test_drop_table(id);

-- This will trigger the event
DROP TABLE test_drop_table CASCADE;
Result:
CREATE TABLE
CREATE INDEX
NOTICE:  Dropped table public.test_drop_table: normal
NOTICE:  Dropped index public.test_drop_idx: cascade
DROP TABLE

🎯 Example 3: Prevent Accidental Drops

-- Event trigger to prevent dropping protected tables
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION prevent_critical_drops()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE
    obj record;
BEGIN
    FOR obj IN SELECT * FROM pg_event_trigger_dropped_objects()
    LOOP
        IF obj.object_identity ~ '^public\.(customers|orders|products)$' THEN
            RAISE EXCEPTION 'Cannot drop critical table: %', obj.object_identity;
        END IF;
    END LOOP;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER protect_tables ON sql_drop
EXECUTE FUNCTION prevent_critical_drops();
Result:
CREATE FUNCTION
CREATE EVENT TRIGGER

🎯 Example 4: Audit Trail for Drops

-- Create audit table for drops
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS drop_audit (
    drop_time timestamptz DEFAULT now(),
    object_type text,
    object_identity text,
    dropped_by text,
    is_cascade boolean
);

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION audit_drops()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE
    obj record;
BEGIN
    FOR obj IN SELECT * FROM pg_event_trigger_dropped_objects()
    LOOP
        INSERT INTO drop_audit (object_type, object_identity, dropped_by, is_cascade)
        VALUES (obj.object_type, obj.object_identity, current_user, NOT obj.normal);
    END LOOP;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER audit_sql_drop ON sql_drop
EXECUTE FUNCTION audit_drops();
Result:
CREATE TABLE
CREATE FUNCTION
CREATE EVENT TRIGGER

🎯 Example 5: Dropped Objects Details

-- Query dropped objects structure
SELECT 
    classid::regclass AS catalog_table,
    objid AS object_id,
    objsubid AS subobject_id,
    original AS was_normal_drop,
    normal AS is_normal,
    is_temporary,
    object_type,
    schema_name,
    object_name,
    object_identity
FROM pg_event_trigger_dropped_objects()
LIMIT 5;
Result:
 catalog_table | object_id | subobject_id | was_normal_drop | is_normal | is_temporary | object_type | schema_name | object_name |    object_identity
---------------+-----------+--------------+-----------------+-----------+--------------+-------------+-------------+-------------+----------------------
 pg_class      |     16385 |            0 | t               | t         | f            | table       | public      | test_table  | public.test_table
(1 row)
Explanation: pg_event_trigger_dropped_objects() provides details on dropped database objects. Only available in sql_drop event triggers. normal field indicates direct drop vs cascade. is_temporary identifies temp objects. Use for comprehensive audit trails, preventing accidental deletions, tracking dependencies, implementing custom cascade logic, regulatory compliance.

9.30.3 Handling a Table Rewrite Event

Table rewrite: pg_event_trigger_table_rewrite_oid() returns OID of table being rewritten. Table rewrites occur during: ALTER TABLE ... TYPE, ALTER TABLE ... SET TABLESPACE, CLUSTER, VACUUM FULL. Event: table_rewrite. Useful for monitoring long operations, preventing disruptive rewrites, maintenance scheduling.

🎯 Example 1: Detect Table Rewrites

-- Event trigger to log table rewrites
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION log_table_rewrites()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE
    rewrite_oid oid;
    table_name text;
BEGIN
    SELECT pg_event_trigger_table_rewrite_oid() INTO rewrite_oid;
    SELECT relname INTO table_name 
    FROM pg_class 
    WHERE oid = rewrite_oid;
    
    RAISE NOTICE 'Table rewrite triggered for: % (OID: %)', 
        table_name, rewrite_oid;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER monitor_rewrites ON table_rewrite
EXECUTE FUNCTION log_table_rewrites();
Result:
CREATE FUNCTION
CREATE EVENT TRIGGER

🎯 Example 2: Prevent Rewrites on Large Tables

-- Prevent rewrites on tables larger than 1GB
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION prevent_large_rewrites()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE
    rewrite_oid oid;
    table_size bigint;
    table_name text;
BEGIN
    SELECT pg_event_trigger_table_rewrite_oid() INTO rewrite_oid;
    SELECT 
        pg_total_relation_size(rewrite_oid),
        relname
    INTO table_size, table_name
    FROM pg_class
    WHERE oid = rewrite_oid;
    
    IF table_size > 1073741824 THEN  -- 1GB
        RAISE EXCEPTION 'Cannot rewrite large table % (size: %)',
            table_name, pg_size_pretty(table_size);
    END IF;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER block_large_rewrites ON table_rewrite
EXECUTE FUNCTION prevent_large_rewrites();
Result:
CREATE FUNCTION
CREATE EVENT TRIGGER

🎯 Example 3: Test Rewrite Trigger

-- Create test table and trigger rewrite
CREATE TABLE test_rewrite (id int, data text);
INSERT INTO test_rewrite SELECT generate_series(1, 100), 'test data';

-- This will trigger table rewrite event
ALTER TABLE test_rewrite ALTER COLUMN data TYPE varchar(50);
Result:
CREATE TABLE
INSERT 0 100
NOTICE:  Table rewrite triggered for: test_rewrite (OID: 16390)
ALTER TABLE

🎯 Example 4: Rewrite Audit with Timing

-- Create audit table for rewrites
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS rewrite_audit (
    audit_time timestamptz DEFAULT now(),
    table_oid oid,
    table_name text,
    table_size bigint,
    initiated_by text,
    operation text
);

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION audit_table_rewrites()
RETURNS event_trigger AS $$
DECLARE
    rewrite_oid oid;
    tbl_name text;
    tbl_size bigint;
BEGIN
    SELECT pg_event_trigger_table_rewrite_oid() INTO rewrite_oid;
    
    SELECT relname, pg_total_relation_size(oid)
    INTO tbl_name, tbl_size
    FROM pg_class
    WHERE oid = rewrite_oid;
    
    INSERT INTO rewrite_audit (table_oid, table_name, table_size, initiated_by, operation)
    VALUES (rewrite_oid, tbl_name, tbl_size, current_user, tg_tag);
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE EVENT TRIGGER audit_rewrites ON table_rewrite
EXECUTE FUNCTION audit_table_rewrites();
Result:
CREATE TABLE
CREATE FUNCTION
CREATE EVENT TRIGGER

🎯 Example 5: Identify Rewrite-Causing Operations

-- Operations that cause table rewrites:
-- 1. ALTER TABLE ... TYPE (changing column type)
-- 2. ALTER TABLE ... SET TABLESPACE
-- 3. CLUSTER
-- 4. VACUUM FULL

-- Check which operations trigger rewrites
SELECT 
    'ALTER COLUMN TYPE' AS operation,
    'Changes data type' AS reason,
    'table_rewrite' AS event_fired
UNION ALL
SELECT 'ALTER SET TABLESPACE', 'Moves table files', 'table_rewrite'
UNION ALL
SELECT 'CLUSTER', 'Reorganizes by index', 'table_rewrite'
UNION ALL  
SELECT 'VACUUM FULL', 'Compacts table', 'table_rewrite';
Result:
      operation        |        reason        | event_fired
-----------------------+----------------------+--------------
 ALTER COLUMN TYPE     | Changes data type    | table_rewrite
 ALTER SET TABLESPACE  | Moves table files    | table_rewrite
 CLUSTER               | Reorganizes by index | table_rewrite
 VACUUM FULL           | Compacts table       | table_rewrite
(4 rows)
Explanation: Table rewrite events fire when tables are completely rebuilt. pg_event_trigger_table_rewrite_oid() returns affected table OID. Rewrites are expensive - copy all data to new file. Operations causing rewrites: ALTER COLUMN TYPE (incompatible types), SET TABLESPACE, CLUSTER, VACUUM FULL. Use triggers to prevent unexpected rewrites during business hours, audit maintenance operations, enforce rewrite policies on large tables.

9.31 Statistics Information Functions

Statistics functions: Query statistics objects. pg_statistic contains column statistics. Extended statistics: pg_statistic_ext. MCV (Most Common Values) lists: pg_mcv_list_items(). Essential for understanding planner decisions, analyzing data distribution, query optimization.

9.31.1 Inspecting MCV Lists

🎯 Example: MCV Inspection

-- View table statistics
SELECT 
    schemaname,
    tablename,
    attname,
    null_frac,
    n_distinct,
    most_common_vals,
    most_common_freqs
FROM pg_stats
WHERE tablename = 'products'
  AND attname = 'category'
LIMIT 1;
Result:
 schemaname | tablename | attname  | null_frac | n_distinct | most_common_vals            | most_common_freqs
------------+-----------+----------+-----------+------------+-----------------------------+-------------------
 public     | products  | category |      0.00 |        3.0 | {Electronics,Furniture,...} | {0.6,0.25,0.15}
(1 row)
Explanation: pg_stats view shows column statistics. null_frac = NULL percentage. n_distinct = unique values estimate. most_common_vals = frequent values. most_common_freqs = their frequencies. Statistics guide query planner. Run ANALYZE to update. Essential for understanding data distribution, troubleshooting query plans.
